topics.title: Internal Sensors: Position - Optical Encoders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Internal Sensors: Position - Optical Encoders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Optical Encoder** is a non-contact, electro-mechanical position sensing device that converts the angular or linear position of a shaft into a corresponding digital or analog electrical signal by exploiting the principle of **optical interruption** between a light source and a photodetector through a patterned code wheel (disc) or strip. Optical encoders are classified as **internal sensors** because they are physically coupled (mounted) to the rotating/linear element being measured, making them part of the actuator assembly itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Optical encoders fall under **Internal Position Sensors** used in servo systems, robotics, CNC machines, and intelligent motion control subsystems. They provide **digital position feedback** essential for closed-loop control.

### 1.2 Two Principal Variants

| Variant | Output | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Incremental Optical Encoder** | Pulses relative to motion (no absolute position) | Generates A, B (quadrature) and Z (index) channels |
| **Absolute Optical Encoder** | Unique digital word per shaft angle | Uses **Gray Code** pattern on concentric tracks |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Spinning Stroboscope:**
> Imagine a cardboard disc with alternating transparent and opaque radial slots mounted on a bicycle wheel. Shine a flashlight from one side and place a photodiode on the other. Every time a slot aligns, light passes and the photodiode registers a pulse. Counting pulses tells you how far the wheel turned. This is exactly how an **incremental encoder** works.
>
> For the **absolute encoder**, imagine instead a disc where each angular sector has a **unique binary fingerprint** (e.g., 0000, 0001, 0011, 0010, 0110, ...). Even if power is cut and restored, the sensor immediately knows the exact angular position by reading this fingerprint — no counting from a reference is needed.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards

- **Standard operating wavelength of LED source:** λ ≈ **850 nm to 940 nm** (near-infrared)
- **Photodetector type:** Photodiode / Phototransistor (typically Si-based)
- **Typical supply voltage:** **5 V DC** or **24 V DC**
- **Resolution range:** **10 PPR (pulses per revolution)** to **10,000+ PPR** for incremental; up to **16-bit (65,536 positions/rev)** for absolute

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — Component-Level Breakdown

An optical encoder consists of four primary subsystems:

1. **Light Source (Emitter):**
   - Typically an **LED** (Light Emitting Diode).
   - Provides a stable, collimated beam of light.
   - Reliability is critical — failure of the LED is the most common encoder fault.

2. **Code Wheel (Code Disc / Slit Disc):**
   - A glass, plastic, or metal disc with precisely etched radial lines (incremental) or concentric circular tracks (absolute).
   - Etched using photolithographic techniques → extremely high precision.

3. **Photodetector (Receiver):**
   - A **photodiode** or **phototransistor** array aligned with the code wheel.
   - Generates a current proportional to incident light intensity.

4. **Signal Conditioning Circuit:**
   - Converts photodetector analog current into a clean **square-wave digital signal** (TTL/CMOS compatible).
   - Often includes a Schmitt trigger for noise immunity.

### 2.2 Incremental Encoder Working — Quadrature Decoding

The code wheel has two tracks offset by **90° electrical**, producing two output channels:

- **Channel A:** Sine-like waveform
- **Channel B:** Same waveform, shifted by **+90°** (or –90° depending on direction)

> [!NOTE]
> The **phase relationship** between A and B tells the controller:
> - A leads B → rotation in **clockwise (CW)** direction
> - B leads A → rotation in **counter-clockwise (CCW)** direction
>
> This is called **quadrature decoding** and effectively **quadruples** the resolution (×4 counting: rising-edge of A, falling-edge of A, rising-edge of B, falling-edge of B).

A third **Index / Z-channel** (Reference) provides **one pulse per revolution**, used for homing and origin detection.

### 2.3 Absolute Encoder Working — Gray Code

- The code wheel has **N concentric tracks**, where N = number of bits.
- Each angular sector has a unique **N-bit pattern**.
- **Gray Code** is universally preferred over natural binary because:
  - In Gray code, **only one bit changes** between successive positions.
  - This eliminates erroneous intermediate readings caused by misalignment of multiple photodetectors simultaneously.

**Conversion from Natural Binary to Gray Code:**
$$
G_n = B_{n+1} \oplus B_n
$$

Where:
- $B_n$ = nth bit of binary number
- $G_n$ = nth bit of Gray code
- $\oplus$ = XOR operation

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution (Incremental) | $R_{inc} = 4 \cdot N_{slots}$ | counts/rev | N_slots = slots on disc; ×4 from quadrature decoding |
| Resolution (Absolute) | $R_{abs} = 2^N$ | positions/rev | N = number of tracks / bits |
| Angular resolution | $\theta_{min} = \dfrac{360°}{R}$ | degrees | R = resolution |
| Linear resolution | $L_{min} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D}{R}$ | mm | D = wheel diameter |
| Shaft rotational speed | $n = \dfrac{f_{out}}{R_{inc}} \cdot 60$ | RPM | f_out = output pulse frequency (Hz) |
| Maximum detectable speed | $f_{max} = \dfrac{n_{max} \cdot R_{inc}}{60}$ | Hz | Limited by photodetector bandwidth |
| Velocity from pulses | $v = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R \cdot \Delta t}$ | rev/sec | ΔP = pulse count change |
| Direction logic | A leads B → CW; B leads A → CCW | — | Quadrature phase rule |
| Gray → Binary | $B_n = G_n \oplus B_{n+1}$ | — | Reverse conversion |
| Error tolerance | Gray code single-bit-change | — | Eliminates multi-bit read errors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Optical encoders are the **de facto standard** for digital position feedback in CNC machines, robotic arm joints, BLDC motor commutation, printing presses, elevators, and aerospace actuators. They provide sub-arc-second precision when paired with precision bearings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quadrature Resolution Multiplication

**Given:** An incremental optical encoder disc has $N_{slots}$ = 1000 slots evenly distributed around its circumference. The output channels A and B are in perfect quadrature.

**To Find:** Effective resolution per revolution.

**Step 1 — Physical Pulse Count:**
Each physical slot crossing produces one pulse on channel A and one pulse on channel B.

$$
P_{physical} = N_{slots} = 1000 \text{ pulses/rev}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize Quadrature Advantage:**
Quadrature decoding counts edges on BOTH A and B channels, on BOTH rising AND falling edges:

$$
\text{Edges per slot} = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Resolution Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{inc} &= 4 \cdot N_{slots} \\
R_{inc} &= 4 \times 1000 \\
R_{inc} &= 4000 \text{ counts/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Angular Resolution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{min} &= \frac{360°}{R_{inc}} \\
\theta_{min} &= \frac{360°}{4000} \\
\theta_{min} &= 0.09° = 324^{\prime\prime} \text{ arc-seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 1000-slot disc with ×4 decoding provides **324 arc-seconds** resolution — sufficient for most CNC and robotic applications.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Absolute Encoder Resolution from Number of Tracks

**Given:** An absolute optical encoder uses N = 12 concentric tracks with unique Gray code patterns.

**Step 1 — Apply the Resolution Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{abs} &= 2^N \\
R_{abs} &= 2^{12} \\
R_{abs} &= 4096 \text{ unique positions/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Resolution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{min} &= \frac{360°}{4096} \\
\theta_{min} &= 0.0879° \approx 0.088° \\
\theta_{min} &\approx 316.4^{\prime\prime} \text{ arc-seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification of Gray Code Property:**
For N = 12 bits, the number of possible bit-pattern transitions is $2^{12} = 4096$. In Gray code, each adjacent position differs by exactly **one bit**, ensuring that at most one photodetector changes state at any transition → no spurious intermediate readings.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Natural Binary → Gray Code Conversion

**Given:** Decimal number B = 13 → Binary = **1101**

**Step 1 — Write Binary Bits (MSB first):**
$B_3 = 1, \quad B_2 = 1, \quad B_1 = 0, \quad B_0 = 1$

**Step 2 — Apply Conversion Rule $G_n = B_{n+1} \oplus B_n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_3 &= B_3 = 1 \text{ (MSB is preserved)} \\
G_2 &= B_3 \oplus B_2 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_1 &= B_2 \oplus B_1 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_0 &= B_1 \oplus B_0 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Assemble Gray Code:**

$$
G = 1011_{Gray}
$$

**Verification:** Successive binary numbers 13 (1101) and 14 (1110) differ in 3 bits → potential misread. Their Gray codes 1011 and 1001 differ in only **1 bit** ✓.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Incremental vs. Absolute

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (A, B, Z) | Multi-bit digital word |
| Position at power-up | Lost — must home to reference | Retained immediately |
| Code pattern | Single track of slots | N concentric tracks |
| Code type | Binary edges (slot/no slot) | Gray Code |
| Resolution method | Pulse counting | Direct read |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Typical use | Motors, CNC | Robotic joints, telescopes |
| Vulnerable to noise? | Yes (missing pulses) | No (each position absolute) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Incremental Optical Encoder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LED[LED Light Source 850nm] --> LENS[Collimating Lens]
    LENS --> DISC[Code Wheel with Slots]
    DISC --> MASK[Fixed Slit Mask]
    MASK --> PDA[Photodiode Channel A]
    MASK --> PDB[Photodiode Channel B]
    MASK --> PDZ[Photodiode Channel Z Index]
    PDA --> SIGA[Comparator Schmitt Trigger]
    PDB --> SIGB[Comparator Schmitt Trigger]
    PDZ --> SIGZ[Comparator Schmitt Trigger]
    SIGA --> OUTA[Output A Square Wave]
    SIGB --> OUTB[Output B Square Wave 90 deg Shift]
    SIGZ --> OUTZ[Output Z One Pulse per Rev]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Absolute Optical Encoder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LED2[LED Light Source] --> COLL[Collimator]
    COLL --> DISCA[Code Wheel N Tracks Gray Code Pattern]
    DISCA --> MSKA[Multi Track Slit Mask]
    MSKA --> PDARR[Photodiode Array N Channels]
    PDARR --> AMP[Amplifier Bank N Channels]
    AMP --> LATCH[Parallel Latch Register]
    LATCH --> BUS[N bit Parallel Digital Output Gray Code]
    LATCH --> CONV[Optional Gray to Binary Converter]
    CONV --> CTR[Microcontroller or PLC Input]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Quadrature Direction Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Decoder Reads A and B States] --> AHI{Is A High?}
    AHI -->|Yes| BHI1{Is B High?}
    AHI -->|No| BLO1{Is B High?}
    BHI1 -->|Yes| CW[Clockwise Direction Increment Counter]
    BHI1 -->|No| CCW[Counterclockwise Direction Decrement Counter]
    BLO1 -->|Yes| CCW2[Counterclockwise Direction Decrement Counter]
    BLO1 -->|No| CW2[Clockwise Direction Increment Counter]
    CW --> UPDATE[Update Position Register]
    CCW --> UPDATE
    CCW2 --> UPDATE
    CW2 --> UPDATE
    UPDATE --> END[Repeat at Next Edge]
```

### 4.4 Signal Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Shaft as Rotating Shaft
    participant A as Channel A
    participant B as Channel B
    participant Z as Channel Z Index
    Shaft->>A: Slot crossings generate pulses
    Shaft->>B: Pulses delayed by 90 degrees
    Shaft->>Z: One pulse per full revolution
    Note over A,B: A leads B implies CW rotation
    Note over B,A: B leads A implies CCW rotation
    Note over Z: Used for homing and origin reference
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*What is an optical encoder? Differentiate between incremental and absolute optical encoders in terms of output and position retention at power-up.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **optical encoder** is a non-contact position sensor that uses a light source, a patterned code wheel, and photodetectors to convert rotational/linear motion into electrical signals.

| Feature | Incremental | Absolute |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (A, B, Z) | Unique N-bit digital word |
| Position at power-up | **Lost** — needs homing | **Retained** immediately |
| Code | Single track of slots | N concentric tracks (Gray code) |

**(3 marks — definition 1M, differentiation table 2M)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Why is Gray code used in absolute optical encoders instead of natural binary code?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In a natural binary code disc, multiple photodetectors may change state simultaneously as the shaft rotates. Due to mechanical misalignment and propagation delays, this can produce **erroneous intermediate readings** (e.g., transitioning from 0011 to 0100 might briefly read 0000 or 0111).

In **Gray code**, only **one bit changes** between successive positions, eliminating this hazard. This ensures a clean, monotonic digital output free of spurious codes.

Additionally, Gray code requires fewer transitions, reducing dynamic noise and improving reliability at high speeds.

**(3 marks — explanation of single-bit-change property and error immunity)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an incremental optical encoder. Discuss the concept of quadrature decoding and how it improves resolution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An incremental optical encoder has 2500 slots on its code disc. Calculate:
(i) The effective resolution with ×4 quadrature decoding.
(ii) The minimum angular displacement that can be detected.
(iii) The maximum shaft speed (RPM) if the photodetector bandwidth is 100 kHz. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working — 7 Marks:**

The incremental optical encoder consists of:

1. **LED Light Source** — emits near-infrared (λ ≈ 850 nm) light.
2. **Collimating Lens** — focuses the light into a parallel beam.
3. **Code Disc (Code Wheel)** — a glass/plastic disc with $N$ precisely etched radial slots (transparent/opaque pattern).
4. **Slit Mask** — a stationary disc with matching slots positioned on the receiver side.
5. **Photodetectors (A, B, Z)** — photodiodes/phototransistors that receive the modulated light.
6. **Signal Conditioning** — Schmitt trigger comparators producing clean square waves.

**Working Principle:**
As the shaft rotates, the slots alternately transmit and block light → photodetector generates a pulsed current → comparator outputs a square wave.

**Quadrature Decoding (×4):**
The A and B channels are physically displaced by **90° electrical** on the code disc. By detecting all 4 edge transitions (rising-A, falling-A, rising-B, falling-B) per slot, the effective resolution is quadrupled. The phase relationship (A leads B vs B leads A) also indicates direction of rotation. The Z (index) channel provides one reference pulse per revolution for homing.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**(Marks distribution: construction 2M, working 2M, quadrature 2M, diagram 1M)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $N_{slots} = 2500$
- Quadrature decoding = ×4
- $f_{max} = 100 \text{ kHz} = 10^5 \text{ Hz}$

**(i) Effective Resolution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{inc} &= 4 \cdot N_{slots} \\
R_{inc} &= 4 \times 2500 \\
R_{inc} &= 10{,}000 \text{ counts/revolution}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**(ii) Minimum Angular Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{min} &= \frac{360°}{R_{inc}} \\
\theta_{min} &= \frac{360°}{10{,}000} \\
\theta_{min} &= 0.036° \\
\theta_{min} &= 0.036 \times 3600^{\prime\prime} \\
\theta_{min} &= 129.6^{\prime\prime} \text{ arc-seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 Marks)**

**(iii) Maximum Shaft Speed:**

Rearranging the pulse frequency formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{max} &= \frac{f_{max} \times 60}{R_{inc}} \\
n_{max} &= \frac{10^5 \times 60}{10{,}000} \\
n_{max} &= 600 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 Marks)**

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an absolute optical encoder. Why is Gray code preferred over natural binary? Derive the conversion formula $G_n = B_{n+1} \oplus B_n$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Convert the following natural binary numbers to Gray code and verify the single-bit-change property:
(i) B = 101100
(ii) B = 110010

Also, calculate the resolution and angular resolution of a 16-bit absolute optical encoder. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Absolute Encoder — 7 Marks:**

An absolute optical encoder consists of:

1. **LED + Collimator** — provides uniform illumination.
2. **Code Disc** — contains **N concentric tracks**, each track corresponding to one bit. Each track has alternating transparent/opaque sectors encoding Gray code bits.
3. **Photodiode Array** — N photodetectors, one per track.
4. **Signal Processing Electronics** — amplifiers and parallel output drivers.

**Working Principle:**
Each unique angular position of the disc is illuminated, and the photodetector array simultaneously reads the N-bit pattern for that position. The output is a **parallel N-bit digital word** that directly represents the absolute shaft angle without any counting or homing required.

**Gray Code Justification:**
In natural binary, multiple bits change simultaneously between consecutive positions, leading to **misalignment errors** at sector boundaries. Gray code ensures **only one bit transitions at a time**, eliminating spurious readings and improving noise immunity at high speeds.

**Derivation of $G_n = B_{n+1} \oplus B_n$:**

The MSB of binary is preserved:

$$
G_{n-1} = B_{n-1}
$$

Each subsequent Gray bit is the XOR of the current binary bit and the previous (higher-order) binary bit:

$$
G_n = B_{n+1} \oplus B_n
$$

This is verified by induction — since $G_n$ depends only on $B_{n+1}$ and $B_n$, a single change in $B$ (from increment) propagates to change only one $G$.

**(Marks: working 2M, Gray code justification 2M, derivation 2M, diagram 1M)**

---

**(b) Conversion and 16-bit Encoder — 7 Marks:**

**(i) B = 101100:**

Bits: $B_5=1, B_4=0, B_3=1, B_2=1, B_1=0, B_0=0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_5 &= 1 \\
G_4 &= B_5 \oplus B_4 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_3 &= B_4 \oplus B_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
G_2 &= B_3 \oplus B_2 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_1 &= B_2 \oplus B_1 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_0 &= B_1 \oplus B_0 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gray code = 111010** **(2 Marks)**

**(ii) B = 110010:**

Bits: $B_5=1, B_4=1, B_3=0, B_2=0, B_1=1, B_0=0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_5 &= 1 \\
G_4 &= 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_3 &= 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_2 &= 0 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
G_1 &= 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
G_0 &= 1 \oplus 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gray code = 101011** **(2 Marks)**

**Verification of single-bit-change:**
B = 101100 (44) and B = 110010 (50) — natural binary differs in many bits. Their Gray codes 111010 and 101011 differ in bit-by-bit XOR = 010001 → only certain bits. Note: For consecutive decimal numbers, Gray codes differ in exactly one bit — verified against e.g., 101100 (44) and 101101 (45) → Gray = 111010 and 111011 → differ in only bit 0. ✓

**(1 Mark)**

**16-bit Absolute Encoder Resolution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{abs} &= 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ positions/rev} \\
\theta_{min} &= \frac{360°}{65{,}536} = 0.00549° \approx 19.78^{\prime\prime} \text{ arc-seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ×4 factor:** Students often write $R = N_{slots}$ instead of $R = 4 \cdot N_{slots}$ for incremental encoders — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Gray code direction:** Conversion $B \to G$ is NOT the same as $G \to B$. Use $G_n = B_{n+1} \oplus B_n$ for binary-to-Gray; use $B_n = G_n \oplus B_{n+1}$ for Gray-to-binary — **lose 1 mark if reversed**.
> 3. **Unit errors in RPM/Hz:** Always convert Hz to RPM using the factor 60 — **lose 0.5 mark per mistake**.
> 4. **Ignoring photodetector bandwidth:** Maximum speed depends on $f_{max}$ of the detector, not just the disc — **lose 1 mark**.
> 5. **Drawing mistakes:** Channel A and B must be clearly offset by 90° in diagrams — unlabeled diagrams receive partial credit only.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Optical encoders are **non-contact, internal position sensors** with high reliability and resolution.
- ✅ **Incremental encoder** → pulses (A, B, Z); requires homing; **quadrature decoding** multiplies resolution by **×4**.
- ✅ **Absolute encoder** → unique N-bit digital word; **position retained at power-up**; uses **Gray code** for single-bit-transition safety.
- ✅ **Gray code formula:** $G_n = B_{n+1} \oplus B_n$ (binary → Gray); $B_n = G_n \oplus B_{n+1}$ (Gray → binary).
- ✅ Resolution formulas:
   - Incremental: $R_{inc} = 4 \cdot N_{slots}$ counts/rev
   - Absolute: $R_{abs} = 2^N$ positions/rev
- ✅ Angular resolution: $\theta_{min} = 360° / R$
- ✅ Linear resolution: $L_{min} = \pi D / R$
- ✅ Maximum speed limit: $n_{max} = (f_{max} \times 60) / R_{inc}$
- ✅ Direction logic: **A leads B → CW**; **B leads A → CCW**.
- ✅ Standard LED wavelength: **λ ≈ 850 nm (near-infrared)**.
- ✅ **Z-channel** provides one reference pulse per revolution for homing.
- ✅ Applications: CNC machines, robotics, BLDC motor commutation, printers, elevators, aerospace actuators.
- ✅ Advantages: high precision, digital output, noise immunity, long life, fast response.
- ✅ Disadvantages: sensitive to dust/contamination, LED degradation over time, limited high-temperature operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potentiometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Potentiometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **potentiometer** (often abbreviated as **"pot"**) is a three-terminal electromechanical resistive component that functions as an **adjustable voltage divider**. It consists of a uniform resistive element terminated by two fixed end terminals, and a movable wiper terminal that slides (rotates or translates) along the element, tapping off a continuously variable fraction of the total applied voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET414 — Components of Intelligent Systems):**
> A potentiometer is a passive transducer that converts **mechanical displacement** (linear or angular) into a **proportional electrical output voltage**. In the context of intelligent systems, it is classified as a **position sensor** and is extensively used as a **feedback element** in servo loops, robotic manipulators, and adaptive control architectures.

Mathematically, the device can be modelled as two variable resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ in series, where the wiper position determines their ratio. The output voltage follows the classical voltage-divider relation:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

where $R_1$ is the resistance between the wiper and one end, and $R_2$ is the resistance between the wiper and the other end. The sum $R_1 + R_2$ is constant and equals the total (nominal) resistance of the device, typically ranging from **1 kΩ to 1 MΩ**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tap of Electronics

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a long horizontal water pipe with two open ends. A sliding valve can be moved along its length. The valve position determines how much water pressure reaches a small outlet tap connected to it. If the valve sits at the extreme left, almost no pressure is delivered; if it slides to the extreme right, full pressure is delivered. The valve's position therefore **linearly translates mechanical displacement into a proportional hydraulic signal**.

A potentiometer does **exactly the same thing**, except the "fluid" is electrical current and the "pressure" is voltage. The resistive track is the pipe, the wiper is the sliding valve, and the output terminal is the tap. The wiper's mechanical position is converted into a proportional DC voltage that downstream electronics (microcontrollers, ADC, op-amps) can read to infer the physical position. This simple, robust, and noiseless (in the ideal case) mechanism is why potentiometers remain a cornerstone sensor in **intelligent robotic joints, throttle-by-wire systems, joystick interfaces, and CNC machine tool slides**.

## 1.3 Role in Intelligent Systems — Why It Matters

In a modern intelligent system, the **feedback loop** is what distinguishes a "smart" actuator from a dumb one. A robot arm commanded to move to angle 45° must **know** where its joint actually is — this is achieved by mounting a rotary potentiometer on the joint axis. The pot produces a voltage proportional to the joint angle; the controller compares this to the command signal and drives the motor to close the error. Without such a position sensor, the system cannot be intelligent, adaptive, or closed-loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics and Constants to Remember (KTU 2024 High-Yield):**
> - Total Resistance $R_T$: **1 kΩ, 5 kΩ, 10 kΩ, 50 kΩ, 100 kΩ, 1 MΩ** (E-series preferred values)
> - Power Rating: typically **0.25 W, 0.5 W, 1 W, 2 W**
> - Tolerance: typically **±5 %, ±10 %, ±20 %**
> - Linearity (independent linearity): typically **±0.1 % to ±5 %** of full scale
> - Rotational Life: **10,000 to 1,000,000 cycles** (mechanical)
> - Taper Types: **Linear (B), Logarithmic/Audio (A), Anti-log (C)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Voltage Divider

A potentiometer is fundamentally a **tapped resistor**. When a DC or AC excitation voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the two end terminals, the resistive track behaves as a uniform voltage gradient. The wiper taps off a voltage that depends solely on its fractional position along the track.

- **Step 1:** Apply reference voltage $V_{in}$ across terminals 1 and 3.
- **Step 2:** The track establishes a linear potential gradient from $V_{in}$ to 0 V.
- **Step 3:** The wiper (terminal 2) physically contacts the track at a point determined by mechanical input (shaft rotation or slider translation).
- **Step 4:** Output $V_{out}$ is measured between the wiper and one end terminal.
- **Step 5:** For a **linear taper** potentiometer, $V_{out}$ is a **strictly linear function** of mechanical displacement.

## 2.2 Linear Taper — The Most Common Configuration

For a linear-taper pot, the resistance from one end to the wiper is directly proportional to the displacement. If the wiper moves a fraction $\alpha$ of the total travel, then:

$$R_2 = \alpha \cdot R_T \quad \text{and} \quad R_1 = (1 - \alpha) \cdot R_T$$

Substituting into the voltage-divider relation:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\alpha \cdot R_T}{R_T} = \alpha \cdot V_{in}$$

This confirms that for a **linear-taper pot, $V_{out}$ is a linear function of position**, and the device behaves as an ideal **mechanical-to-voltage transducer**.

## 2.3 Logarithmic (Audio) Taper

Used in audio volume controls, the logarithmic taper produces an output that is exponential with displacement, mimicking the human ear's logarithmic response to sound intensity. The resistance versus position follows:

$$R_2(\alpha) = R_T \cdot 10^{2\alpha - 1}$$

This yields $V_{out}$ that rises exponentially with rotation, providing perceptually linear volume change.

## 2.4 Loading Effect — The Hidden Trap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU examiners frequently test this concept.** A potentiometer is a **passive device**, and its output can be **distorted** if loaded by a finite-impedance external circuit. The voltage-divider formula $V_{out} = \alpha V_{in}$ is **valid only when the load draws zero current** (i.e., into a high-impedance buffer or ADC input). When loaded by a finite $R_L$:

$$V_{out}^{\text{loaded}} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_L \,\vert\, R_2}{R_1 + (R_L \,\vert\, R_2)}$$

where $R_L \,\vert\, R_2$ denotes the **parallel combination** of the load resistance and the lower half of the pot. The error due to loading is most severe at the **mid-stroke position** where $R_1 \approx R_2$ and the Thevenin output resistance of the divider is highest ($= R_T/4$).

## 2.5 Sensitivity and Resolution

The **static sensitivity** of a linear-taper pot is defined as the change in output voltage per unit mechanical displacement:

$$\text{Linear Pot: } S = \frac{dV_{out}}{dx} = \frac{V_{in}}{L}$$

where $L$ is the total mechanical travel length. For a rotary pot of total angle $\theta_{max}$:

$$\text{Rotary Pot: } S = \frac{dV_{out}}{d\theta} = \frac{V_{in}}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Resolution** is the smallest mechanical change that produces a detectable electrical change. For a **wirewound pot**, the resolution is finite (limited by the discrete number of turns), whereas a **conductive-plastic or cermet film pot** offers theoretically **infinite resolution** because the resistance element is continuous.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units / Condition** |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|
| $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage-divider output (no load) | Volts ; valid for $R_L \to \infty$ |
| $V_{out} = \alpha \cdot V_{in}$ | Linear-taper output | $\alpha = x/x_{max}$ (dimensionless, 0 to 1) |
| $R_T = R_1 + R_2$ | Total pot resistance | Ohms (constant) |
| $R_{Th} = R_1 \,\vert\, R_2$ | Thevenin output resistance of divider | Ohms; max value $= R_T/4$ at mid-stroke |
| $V_{out}^{loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_L \,\vert\, R_2}{R_1 + (R_L \,\vert\, R_2)}$ | Output with finite load $R_L$ | Volts ; loading error = $f(\alpha, R_L/R_T)$ |
| $S = V_{in}/L$ | Sensitivity (linear pot) | V/m or V/rad |
| $P_{max} = V_{in}^2/R_T$ | Maximum safe dissipation | Watts ; $V_{in}$ must satisfy $P \le P_{rating}$ |
| $R_2(\alpha) = R_T \cdot 10^{2\alpha - 1}$ | Log-taper resistance profile | Ohms ; audio taper |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Intelligent Systems

| **Application Domain** | **Use of Potentiometer** |
|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Robotic manipulators | Joint angle feedback in PID servo loops |
| CNC machine tools | Tool-head and table position sensing |
| Joystick / game controllers | 2-axis or 3-axis manual command input |
| Automotive throttle-by-wire | Pedal-position feedback to ECU |
| Volume / tone controls (audio) | Log-taper pot matches human hearing |
| Calibration trimmers | One-time factory adjustment of analog circuits |
| Servo motors (RC hobby) | Internal angle feedback of hobby servos |
| Force / pressure transducers | Sense element in some load cells (with strain) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage of an Ideal Potentiometer Voltage Divider

**Given:** A linear-taper potentiometer with total resistance $R_T$ connected across a stable DC source $V_{in}$. The wiper is positioned at fraction $\alpha$ along the track, with $\alpha \in [0, 1]$. The output terminals are open-circuit (no load).

**To find:** $V_{out}$ as a function of $\alpha$ and $V_{in}$.

**Step 1 — Model the pot as two series resistors.**
The track is split by the wiper into two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ in series, satisfying $R_1 + R_2 = R_T$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Since the track is uniform, the resistance is proportional to length:} \\
R_1 &= (1 - \alpha)\,R_T \\
R_2 &= \alpha\,R_T
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the series-circuit current.**
With terminals 1 and 3 connected to $V_{in}$, the current through the track (ignoring wiper loading) is:

$$I = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_T}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the voltage across $R_2$.**
Using Ohm's Law, the output voltage (taken across $R_2$) is:

$$V_{out} = I \cdot R_2 = \frac{V_{in}}{R_T} \cdot \alpha R_T$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**
The $R_T$ terms cancel, giving the canonical result:

$$V_{out} = \alpha \cdot V_{in}$$

This is the **fundamental linear-taper potentiometer equation** that students must memorize for KTU exams. It shows that for a linear taper, the output is **strictly proportional to mechanical position**, independent of the total resistance $R_T$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Loaded Output Voltage (Loading Effect)

**Given:** Same linear-taper pot, but now an external load resistance $R_L$ is connected between the wiper (terminal 2) and ground (terminal 3). The source $V_{in}$ remains applied across terminals 1 and 3.

**To find:** $V_{out}^{loaded}$ in terms of $V_{in}$, $\alpha$, $R_T$, and $R_L$.

**Step 1 — Identify the loaded portion.**
With $R_L$ connected from wiper to ground, the lower half of the pot ($R_2$) is now in **parallel** with $R_L$:

$$R_{2}^{eq} = R_2 \,\vert\, R_L = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Redraw the circuit.**
The circuit is now a simple voltage divider with $R_1$ in series and $R_{2}^{eq}$ across the output:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out}^{loaded} &= V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{2}^{eq}}{R_1 + R_{2}^{eq}} \\
&= V_{in} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{R_2 R_L}{R_2 + R_L}}{R_1 + \dfrac{R_2 R_L}{R_2 + R_L}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(R_2 + R_L)$.**
This clears the inner fraction:

$$V_{out}^{loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2 R_L}{R_1(R_2 + R_L) + R_2 R_L}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the denominator.**

$$R_1 R_2 + R_1 R_L + R_2 R_L$$

**Step 5 — Group terms.**

$$V_{out}^{loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2 R_L}{R_1 R_2 + R_L(R_1 + R_2)}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $R_1 = (1-\alpha)R_T$ and $R_2 = \alpha R_T$, with $R_1 + R_2 = R_T$.**

$$V_{out}^{loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\alpha R_T R_L}{(1-\alpha)\alpha R_T^2 + R_L R_T}$$

**Step 7 — Factor $R_T$ in the denominator.**

$$V_{out}^{loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\alpha R_T R_L}{\alpha(1-\alpha) R_T^2 + R_L R_T}$$

**Step 8 — Divide numerator and denominator by $R_T$.**

$$V_{out}^{loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\alpha R_L}{\alpha(1-\alpha) R_T + R_L}$$

**Step 9 — Special case: $R_L \to \infty$ (no load).**
The second term in the denominator vanishes, and we recover:

$$V_{out}^{loaded} \;\xrightarrow{R_L \to \infty}\; V_{in} \cdot \alpha = V_{out}^{\text{no load}}$$

**Step 10 — Loading error definition.**
The fractional loading error is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{out}^{\text{no load}} - V_{out}^{loaded}}{V_{out}^{\text{no load}}} = 1 - \frac{R_L}{\alpha(1-\alpha) R_T + R_L}$$

This error is **maximum at mid-stroke** ($\alpha = 0.5$), where the denominator becomes smallest, confirming the well-known result that the loading effect is worst at the 50% point.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Sensitivity of a Rotary Potentiometer

**Given:** A rotary linear-taper pot with total mechanical rotation $\theta_{max}$ (typically 270° or 300°) and applied voltage $V_{in}$.

**To find:** Sensitivity $S = dV_{out}/d\theta$.

**Step 1 — Express the position fraction.**

$$\alpha = \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the no-load output equation.**

$$V_{out}(\theta) = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the derivative with respect to $\theta$.**

$$S = \frac{dV_{out}}{d\theta} = \frac{V_{in}}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**
The sensitivity is **constant** (independent of $\theta$) for a linear-taper pot. This constancy is the chief advantage of linear taper in measurement applications.

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
If $V_{in} = 5$ V and $\theta_{max} = 270°$, then:

$$S = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{270°} \approx 0.01852 \text{ V/deg}$$

This means the output changes by about **18.5 mV per degree of rotation**, which is easily resolved by a 10-bit ADC (5 V / 1024 ≈ 4.9 mV/LSB).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Potentiometer Types and Characteristics

| **Parameter** | **Wirewound** | **Cermet** | **Conductive Plastic** | **Carbon Film** | **Digital (IC)** |
|---------------|---------------|------------|------------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Construction | Resistance wire wound on former | Metal glaze on ceramic | Carbon-polymer film | Carbon composition | FET-based resistor network |
| Resolution | Discrete (finite) | Effectively infinite | Effectively infinite | Effectively infinite | Stepped (finite, e.g., 256 taps) |
| Linearity | ±0.1 % to ±1 % | ±0.5 % to ±2 % | ±0.5 % to ±2 % | ±5 % to ±20 % | ±1 LSB (typical) |
| Rotational Life | 100 k cycles | 200 k cycles | 1 M+ cycles | 50 k cycles | 1 M wiper cycles |
| Power Rating | Up to 5 W+ | 0.5 W to 2 W | 0.5 W to 1 W | 0.25 W typical | mW level |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | Higher | Lowest | Moderate |
| Noise | Some (wire pitch) | Very low | Very low | Moderate | Quantization noise |
| KTU Use Case | Precision instruments | Industrial controls | Robotic feedback | Consumer / hobby | Microcontroller interfaces |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A rotary linear-taper potentiometer of total resistance 10 kΩ and total rotation 300° is connected across a 5 V DC supply. A load resistor of 10 kΩ is connected between the wiper and the grounded end terminal. The shaft is rotated to 90° from the start. Calculate (a) the no-load output voltage, (b) the loaded output voltage, and (c) the loading error as a percentage.

**Solution:**

(a) **No-load output voltage:**

$$\alpha = \frac{90°}{300°} = 0.3$$

$$V_{out}^{NL} = 5 \text{ V} \times 0.3 = 1.5 \text{ V}$$

(b) **Loaded output voltage:**

First compute $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$R_2 = \alpha R_T = 0.3 \times 10\,000 = 3\,000 \text{ Ω}$$

$$R_1 = (1 - \alpha) R_T = 0.7 \times 10\,000 = 7\,000 \text{ Ω}$$

Now compute the parallel equivalent of $R_2$ and $R_L$:

$$R_{2}^{eq} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{3\,000 \times 10\,000}{3\,000 + 10\,000} = \frac{30 \times 10^6}{13\,000} \approx 2307.69 \text{ Ω}$$

Apply the loaded voltage-divider formula:

$$V_{out}^{L} = 5 \text{ V} \times \frac{2307.69}{7000 + 2307.69} = 5 \times \frac{2307.69}{9307.69}$$

$$V_{out}^{L} \approx 5 \times 0.2479 \approx 1.240 \text{ V}$$

(c) **Loading error:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{out}^{NL} - V_{out}^{L}}{V_{out}^{NL}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.5 - 1.240}{1.5} \times 100\%$$

$$\varepsilon \approx 17.33\%$$

This example highlights the severity of loading when the load resistance is comparable to the pot resistance. **Rule of thumb:** for loading error below 1 %, choose $R_L \ge 100 \cdot R_T$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Potentiometer as a Three-Terminal Adjustable Voltage Divider

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["V IN DC SOURCE"] -->|terminal 1| TRACK["UNIFORM RESISTIVE TRACK WITH LINEAR VOLTAGE GRADIENT"]
    TRACK -->|terminal 3| GND["GROUND 0V"]
    WIP["MOVABLE WIPER TERMINAL 2"] -->|slides along track| TRACK
    WIP --> VOUT["V OUT TO ADC OR BUFFER AMPLIFIER"]
    VOUT --> LOAD["HIGH IMPEDANCE LOAD R L"]
    LOAD --> GND
    VCC -.->|voltage gradient| GRAD["V AT WIPER = ALPHA TIMES V IN"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Potentiometer in a Closed-Loop Intelligent Servo System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REF["REFERENCE COMMAND INPUT DESIRED ANGLE"] --> COMP["COMPARATOR SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    CTRL["PID CONTROLLER MICROCONTROLLER OR DSP"] --> DRV["POWER AMPLIFIER DRIVER STAGE"]
    DRV --> MTR["DC SERVO MOTOR ACTUATOR"]
    MTR --> JNT["ROBOTIC JOINT MECHANICAL OUTPUT"]
    JNT --> POT["ROTARY POTENTIOMETER POSITION SENSOR FEEDBACK"]
    POT --> AMP["BUFFER AMPLIFIER HIGH INPUT Z"]
    AMP --> ADC["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER"]
    ADC --> FB["DIGITAL FEEDBACK SIGNAL"]
    FB --> COMP
    POT -.->|measured angle| FB
```

flowchart TD
    ROOT["POTENTIOMETER TYPES"]
    ROOT --> ROT["ROTARY TYPE"]
    ROOT --> LIN["LINEAR SLIDE TYPE"]

    ROT --> SING["SINGLE TURN 270 OR 300 DEG"]

## 4.4 Physical Construction Schematic — Rotary Wirewound Potentiometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SHAFT["INPUT SHAFT MECHANICAL INPUT"] --> COUPL["WIPER COUPLER"]
    COUPL --> WCONT["METAL WIPER CONTACT"]
    WCONT -->|slides on| WIRE["RESISTANCE WIRE WOUND ON FORMER"]
    WIRE --> T1["TERMINAL 1 V IN POSITIVE END"]
    WIRE --> T3["TERMINAL 3 GROUND END"]
    WCONT --> T2["TERMINAL 2 V OUT WIPER"]
    HOUS["HOUSING AND MOUNTING BUSH"] -.-> SHAFT
    BRUSH["BRUSH ASSEMBLY SPRING LOADED"] -.-> WCONT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams above:** The central concept is that the **wiper mechanically samples** the voltage gradient on the resistive track. The output voltage is therefore a **direct, instantaneous, and noiseless (in the ideal case) analog of the wiper position**. In all four diagrams, the wiper (terminal 2) is the key active element — it is the only moving part and the only source of mechanical wear.
