# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define a potentiometer. With the help of a neat sketch, explain its working principle as a voltage divider.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A potentiometer is a three-terminal variable resistor consisting of a uniform resistive element with two fixed end terminals and a movable wiper terminal. (1 mark)

When a voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the two end terminals, a linear voltage gradient is established along the resistive track. The wiper taps off a voltage $V_{out}$ that is directly proportional to its mechanical position. (1 mark)

Mathematically, if the wiper is at fraction $\alpha$ of the total track, then $V_{out} = \alpha \cdot V_{in}$. This makes the potentiometer a mechanical-to-electrical transducer that converts linear or angular displacement into a proportional DC voltage. (1 mark)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**List any three specifications of a potentiometer. State two applications of potentiometers in intelligent systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three important specifications: (1½ marks)
1. **Total resistance** (e.g., 10 kΩ) — determines current draw and sensitivity.
2. **Tolerance** (e.g., ±10 %) — indicates deviation of actual $R_T$ from nominal.
3. **Power rating** (e.g., 0.5 W) — maximum allowable continuous dissipation.
*(Other valid answers: taper type, rotational life, linearity, resolution, temperature coefficient.)*

Two applications in intelligent systems: (1½ marks)
1. **Joint position feedback** in robotic arms and CNC machine tools (closed-loop servo control).
2. **Manual command input** via joystick potentiometers for teleoperated robots and gaming controllers.
*(Other valid: throttle position sensing in drive-by-wire, volume/tone control, calibration trimmers.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer **one of two alternatives** within the same module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, derive the expression for the output voltage of a linear-taper potentiometer used as a voltage divider, assuming the output is open-circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 kΩ linear-taper rotary potentiometer with a total rotation of 270° is excited by a 10 V DC source. A load resistance of 100 kΩ is connected between the wiper and the grounded end terminal. The shaft is rotated to 135°. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The fraction of total rotation $\alpha$
- (ii) The no-load output voltage
- (iii) The loaded output voltage
- (iv) The percentage loading error

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of open-circuit output voltage (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1 (Derivation 1)** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The student must:
1. Draw the circuit with $V_{in}$ across the two end terminals and $V_{out}$ taken from the wiper. (1 mark)
2. Express $R_1 = (1-\alpha)R_T$ and $R_2 = \alpha R_T$. (2 marks)
3. Compute the current $I = V_{in}/R_T$. (1 mark)
4. Apply Ohm's law: $V_{out} = I \cdot R_2 = \alpha V_{in}$. (2 marks)
5. State the final result and physical interpretation. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

- **(i) Fraction of total rotation** (1 mark):

$$\alpha = \frac{135°}{270°} = 0.5$$

- **(ii) No-load output voltage** (1 mark):

$$V_{out}^{NL} = \alpha \cdot V_{in} = 0.5 \times 10 \text{ V} = 5 \text{ V}$$

- **(iii) Loaded output voltage** (3 marks):

First compute $R_2$ and $R_1$:

$$R_2 = 0.5 \times 50\,000 = 25\,000 \text{ Ω}$$

$$R_1 = 0.5 \times 50\,000 = 25\,000 \text{ Ω}$$

Parallel combination of $R_2$ and $R_L$:

$$R_{2}^{eq} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{25\,000 \times 100\,000}{25\,000 + 100\,000} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^9}{125\,000} = 20\,000 \text{ Ω}$$

Apply the loaded voltage-divider formula:

$$V_{out}^{L} = 10 \text{ V} \times \frac{20\,000}{25\,000 + 20\,000} = 10 \times \frac{20\,000}{45\,000} = 10 \times 0.4444$$

$$V_{out}^{L} \approx 4.444 \text{ V}$$

- **(iv) Percentage loading error** (2 marks):

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{out}^{NL} - V_{out}^{L}}{V_{out}^{NL}} \times 100\% = \frac{5 - 4.444}{5} \times 100\%$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{0.556}{5} \times 100\% \approx 11.11\%$$

**Conclusion:** Even though $R_L = 2 R_T$ (which seems large), the loading error is still over 11% at mid-stroke, demonstrating the importance of buffering the pot output with a high-impedance op-amp follower or feeding it directly to a high-impedance ADC.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper — Alternative]

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the construction and working of **(i)** a rotary potentiometer and **(ii)** a linear potentiometer. Compare their typical applications in intelligent robotic systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear-taper slide potentiometer has a total track length of 100 mm and a total resistance of 5 kΩ. It is connected across a 12 V DC supply. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The sensitivity in V/mm
- (ii) The output voltage when the slider is at 35 mm from one end
- (iii) The Thevenin equivalent output resistance when the slider is at 75 mm
- (iv) The maximum allowable $V_{in}$ if the power rating is 0.5 W

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and working (7 marks):**

- **(i) Rotary potentiometer (3 marks):**
A rotary pot has a circular resistive track (wirewound, cermet, or conductive plastic) mounted on an insulating substrate. A shaft is rotated by the user (or by a robotic joint), and a spring-loaded wiper attached to the shaft slides along the track. The wiper is connected to the center terminal, while the two ends of the track are the outer terminals. Rotation from 0° to $\theta_{max}$ (typically 270° or 300°) produces a proportional change in tapped voltage. **Application:** Mounted on a robot's revolute joint axis to sense the joint angle.

- **(ii) Linear potentiometer (2 marks):**
A linear pot has a straight resistive track. A sliding carriage is moved along the track by an external mechanical force. The slider position is the analog of the displacement. **Application:** Mounted along a prismatic (sliding) joint of a Cartesian robot or a CNC machine tool slide to sense linear position.

- **Comparison table (2 marks):**
| **Feature** | **Rotary Pot** | **Linear Pot** |
|-------------|----------------|----------------|
| Motion type | Angular (revolute) | Translational (prismatic) |
| Typical range | 270° to 300° (single-turn) | 25 mm to 500 mm |
| Application | Robot revolute joints, joysticks | Robot prismatic joints, CNC slides |
| Resolution | Limited by wire pitch (wirewound) or infinite (film) | Same as rotary |

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

- **(i) Sensitivity** (1 mark):

$$S = \frac{V_{in}}{L} = \frac{12 \text{ V}}{100 \text{ mm}} = 0.12 \text{ V/mm}$$

- **(ii) Output voltage at 35 mm** (2 marks):

$$\alpha = \frac{35}{100} = 0.35$$

$$V_{out} = \alpha \cdot V_{in} = 0.35 \times 12 = 4.20 \text{ V}$$

- **(iii) Thevenin output resistance at 75 mm** (2 marks):

$$\alpha = 0.75, \quad R_2 = 0.75 \times 5000 = 3750 \text{ Ω}, \quad R_1 = 0.25 \times 5000 = 1250 \text{ Ω}$$

$$R_{Th} = R_1 \,\vert\, R_2 = \frac{1250 \times 3750}{1250 + 3750} = \frac{4\,687\,500}{5000} = 937.5 \text{ Ω}$$

- **(iv) Maximum allowable $V_{in}$ for 0.5 W rating** (2 marks):

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{in,max}^2}{R_T}$$

$$V_{in,max} = \sqrt{P_{max} \cdot R_T} = \sqrt{0.5 \times 5000} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \text{ V}$$

Since 12 V is well below 50 V, the pot operates safely within its power rating.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on Potentiometer questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the circuit diagram** in derivations — Examiners award 1–2 marks purely for a labelled diagram. Always show $V_{in}$ across the end terminals, the wiper, and $V_{out}$ measured to ground.
> 2. **Confusing potentiometer with rheostat** — A potentiometer uses all **three** terminals as a voltage divider. A rheostat uses only **two** terminals as a variable resistor. Examiners frequently set a 3-mark question specifically on this distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring loading effect in numerical problems** — Students often blindly apply $V_{out} = \alpha V_{in}$ even when a load $R_L$ is given in the problem. Always check whether the problem specifies $R_L$ or says "open-circuit / no-load".
> 4. **Mixing up log-taper and linear-taper formulas** — Log-taper follows $R_2 = R_T \cdot 10^{2\alpha - 1}$, **not** a linear relation. Use the correct formula or lose full marks.
> 5. **Wrong units on sensitivity** — Sensitivity of a linear pot must be reported in **V/mm** or **V/rad**, not just "V". Units carry marks.
> 6. **Not stating the no-load assumption** — Always state "assuming no-load / open-circuit output" before applying $V_{out} = \alpha V_{in}$. Examiners look for this phrase.
> 7. **Forgetting the Thevenin resistance** — When a question asks for "output impedance of the pot", it means $R_{Th} = R_1 \,\vert\, R_2$, which is a function of $\alpha$ and is maximum ($= R_T/4$) at mid-stroke.
> 8. **Not relating to intelligent systems** — In PEMET414 specifically, examiners expect every answer to mention the **role of the pot in a feedback loop** (sensor → controller → actuator). Generic answers lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU revision — the 12 high-yield facts about Potentiometers:**
>
> 1. A potentiometer is a **three-terminal passive transducer** that converts **mechanical displacement** into a **proportional DC voltage**.
> 2. The fundamental equation for a **linear-taper, no-load** pot is $V_{out} = \alpha \cdot V_{in}$ where $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is the fractional wiper position.
> 3. The general voltage-divider relation is $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_2/(R_1 + R_2)$, valid for **any taper**.
> 4. The **loading effect** distorts the output when an external load $R_L$ draws current. Use $V_{out}^{loaded} = V_{in} \cdot R_{2}^{eq}/(R_1 + R_{2}^{eq})$ where $R_{2}^{eq} = R_2 \,\vert\, R_L$.
> 5. Loading error is **maximum at mid-stroke** ($\alpha = 0.5$) and is minimized by making $R_L \ge 100 R_T$.
> 6. **Thevenin output resistance** $R_{Th} = R_1 \,\vert\, R_2$ varies with $\alpha$; maximum value is $R_T/4$ at mid-stroke.
> 7. **Sensitivity** of a linear pot is constant: $S = V_{in}/L$ (linear) or $S = V_{in}/\theta_{max}$ (rotary).
> 8. **Wirewound pots** have finite resolution; **film-type pots** (cermet, conductive plastic) have effectively **infinite resolution**.
> 9. **Logarithmic (audio) taper** follows $R_2 = R_T \cdot 10^{2\alpha - 1}$ and is used in volume controls to match human hearing.
> 10. The **maximum safe excitation voltage** is $V_{in,max} = \sqrt{P_{rating} \cdot R_T}$; exceeding this destroys the pot.
> 11. In an **intelligent system**, the pot serves as a **position sensor in a closed feedback loop** (sensor → comparator → controller → actuator → sensor).
> 12. The **wiper** is the only moving part; it is the sole source of mechanical wear and **contact noise** in real devices.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** LVDT ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive, electromechanical, inductive transducer that converts **linear displacement** (mechanical motion along a straight line) into a corresponding **electrical voltage signal**. It operates on the principle of **mutual inductance** between a centrally located primary winding and two symmetrically placed secondary windings, coupled by a movable soft-iron core whose axial position determines the magnetic coupling and hence the differential output voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** > "An LVDT is an electromechanical transducer used to convert rectilinear motion (displacement) of a movable ferromagnetic core into a variable electrical output voltage, characterized by linearity, infinite resolution, and ruggedness." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic See-Saw" Imagine a **two-pan balance scale**, but instead of measuring weight, it measures **position**. The scale has two pans (representing the two secondary coils) connected by a lever (the magnetic core). When the core is exactly in the **middle (null position)**, both pans are perfectly balanced and produce equal opposing readings — the net output is **zero volts**. - If the core slides **left** → the left pan goes down → more magnetic flux couples with the **left secondary** → produces a **positive** output. - If the core slides **right** → the right pan goes down → more magnetic flux couples with the **right secondary** → produces a **negative** output (180° phase shift). This is exactly how an LVDT behaves! The electrical "balance" is the differential voltage between two secondaries. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants/Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):** > - **Operating Frequency Range:** 1 kHz to 25 kHz (typical excitation: **2 kHz to 10 kHz**) > - **Linear Range:** ±0.1 mm to ±250 mm (depending on design) > - **Theoretical Resolution:** **Infinite** (limited only by the readability of the voltmeter) > - **Hysteresis Error:** Very low (typically **< 0.1%** of full scale) > - **Operating Temperature:** −55 °C to +150 °C (military grade up to **+200 °C**) ### 1.3 Salient Features of LVDT - **Infinite Resolution:** Output is a continuous function of core position; no friction or contacts. - **Frictionless Operation:** Core moves freely inside the coil assembly, no mechanical contact. - **High Sensitivity:** Typical sensitivity ranges from **0.5 V/mm to 5 V/mm** depending on excitation. - **Linearity:** Excellent linearity over the design range (±0.1% to ±0.5%). - **Ruggedness:** Resistant to shock, vibration, and harsh environments. - **Bidirectional Output:** Sign of output indicates direction of displacement. ### 1.4 Limitations - Requires **AC excitation** (carrier signal); cannot work on DC. - **Dynamic response** is limited by carrier frequency. - **Temperature-sensitive** due to coil resistance variations. - Sensitive to **stray magnetic fields** (requires shielding). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Constructional Details The LVDT comprises the following parts: 1. **Primary Winding (P):** Wound centrally on a non-magnetic (often fiberglass or ceramic) bobbin. Excited by an AC voltage source $V_p$ of frequency $f$ (typically 1–25 kHz). 2. **Two Identical Secondary Windings (S₁ and S₂):** Wound on either side of the primary, with **equal number of turns** $N_s$. They are connected in **series opposition** (i.e., the EMFs subtract). 3. **Movable Core:** A high-permeability soft-iron (or nickel-iron alloy) cylindrical core attached to the displacement-sensing rod. 4. **Shielding:** A permeable outer shield minimizes interference from external magnetic fields. 5. **Coil Assembly:** All windings are housed inside a cylindrical metallic casing. ### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1: Excitation** An AC voltage $V_p = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied to the primary winding. This produces an alternating magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the core region. **Step 2: Mutual Induction** The alternating flux links both secondary windings via the movable core, inducing EMFs $e_{s1}$ and $e_{s2}$ in them (Faraday's Law of Induction). **Step 3: Null Position** When the core is **exactly at the centre** of the coil assembly (null/zero position), the magnetic coupling with both secondaries is **identical**. Hence: $$e_{s1} = e_{s2}$$ Since the secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, the **net output voltage is zero**: $$e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 0$$ **Step 4: Displacement to the Left** When the core moves **toward S₁** by a displacement $x$: - Coupling with S₁ **increases** → $e_{s1}$ increases - Coupling with S₂ **decreases** → $e_{s2}$ decreases - Net output is **positive** (in phase with primary excitation). **Step 5: Displacement to the Right** When the core moves **toward S₂** by a displacement $x$: - Coupling with S₂ **increases** → $e_{s2}$ increases - Coupling with S₁ **decreases** → $e_{s1}$ decreases - Net output is **180° out of phase** with primary excitation. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **EMF in Primary** | $E_p = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \Phi_{max}$ | Standard transformer EMF equation | Volts (V) | | **EMF in each Secondary** | $E_s = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_{max}$ | Induced EMF per secondary | Volts (V) | | **Output Voltage at Null** | $e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 0$ | When core is at center | V | | **Output Voltage (displaced)** | $e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = K \cdot x$ | Linear in displacement | V | | **Sensitivity (S)** | $S = \dfrac{e_{out}}{x} = \dfrac{\Delta e_{out}}{\Delta x}$ | Slope of output vs displacement curve | V/mm | | **Mutual Inductance** | $M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | Coupling between coils | Henry (H) | | **Coupling Coefficient** | $k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ | 0 ≤ k ≤ 1 | dimensionless | | **Differential Output (Linear Region)** | $e_{out} = 2 \, E_s \cdot \dfrac{x}{x_{max}}$ | Valid within linear range | V | | **Carrier Frequency** | $f_{carrier} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | LC resonance of coils | Hz | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** LVDTs are extensively used in **aerospace flight-control actuators**, **hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder position feedback**, **civil engineering structural health monitoring** (bridge deflection), **automotive suspension testing**, and **biomedical implants** (e.g., artificial heart valve feedback). In intelligent systems, they form the primary sensing layer in **closed-loop PID controllers** and **MEMS-based feedback architectures**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Output Voltage Expression **Starting Point — Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** Consider the primary winding excited by: $$V_p = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ The peak flux established in the primary is given by: $$\Phi_p = \dfrac{V_0}{4.44 \, f \, N_p}$$ This flux, after being guided by the movable core, links the two secondaries. **Step 1: EMF Induced in Each Secondary** For the upper secondary (S₁), with the core displaced by a distance $x$ from the null position: $$e_{s1} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_1$$ For the lower secondary (S₂): $$e_{s2} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_2$$ Where $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$ are the flux components linked with $S_1$ and $S_2$ respectively. **Step 2: Relating Flux to Core Position** The flux linkage is approximately **linear** with core displacement within the operating range. Using linear proportionality: $$\Phi_1 = \Phi_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{x}{x_{max}}\right)$$ $$\Phi_2 = \Phi_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{x_{max}}\right)$$ Where: - $\Phi_0$ = flux at null (geometric mean flux) - $x_{max}$ = maximum design displacement - $x$ = instantaneous displacement (positive = towards S₁) **Step 3: Differential Output** Since the secondaries are wound in **opposite sense** (series opposition): $$e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$$ Substituting: $$e_{out} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_0 \left[\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{x_{max}}\right) - \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{x_{max}}\right)\right]$$ $$e_{out} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_0 \cdot \dfrac{2x}{x_{max}}$$ $$\boxed{e_{out} = \dfrac{8.88 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_0}{x_{max}} \cdot x}$$ **Step 4: Sensitivity Derivation** Sensitivity is defined as the rate of change of output voltage with respect to displacement: $$S = \dfrac{de_{out}}{dx}$$ Differentiating the boxed equation: $$\boxed{S = \dfrac{8.88 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_0}{x_{max}} \quad \text{(V/mm)}}$$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — LVDT Performance Equations | Operating Condition | Core Position | Output Voltage $e_{out}$ | Phase Relation with $V_p$ | |---|---|---|---| | **Null Position** | $x = 0$ | $0$ V | — (Null) | | **Displaced toward S₁** | $x > 0$ | Positive | **In Phase** with $V_p$ | | **Displaced toward S₂** | $x < 0$ | Negative | **180° Out of Phase** with $V_p$ | | **Beyond Linear Range** | $\vert x \vert > x_{max}$ | Saturated, non-linear | Undefined | | **Full Scale (max)** | $x = \pm x_{max}$ | $\pm e_{full}$ | ± In-phase / Out-of-phase | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Phase Matters:** A bare voltmeter can measure the **magnitude** of $e_{out}$, but to determine **direction** of displacement, a **phase-sensitive detector (PSD)** or **synchronous demodulator** is mandatory. This converts the AC LVDT signal back to a DC voltage whose **polarity** indicates direction. ### 3.3 Frequency Response Analysis The dynamic response of the LVDT is fundamentally limited by the **carrier frequency** $f_c$ of the AC excitation. According to Nyquist sampling considerations for modulated signals: $$f_{max \, measurable} \approx \dfrac{f_c}{10}$$ For a typical $f_c = 5$ kHz, the maximum measurable dynamic displacement frequency is approximately **500 Hz**, which is adequate for most mechanical vibration measurements. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 LVDT Constructional Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR A[AC Excitation Source
2-10 kHz Sine Wave] -->|V_primary| B[Primary Winding
N_p turns, Central] B -->|Mutual Induction via Core| C1[Secondary S1
Upper Coil N_s] B -->|Mutual Induction via Core| C2[Secondary S2
Lower Coil N_s] C1 -->|e_s1| D[Series Opposition
Subtraction Node] C2 -->|e_s2| D D -->|e_out = e_s1 - e_s2| E[Differential Output
AC Voltage Signal] F[Movable Soft-Iron Core
Position x] -.->|Couples Flux| B F -.->|Couples Flux| C1 F -.->|Couples Flux| C2 E --> G[Phase Sensitive Detector
Demodulator] G --> H[DC Output Signal
+/- polarity indicates direction] H --> I[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier + Filter] I --> J[Display / Controller
PID / SCADA] ``` ### 4.2 Signal Conditioning Chain Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A[LVDT Sensor Head
Primary + 2 Secondaries] --> B[Oscillator Circuit
Provides Excitation] B -->|AC Carrier| A A -->|Differential AC Output| C[Phase Sensitive Detector
PSD Circuit] C -->|Rectified DC + Polarity| D[DC Amplifier
Gain Stage] D --> E[Low Pass Filter
Removes Carrier Residue] E --> F[Calibrated Output
+/- 10V DC Standard] F --> G[Microcontroller / PLC
For Intelligent Decision] G --> H[Actuator Feedback Loop
Closed Loop Control] ``` ### 4.3 Position vs. Output Voltage Characteristic Curve ```mermaid graph LR A[Core at S2 Extreme
x = -x_max] -->|e_out = -V_max| B[Null Zone
Linear Region] B -->|x = 0, e_out = 0| C[Null Zone Linear Region] C -->|e_out = +V_max| D[Core at S1 Extreme
x = +x_max] style B fill:#e8f5e8 style C fill:#e8f5e8 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:** > The diagrams above represent the **functional flow of an LVDT-based intelligent measurement system**. The **excitation oscillator** provides the carrier signal; the **LVDT sensor** modulates this carrier based on physical displacement; the **phase-sensitive detector** recovers the displacement information including its sign; the **amplifier and filter** condition the signal to industrial standards (±10 V DC or 4–20 mA); and finally the **microcontroller** uses this signal for intelligent decision-making (e.g., closing a valve, adjusting a robotic arm, or alarming on a structural deflection). ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 (Part A) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]` **Define LVDT. List any four advantages of LVDT over other displacement transducers.** **Model Answer:** LVDT stands for **Linear Variable Differential Transformer**. It is a passive inductive transducer used to convert linear displacement into a proportional electrical voltage. It consists of a primary winding excited by an AC source and two identical secondary windings connected in series opposition, coupled by a movable soft-iron core. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Infinite resolution** — output is a continuous function of core position; no friction or contact wear. 2. **High linearity** — typically ±0.1% to ±0.5% over the design range. 3. **Frictionless operation** — core does not touch the coil assembly, ensuring long life. 4. **High sensitivity and ruggedness** — works in harsh environments, vibration, and shock. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write "LVDT is used to measure weight or pressure." This is **incorrect**. LVDT specifically measures **linear displacement**. Marks are deducted if the application domain is wrong. --- #### Question 2 (Part A) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]` **Explain the concept of null position in LVDT. Why is phase-sensitive detection required?** **Model Answer:** **Null Position:** When the movable core is positioned **symmetrically** with respect to both secondaries, the magnetic coupling with $S_1$ and $S_2$ is equal. Hence $e_{s1} = e_{s2}$, and the differential output is **zero volts**. This is the reference (zero-displacement) point. **Phase-Sensitive Detection (PSD) Required Because:** A bare AC voltmeter measures only the **magnitude** of $e_{out}$, not its **phase**. Since the LVDT's output is **bidirectional** (in-phase for +x, out-of-phase for −x), a **phase-sensitive detector** is required to: 1. Determine the **direction** of displacement (sign of x). 2. Convert the **AC modulated signal** back to a **DC voltage** proportional to displacement. 3. Reject **common-mode noise** and carrier frequency artifacts. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (Part B — Option A) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a)** With the help of a neat constructional diagram, explain the working principle of LVDT. Discuss the variation of output voltage with core displacement. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An LVDT has a sensitivity of 0.5 V/mm. If the core is displaced by 8 mm from the null position, calculate (i) the output voltage, (ii) the differential secondary EMFs if each secondary contributes equally. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Construction and Working:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for construction.)* The LVDT has three windings: - **Primary (P)** — central, energized by AC excitation $V_p = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$ at frequency 1–25 kHz. - **Two secondaries (S₁ and S₂)** — identical, wound on either side, connected in **series opposition**. - **Movable soft-iron core** — attached to the displacement-sensing shaft. **Working:** 1. AC excitation produces alternating flux in the core. 2. When core is at **null**, flux coupling with S₁ and S₂ is equal → $e_{s1} = e_{s2}$ → $e_{out} = 0$. 3. When core moves **toward S₁**, coupling with S₁ increases → $e_{out} = +(K \cdot x)$ (in-phase with $V_p$). 4. When core moves **toward S₂**, coupling with S₂ increases → $e_{out} = -(K \cdot x)$ (180° out of phase with $V_p$). **Output Voltage vs. Displacement Curve:** - **Linear region:** $e_{out} = K \cdot x$ (slope = sensitivity) - **Beyond $\pm x_{max}$:** output saturates and becomes non-linear. **Part (b) — Numerical:** **Given:** - Sensitivity $S = 0.5$ V/mm - Displacement $x = 8$ mm **(i) Output Voltage:** $$e_{out} = S \times x = 0.5 \, \text{V/mm} \times 8 \, \text{mm}$$ $$\boxed{e_{out} = 4 \, \text{V}}$$ **(ii) Differential Secondary EMFs:** Since each secondary contributes equally, the output is the **difference** of two equal-but-offset quantities. Let $e_{s1}$ be the EMF of the secondary closer to the core, and $e_{s2}$ be the EMF of the farther one. Given: $e_{s1} - e_{s2} = e_{out} = 4$ V, and each contributes equally to the differential. At null, each secondary produces an EMF $E_0$. After displacement, we can model: $$e_{s1} = E_0 + \Delta E$$ $$e_{s2} = E_0 - \Delta E$$ So: $e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 2\Delta E = 4$ V → $\Delta E = 2$ V Therefore: $$\boxed{e_{s1} = E_0 + 2 \, \text{V}, \quad e_{s2} = E_0 - 2 \, \text{V}}$$ *(Note: The actual value of $E_0$ depends on excitation and frequency; typically $E_0$ is much larger than $\Delta E$. The KTU convention expects the differential $\Delta E = 2$ V to be the answer.)* --- #### Question B (Part B — Option B) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Question B (14 Marks):** **(a)** Derive the expression for output voltage of an LVDT in terms of displacement. Define sensitivity and state its units. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain why LVDT requires AC excitation. Discuss the role of a phase-sensitive demodulator with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation of Output Voltage:** *(Full derivation as in Section 3.1.)* Starting from Faraday's Law: $$e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_0 \left[\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{x_{max}}\right) - \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{x_{max}}\right)\right]$$ $$\boxed{e_{out} = \dfrac{8.88 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_0}{x_{max}} \cdot x}$$ **Sensitivity Definition:** Sensitivity of LVDT is defined as the **ratio of change in output voltage to the corresponding change in displacement**: $$S = \dfrac{de_{out}}{dx} = \dfrac{8.88 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_0}{x_{max}}$$ **Units:** V/mm (or V/m in SI). **Part (b) — AC Excitation and Phase-Sensitive Demodulation:** **Why AC Excitation?** LVDT operates on the principle of **mutual inductance**, which requires a **time-varying magnetic flux**. A DC excitation would produce a constant flux, and once steady-state is reached, no EMF would be induced in the secondaries (Faraday's Law requires $d\Phi/dt \neq 0$). Therefore, **AC excitation is mandatory** to: 1. Generate continuously time-varying flux. 2. Produce AC output that can be modulated by displacement. 3. Avoid saturation effects in the magnetic core. **Role of Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD):** A phase-sensitive detector performs three critical functions: 1. **Synchronous Rectification:** Multiplies the LVDT output with a reference signal at the same carrier frequency and phase. 2. **Direction Discrimination:** Since the LVDT output is in-phase for +x and 180° out-of-phase for −x, the PSD produces a **DC voltage whose polarity** indicates the direction of displacement. 3. **Demodulation:** Removes the carrier frequency, leaving only the displacement-proportional DC component. *(Block diagram from Section 4.2 illustrates the PSD stage.)* The output of the PSD is: $$V_{DC} = K_{PSD} \cdot x$$ Where $K_{PSD}$ is the PSD gain, and the **sign** of $V_{DC}$ directly indicates the direction of $x$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Do NOT confuse LVDT with RVDT** (Rotational Variable Differential Transformer). RVDT measures **angular** displacement; LVDT measures **linear** displacement. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for confusing these. > - **Always mention** that the secondaries are wound in **series opposition** (not series aiding). This is a key constructional detail worth **1–2 marks**. > - In numerical problems, **include units** in every step (V, mm, Hz). Marks are deducted for unitless final answers. > - For the **AC excitation question**, the explanation must include the phrase "time-varying flux" or "$d\Phi/dt \neq 0$" — this is the examiner's keyword. > - **Never** state that LVDT works on DC. This is the **most common error** and costs full marks in Part A. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — LVDT** - **Full Form:** Linear Variable Differential Transformer. - **Classification:** Passive, inductive, electromechanical transducer. - **Core Function:** Converts linear displacement into AC voltage signal. - **Principle:** Mutual inductance (Faraday's Law). - **Construction:** 1 primary (central) + 2 secondaries (symmetric) + 1 movable soft-iron core. - **Wiring of Secondaries:** **Series opposition** (anti-series) — critical for differential output. - **Excitation:** AC sine wave, **2 kHz to 10 kHz** typical. - **Null Position:** Core at center → $e_{out} = 0$ V → reference point for zero displacement. - **Direction Sensing:** In-phase output = +x; 180° out-of-phase output = −x. - **Sensitivity:** $S = \dfrac{e_{out}}{x}$, units V/mm. - **Output Voltage:** $e_{out} = K \cdot x$ (linear region). - **Key Advantage:** **Infinite resolution** (continuous output, no friction). - **Key Limitation:** Requires AC excitation (not DC-compatible). - **Signal Conditioning:** Must use **Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD)** for direction and DC conversion. - **Formula to Memorize:** $e_{out} = \dfrac{8.88 \, f \, N_s \, \Phi_0}{x_{max}} \cdot x$ - **Carrier Frequency:** Determines dynamic bandwidth ($f_{max} \approx f_c/10$). - **Applications:** Aerospace actuators, hydraulic cylinder feedback, structural deflection monitoring, robotic arm position control. - **Distinguish from RVDT:** RVDT = angular, LVDT = linear. - **Key Metric to Quote in Exams:** Sensitivity range **0.5 V/mm to 5 V/mm**. - **Shielding Required:** Yes, against stray magnetic fields. - **Material of Core:** Soft iron or nickel-iron alloy (high permeability, low hysteresis).

topics.title: Velocity - Encoders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Velocity - Encoders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Encoders** are electromechanical / optoelectronic transducer devices that directly or indirectly convert the **rotational speed (angular velocity, $\omega$)** or **linear speed ($v$)** of a moving body into a **measurable electrical signal** (analog voltage or digital pulse train), enabling real-time speed feedback in closed-loop control systems of intelligent machines and robots.

In the taxonomy of **Components of Intelligent Systems**, velocity encoders are classified under the *Perception / Sensory Layer* and constitute a critical branch of **tachometric transducers**. The most common implementations encountered in KTU-referenced robotics platforms are:

1. **Optical Incremental Encoders operating in frequency mode** (digital output)
2. **DC Tachogenerators** (analog output)
3. **Hall-Effect Speed Sensors** (used in brushless DC motor commutation)
4. **Resolver-derived velocity** (computed from $\theta$ differentiation)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Speedometer of a Bicycle:**
> Imagine pedaling a bicycle. The faster you pedal, the faster the wheel spins. A velocity encoder is essentially a tiny, precise "speedometer for a machine." Instead of a needle, it produces a stream of electrical pulses — like the *click-click-click* sound of a mechanical counter — where the **rate of clicks (frequency)** is directly proportional to the shaft speed. If the controller counts **1,000 clicks per second**, and the encoder wheel has **1,000 markings**, the shaft has completed exactly **1 revolution per second**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

- **PPR (Pulses Per Revolution):** The number of electrical pulses generated for one full $360°$ rotation of the encoder shaft. Typical industrial values: **96, 100, 360, 500, 1000, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192 PPR**.
- **CPR (Counts Per Revolution):** In quadrature decoding, $CPR = 4 \times PPR$ (because of the $\times 4$ decoding logic on channels A and B).
- **Resolution:** The smallest measurable angular increment: $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{CPR}$.
- **Maximum Operating Frequency ($f_{max}$):** The upper limit of pulse rate, typically **20 kHz to 1 MHz** depending on the device class.
- **Maximum Shaft Speed (RPM):** Computed as $N_{max} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_{max}}{PPR}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Incremental Optical Encoder in Velocity Mode

An incremental encoder consists of a **rotating disk** with evenly spaced transparent and opaque slots, an **LED light source**, and a **photodetector**. As the disk rotates:

1. The light beam is **chopped** at each slot, producing a **pulse train** on the photodetector output.
2. The **frequency $f$ (Hz)** of this pulse train is proportional to the shaft's angular velocity.
3. A second channel (Channel B), physically offset by **90° (one quarter slot)**, is used to determine the **direction of rotation** and to perform **quadrature decoding** ($\times 4$ resolution enhancement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Velocity" mode instead of "Position" mode?**
> The same hardware (incremental encoder) can be used in two distinct operating modes:
> - **Position Mode:** Count the cumulative number of pulses (integration of $f$ over time).
> - **Velocity Mode:** Measure only the **instantaneous frequency** (differentiation of position over time).
> Velocity mode is preferred in real-time motion control because it provides a continuous, low-latency signal suitable for **PID feedback loops**.

## 2.2 Quadrature Decoding Logic

The two channels (A and B) produce square waves in **quadrature** (90° phase shift). By detecting both the **rising and falling edges** of both channels, the effective pulse count is quadrupled:

$$f_{effective} = 4 \times f_{channel}$$

This means a **500 PPR** encoder, when decoded in quadrature, behaves like a **2000 CPR** encoder, quadrupling the velocity resolution.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application Note** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angular velocity from pulse frequency | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot f}{PPR}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | Single-channel frequency mode |
| Quadrature angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot f}{4 \cdot PPR}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | When $\times 4$ decoding is used |
| Revolutions per minute | $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{PPR}$ | $\text{RPM}$ | Direct speed readout in RPM |
| Linear velocity (wheel/robot) | $v = \omega \cdot R$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $R$ = wheel radius in meters |
| Linear velocity from encoder | $v = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot f \cdot R}{PPR}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Combined rotational-to-linear form |
| Resolution (angular) | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{CPR}$ | degrees | Smallest measurable step |
| Maximum permissible RPM | $N_{max} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_{max}}{PPR}$ | $\text{RPM}$ | Set by encoder datasheet |
| Tachogenerator EMF (DC) | $E = K_t \cdot \omega$ | $\text{V}$ | $K_t$ in $\text{V/(rad/s)}$ |
| Velocity by differentiation | $v = \dfrac{dx}{dt} \approx \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Numerical differentiation of position |
| Differentiation error (zero-order hold) | $\sigma_v = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{\Delta t}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Quantization noise amplifies with smaller $\Delta t$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** In the table above, all absolute-value bars (e.g., magnitude of error) are rendered using $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. KTU board examinations expect the **SI unit to be written explicitly** with every numerical answer — never omit units.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Intelligent Systems

- **CNC Machine Tools:** Spindle speed feedback for adaptive feed-rate control.
- **Mobile Robots (AGV/AMR):** Wheel odometry for SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping).
- **Servo Motor Drives:** Inner velocity loop in **cascade PID** architecture.
- **BLDC Motor Commutation:** Hall sensors provide 6-step commutation; back-EMF-based sensorless methods complement this.
- **Conveyor and Process Automation:** Speed feedback for tension control in web-handling machinery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Angular Velocity from Pulse Frequency

**Given:** An incremental encoder produces $PPR$ pulses per revolution, and the measured output frequency is $f$ Hz (pulses per second).

**Step 1 — Define pulse count per revolution:**

$$N_{pulse} = PPR \text{ (pulses / revolution)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute time for one revolution:**

The time taken by the shaft to produce $PPR$ pulses is the time per revolution $T_{rev}$:

$$T_{rev} = \dfrac{PPR}{f} \text{ (seconds per revolution)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to revolutions per second:**

$$n = \dfrac{1}{T_{rev}} = \dfrac{f}{PPR} \text{ (revolutions per second)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to angular velocity using $\omega = 2\pi n$:**

$$\omega = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{f}{PPR} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{PPR} \text{ (rad/s)}$$

> This is the **fundamental equation** linking electrical signal frequency to mechanical angular velocity.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Linear Velocity of a Wheel-Mounted Robot

**Given:** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheels of radius $R$ m, driven through a **gear reduction of ratio $G$** (output shaft : input shaft), and the encoder (on the motor shaft) produces $PPR$ pulses/rev with measured frequency $f$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Angular velocity at the encoder (motor) shaft:**

$$\omega_{motor} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{PPR}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the gear ratio** to find the wheel (output) angular velocity:

$$\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{G} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{PPR \cdot G}$$

**Step 3 — Convert angular to linear velocity** using the rolling-without-slipping condition $v = \omega \cdot R$:

$$v_{robot} = \omega_{wheel} \cdot R = \dfrac{2\pi f \cdot R}{PPR \cdot G}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

> **Problem:** A mobile robot uses a wheel of diameter $D = 20$ cm driven directly by a DC motor. An optical encoder mounted on the motor shaft has $PPR = 500$. The encoder output frequency is measured to be $f = 2500$ Hz. Calculate:
> (a) The angular velocity of the wheel.
> (b) The linear velocity of the robot in m/s.
> (c) The resolution of the encoder in degrees (in quadrature mode).

### Solution

**(a) Angular velocity:**

Applying the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{PPR} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 2500}{500}$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{5000\pi}{500} = 10\pi \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(b) Linear velocity:**

Wheel radius $R = D/2 = 0.10$ m.

$$v = \omega \cdot R = 10\pi \times 0.10 = \pi \approx 3.1416 \text{ m/s}$$

**(c) Resolution in quadrature mode:**

In quadrature, $CPR = 4 \times PPR = 4 \times 500 = 2000$ counts/rev.

$$\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{CPR} = \dfrac{360°}{2000} = 0.18°$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Velocity Sensing Technologies

| **Method** | **Output Type** | **Resolution** | **Bandwidth** | **Best-Use Case** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Incremental Encoder (frequency mode) | Digital pulse train | High (up to 0.04°) | High (kHz) | Servo velocity loop | Requires counter/timer hardware |
| DC Tachogenerator | Analog DC voltage | Continuous, infinite | Moderate | Legacy analog drives | Brush wear, ripple, drift |
| Resolver-derived $\omega$ | Analog (sin/cos) | 12–16 bit | High | Aerospace, harsh environments | Expensive, needs RDC chip |
| Hall-Effect Sensor | 6-step digital | Coarse (60° elec.) | High | BLDC commutation | Too coarse for precise velocity |
| Back-EMF Estimation | Analog → digital | Medium | Medium | Sensorless BLDC | Low-speed inaccuracy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Velocity Encoder System

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **signal flow** from the physical shaft rotation to the digital velocity reading used by the intelligent system controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MECH[MECHANICAL DOMAIN]
        shaftA[Shaft Rotation]
        shaftB[Wheel or Motor Output]
        shaftA --> shaftB
    end

    subgraph ENC[ENCODER MODULE]
        diskA[Optical Disk with PPR Slots]
        ledA[LED Light Source]
        pdetA[Photodetector A]
        pdetB[Photodetector B]
        sqA[Squarer A]
        sqB[Squarer B]
        shaftB --> diskA
        ledA --> diskA
        diskA --> pdetA
        diskA --> pdetB
        pdetA --> sqA
        pdetB --> sqB
    end

    subgraph DEC[QUADRATURE DECODER]
        edgeA[Rising Edge Detect A]
        edgeB[Falling Edge Detect A]
        edgeC[Rising Edge Detect B]
        edgeD[Falling Edge Detect B]
        muxA[4X MUX Logic]
        sqA --> edgeA
        sqA --> edgeB
        sqB --> edgeC
        sqB --> edgeD
        edgeA --> muxA
        edgeB --> muxA
        edgeC --> muxA
        edgeD --> muxA
    end

    subgraph PROC[VELOCITY COMPUTATION UNIT]
        freqA[Frequency Measurement Module]
        calcA[omega equals 2 pi f divided by PPR]
        rpmA[RPM equals 60 f divided by PPR]
        linA[Linear Velocity equals omega times R]
        muxA --> freqA
        freqA --> calcA
        calcA --> rpmA
        rpmA --> linA
    end

    subgraph CTRL[CONTROLLER LAYER]
        pidA[PID Velocity Loop]
        refA[Reference Speed Setpoint]
        errA[Error Signal]
        outA[Actuator PWM Command]
        refA --> errA
        linA --> errA
        errA --> pidA
        pidA --> outA
    end

    outA --> shaftB
```

## 4.2 Quadrature Waveform Timing Diagram (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    chanA[CHANNEL A Square Wave]
    chanB[CHANNEL B Square Wave 90 degrees phase shift]
    out[DECODED DIRECTION SIGNAL]
    chanA --> out
    chanB --> out
    note1[When A leads B by 90 degrees: CW Rotation]
    note2[When B leads A by 90 degrees: CCW Rotation]
    out --> note1
    out --> note2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this architecture matters for the KTU board exam:**
> The examiner expects students to be able to **draw a labeled block diagram** showing the path from the rotating shaft → optical disk → photodetectors → squarers → quadrature decoder → frequency measurement → velocity calculation → controller. The block diagram above is the **canonical model** for an answer worth 7 marks.

## 4.3 Tachogenerator Equivalent Circuit (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    shaftT[Motor Shaft Input]
    armT[Armature Winding]
    magT[Permanent Magnet Field]
    emfT[Induced EMF E equals Kt times omega]
    filT[RC Low Pass Filter]
    adcT[ADC to Digital Controller]
    shaftT --> armT
    magT --> armT
    armT --> emfT
    emfT --> filT
    filT --> adcT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain how an optical incremental encoder can be used to measure the angular velocity of a rotating shaft. Mention the role of quadrature decoding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An optical incremental encoder consists of a slotted disk mounted on the rotating shaft, an LED source, and photodetectors. As the disk rotates, the light beam is interrupted, producing a **pulse train** at the photodetector output. The **frequency $f$** of this pulse train is proportional to the **angular velocity $\omega$** through the relation:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{PPR}$$

where $PPR$ is the pulses-per-revolution of the encoder disk. **Quadrature decoding** uses two photodetectors offset by 90° electrical (Channels A and B). By detecting both the rising and falling edges of both channels, the **effective resolution is multiplied by 4**, allowing more accurate velocity measurement, especially at low speeds where pulse counts per unit time are small.

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark for the working principle, 1 mark for the formula, and 1 mark for the role of quadrature decoding.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between a DC tachogenerator and an optical incremental encoder as velocity sensors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Feature** | **DC Tachogenerator** | **Optical Incremental Encoder** |
|---|---|---|
| Output signal | Analog DC voltage | Digital pulse train |
| Sensitivity | $K_t$ (V per rad/s) | $PPR$ (pulses per rev) |
| Resolution | Continuous (infinite) | Discrete (quantized) |
| Direction sensing | Requires additional polarity circuit | Inherent via quadrature |
| Maintenance | Brush wear | No contact → long life |
| Typical application | Legacy analog drives, large motors | Modern digital servo systems |

*(1 mark per distinguishing point, up to 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (ESE Pattern: 1 Question × 14 Marks)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an optical incremental encoder used as a **velocity sensor**. Derive the relation between pulse frequency and angular velocity. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mobile robot uses wheels of diameter **15 cm** driven through a **gear reduction of 10:1** by a DC motor. An optical encoder on the motor shaft has **PPR = 1000** and produces an output frequency of **4 kHz**. Calculate the **(i)** motor shaft angular velocity, **(ii)** wheel angular velocity, and **(iii)** linear velocity of the robot in m/s. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Working and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Shaft → Slotted Disk → LED + Photodetector A,B
     → Squarers → Quadrature Decoder
     → Frequency Counter → Velocity Calculator → ω, v
```

**Working Description (2 marks):**
The slotted disk rotates with the shaft. Each slot allows LED light to pass to the photodetector, generating one pulse. The pulses are squared and decoded in quadrature to give 4× resolution. The frequency of the decoded pulse train is measured and used to compute $\omega$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

As shown in Section 3.1, the angular velocity is:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{PPR} \text{ rad/s}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given data:**
- Wheel diameter $D = 15$ cm → $R = 0.075$ m
- Gear ratio $G = 10$
- Encoder $PPR = 1000$
- Pulse frequency $f = 4000$ Hz

**(i) Motor shaft angular velocity (3 marks):**

$$\omega_{motor} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{PPR} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 4000}{1000} = 8\pi \approx 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Wheel angular velocity (2 marks):**

$$\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{G} = \dfrac{8\pi}{10} = 0.8\pi \approx 2.5133 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) Linear velocity of robot (2 marks):**

$$v = \omega_{wheel} \times R = 0.8\pi \times 0.075 = 0.06\pi \approx 0.1885 \text{ m/s}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **DC tachogenerator** as a velocity transducer. Derive the expression for the induced EMF. List any **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of using tachogenerators. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An incremental encoder with **PPR = 500** is mounted on a motor shaft. The encoder is operated in **quadrature (×4) decoding mode**. If the controller measures **8000 pulses per second**, calculate the **(i)** effective CPR, **(ii)** angular velocity in rad/s, **(iii)** resolution in degrees, and **(iv)** RPM of the shaft. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) DC Tachogenerator (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):** A DC tachogenerator is a small DC machine whose armature is mechanically coupled to the motor shaft under measurement. The field is provided by a **permanent magnet** (or separate field winding). As the shaft rotates, the armature conductors cut the magnetic flux, inducing an EMF by **Faraday's Law**:

$$E = K_t \cdot \omega$$

where $K_t$ is the **tachogenerator constant** in V/(rad/s), and $\omega$ is the angular velocity. The polarity of the induced voltage indicates the direction of rotation.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
The induced EMF in a DC machine is $E = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$ where $\Phi$ is the flux per pole, $Z$ conductors, $P$ poles, $A$ parallel paths. For a permanent-magnet machine, $\Phi$ is constant, so $E$ is **linearly proportional to $N$ (RPM)**, hence to $\omega$.

**Advantages (1 mark, any two):**
1. Continuous analog output — easy to interface with analog PID.
2. Direct measurement of $\omega$ (no need for differentiation of position).

**Disadvantages (1 mark, any two):**
1. Brush and commutator wear → limited life and maintenance.
2. Output ripple due to commutator segments — requires filtering.
3. Temperature drift of $K_t$ and field flux.

**(b) Numerical on Incremental Encoder (7 marks):**

**Given:** $PPR = 500$, Quadrature mode, $f = 8000$ Hz.

**(i) Effective CPR (2 marks):**

$$CPR = 4 \times PPR = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**(ii) Angular velocity (2 marks):**

In quadrature, the effective pulses per revolution is $4 \times PPR = 2000$, so:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{4 \times PPR} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 8000}{2000} = 8\pi \approx 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

> *Equivalently, using single-channel equation: $\omega = 2\pi f / PPR = 2\pi(8000)/500 = 32\pi$ rad/s if interpreted as single-channel count rate. KTU boards accept either interpretation if consistently used. (Standard interpretation: in quadrature, frequency is 4× the per-channel frequency, so use the divided form.)*

**(iii) Resolution in degrees (1.5 marks):**

$$\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{CPR} = \dfrac{360°}{2000} = 0.18°$$

**(iv) RPM (1.5 marks):**

$$N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{4 \cdot PPR} = \dfrac{60 \times 8000}{2000} = 240 \text{ RPM}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Velocity-Encoder Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 4 in quadrature mode** — students often write $\omega = 2\pi f / PPR$ even when the encoder is in quadrature, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing RPM and rad/s** — when a problem gives RPM, first convert to $\omega$ using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Writing $N$ where $\omega$ is required (or vice versa) is a 0.5-mark deduction.
> 3. **Omitting units in the final answer** — KTU strictly requires SI units; missing units cost 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert mm/cm to m** in linear velocity problems — this is a recurring error worth 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing PPR with CPR** — PPR is the disk specification; CPR is the decoded (often $\times 4$) count. Examiners do not award marks if the wrong variable is used in the resolution formula.
> 6. **Block diagram missing labels** — a block diagram without labels on the signal path (LED → photodetector → squarer → decoder → counter → velocity) is considered incomplete; 2 marks may be lost.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Velocity Encoder** = sensor that converts shaft speed $\omega$ into a measurable signal (pulse train or analog voltage).
- **Optical Incremental Encoder** is the most common digital velocity sensor; uses slotted disk + LED + photodetector.
- **Core Formula (single channel):** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{PPR}$ rad/s.
- **Core Formula (quadrature ×4):** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{4 \cdot PPR}$ rad/s.
- **RPM formula:** $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{PPR}$ (or $N = \dfrac{60 f}{4 \cdot PPR}$ in quadrature).
- **Linear velocity of wheel:** $v = \omega \cdot R$, where $R$ is in **meters**.
- **CPR (Counts Per Revolution)** = $4 \times PPR$ in quadrature mode; used in resolution formula $\theta_{res} = 360°/CPR$.
- **DC Tachogenerator:** $E = K_t \cdot \omega$; analog output, continuous resolution, but suffers from brush wear and ripple.
- **Quadrature decoding** provides **direction information** (A leads B → CW, B leads A → CCW) and **×4 resolution enhancement**.
- **Maximum operating frequency** $f_{max}$ of the encoder dictates the maximum measurable RPM: $N_{max} = 60 f_{max} / PPR$.
- **In intelligent systems**, velocity encoders form the **inner feedback loop** of cascade PID control in servomotors, BLDC drives, CNC spindles, and mobile-robot wheel odometry.
- **For KTU numerical problems**, always state: Given data → Formula → Substitution → Final answer with units.
- **Differentiating position to get velocity** amplifies quantization noise — this is why direct frequency-mode velocity measurement is preferred over numerical differentiation in high-precision systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tachogenerators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tachogenerators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tachogenerator** (also called a **tacho-generator**, **tachometer generator**, or **speed voltage generator**) is a precision electromechanical transducer that converts angular velocity (rotational speed) of a rotating shaft into a proportional analog electrical voltage signal. It functions as an **electromechanical speed-sensing actuator** used primarily in closed-loop feedback control systems to provide continuous, real-time measurement and monitoring of rotational speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A tachogenerator is fundamentally a small, specially-designed electrical machine (either DC or AC) operated as a generator, whose output voltage is directly proportional to the rotational speed of its rotor, expressed mathematically as $E_g \propto N$, where $E_g$ is the generated EMF in Volts and $N$ is the speed in rpm.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Tachogenerators

Tachogenerators are broadly classified into two main families based on the type of electrical output they produce:

**1. DC Tachogenerator**
- Produces a DC output voltage proportional to shaft speed.
- Operates on Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction with a commutator-brush arrangement for rectification.
- Subtypes: (a) Permanent Magnet (PM) type, (b) Electromagnetically Excited (EM) type.

**2. AC Tachogenerator**
- Produces an AC output voltage whose amplitude is proportional to shaft speed.
- Further subdivided into:
  - **(a) Synchronous-type AC Tachogenerator:** Rotor is a permanent magnet; stator carries a single-phase winding.
  - **(b) Asynchronous (Induction/Drag-cup) type AC Tachogenerator:** Uses a thin aluminum drag-cup rotor and a two-phase stator (reference winding and quadrature/output winding).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cycling down a hill and you have a small bicycle dynamo (generator) attached to your wheel. As you pedal faster, the dynamo spins faster, and the headlight it powers becomes brighter because the generated voltage increases. A **tachogenerator works on the exact same physical principle**, but it is built with extreme precision so that its output voltage changes **linearly and predictably** with rotational speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> - **Slow rotation → Low voltage output**
> - **Fast rotation → High voltage output**
> - **The voltage reading on a voltmeter tells you exactly how fast the shaft is spinning.**

In a practical industrial setting, think of a tachogenerator as the "**speedometer of machines**." Just as the speedometer in your car converts wheel rotation into a needle position on a dial, a tachogenerator converts shaft rotation into an electrical signal that can be read by a controller, displayed on a panel meter, or used to regulate motor speed automatically.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used to characterize tachogenerator performance:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Generated EMF | $E_g$ | **Volt (V)** | Output voltage proportional to speed |
| Shaft Speed | $N$ | **rpm** (revolutions per minute) | Input rotational speed |
| Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | **rad/s** | $N \times \dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ |
| Sensitivity / Voltage Gradient | $K_v$ | **V/rpm** or **V/(rad/s)** | Slope of the E vs N characteristic |
| Magnetic Flux per pole | $\Phi$ | **Weber (Wb)** | Flux linking the armature |
| Number of poles | $P$ | dimensionless | Total magnetic poles |
| Number of armature conductors | $Z$ | dimensionless | Total active conductors |
| Number of parallel paths | $A$ | dimensionless | Depends on winding type |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** The fundamental relationship $E_g = K_v \cdot N$ is the **most important equation** for any tachogenerator problem. All derivations reduce to finding $K_v$ correctly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.1.1 Faraday's Law Foundation

Every tachogenerator, whether DC or AC, operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, which states:

> *"The magnitude of the EMF induced in a conductor is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkages with respect to time."*

Mathematically:

$$
e = -\dfrac{d\Psi}{dt} = -N\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

where:
- $e$ = induced EMF in Volts
- $\Psi = N\Phi$ = total flux linkages
- $N$ = number of turns
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux in Webers

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The conversion of mechanical rotation into a proportional electrical signal follows this structured logic:

1. **Mechanical Input:** The shaft of the tachogenerator is mechanically coupled to the rotating shaft whose speed is to be measured (e.g., the motor shaft in a servo system).

2. **Flux Establishment:** A magnetic field is established in the air gap. In **PM-type** units, this flux is produced by permanent magnets (typically Alnico or rare-earth materials). In **EM-type** units, a separate field winding supplied with constant current produces the flux.

3. **Relative Motion:** As the rotor (armature) rotates within the magnetic field, conductors on the rotor sweep through the magnetic flux lines, cutting them at a rate proportional to the rotational speed.

4. **EMF Generation:** According to Faraday's Law, this cutting of flux induces an EMF in each armature conductor.

5. **Commutation / Pickup:**
   - In **DC tachogenerators**: A **commutator-brush assembly** rectifies the AC generated in the armature winding into a unidirectional (DC) output voltage.
   - In **AC tachogenerators**: The output is taken directly from the stator winding as an AC voltage.

6. **Output:** The terminal voltage $V_t$ measured across the output terminals is directly proportional to the shaft speed $N$, with the proportionality constant being the **sensitivity** $K_v$ of the device.

### 2.1.3 Detailed Working of DC Tachogenerator (PM Type)

- The stator houses **permanent magnets** that create a constant, unchanging magnetic field $\Phi$ in the air gap.
- The rotor (armature) is wound with conductors and rotates inside this constant field.
- As the armature rotates, conductors cut the magnetic flux, inducing an alternating EMF in each coil side.
- The **commutator** mechanically rectifies this AC EMF into a unidirectional (DC) output.
- The **brushes** sliding on the commutator surface pick up the DC voltage and deliver it to the external load.
- Since $\Phi$ is constant, the output voltage depends *only* on the speed $N$, giving the linear relationship $E_g \propto N$.

### 2.1.4 Detailed Working of AC Tachogenerator (Induction Drag-Cup Type)

- The stator has **two windings** placed physically at **90 electrical degrees** apart (in space quadrature):
  - **Reference Winding (R):** Supplied with a fixed-frequency, fixed-amplitude AC excitation voltage.
  - **Output / Quadrature Winding (Q):** The output winding, where the speed-proportional voltage is induced.
- The rotor is a **thin hollow aluminum or copper cup (drag cup)** that fits over a stationary iron core.
- The reference winding produces an alternating magnetic flux along one axis (say, the d-axis).
- When the rotor is stationary, the flux from the reference winding is purely along the d-axis and **does not link** the output winding (which is at 90°). Hence, output voltage = 0.
- When the rotor rotates, it "drags" the flux lines with it (rotor flux-cutting action), creating a **cross-axis flux component** along the q-axis.
- This cross-axis flux is time-varying (due to AC excitation) and speed-modulated, inducing an EMF in the output winding whose amplitude is proportional to speed.
- The frequency of the output voltage equals the **frequency of the reference winding supply**, **not** the rotation frequency. This is a key advantage.

## 2.2 EMF Equation of a DC Tachogenerator

The generated EMF in any DC machine is given by the classical machine equation:

$$
E_g = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}
$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- Average EMF per conductor = $\dfrac{P \cdot \Phi}{60}$ Volts (flux cut per revolution per second)
- Number of conductors in series per parallel path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$
- Total EMF = average EMF per conductor × conductors in series

**For Wave Winding:** $A = 2$, so

$$
E_g = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{120}
$$

**For Lap Winding:** $A = P$, so

$$
E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60}
$$

**Sensitivity (Voltage Gradient):**

$$
K_v = \dfrac{E_g}{N} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A} \quad \text{[V/rpm]}
$$

## 2.3 EMF Equation of an AC Tachogenerator (Drag-Cup)

For an induction-type AC tachogenerator, the RMS output voltage is given by:

$$
E_{out} = K_{ac} \cdot N
$$

where $K_{ac}$ is the sensitivity constant depending on the reference winding voltage $V_{ref}$, turns ratio, and machine dimensions:

$$
K_{ac} = \dfrac{k \cdot V_{ref}}{f}
$$

where $k$ is a machine constant and $f$ is the supply frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For AC drag-cup tachogenerators, the output frequency is constant (equal to the reference supply frequency), and only the amplitude varies with speed.** This makes signal conditioning (filtering, amplification) much simpler than for the synchronous type, where the frequency also changes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{P\Phi ZN}{60A}$ | General DC machine EMF equation | V |
| 2 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60}$ | EMF for lap-wound ($A = P$) | V |
| 3 | $E_g = \dfrac{P\Phi Z N}{120}$ | EMF for wave-wound ($A = 2$) | V |
| 4 | $K_v = \dfrac{E_g}{N} = \dfrac{P\Phi Z}{60A}$ | Tachogenerator sensitivity | V/rpm |
| 5 | $E_{out} = K_{ac} \cdot N$ | AC tachogenerator output | V |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Conversion: rpm to rad/s | rad/s |
| 7 | $K_\omega = \dfrac{E_g}{\omega} = \dfrac{60 \cdot K_v}{2\pi}$ | Sensitivity in rad/s form | V·s/rad |
| 8 | $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$ | Terminal voltage under load | V |
| 9 | $\text{Error} = \dfrac{E_{g,ideal} - E_{g,actual}}{E_{g,ideal}} \times 100\%$ | Linearity / accuracy | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall to Avoid:** Never confuse $K_v$ (V/rpm) with $K_\omega$ (V·s/rad). They are related by $K_\omega = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} K_v = \dfrac{30}{\pi} K_v$. Examiners frequently test this conversion.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Tachogenerators are the **workhorse speed sensors** of industrial automation. They are deployed in:

- **DC and AC motor speed control loops** as feedback elements.
- **Paper mills, rolling mills, and textile machines** where precise speed matching between multiple drives is critical.
- **Robotics and CNC machines** for servo-loop stabilization.
- **Tape recorders, turntables, and gramophones** for speed control.
- **Marine and aerospace instrumentation** for propeller and turbine speed measurement.
- **Elevator control systems** for safe speed regulation.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: EMF Equation of a DC Tachogenerator

### Given Assumptions
- The tachogenerator is a DC machine with $P$ poles.
- Total number of armature conductors = $Z$.
- Flux per pole = $\Phi$ Webers (assumed constant in PM-type).
- Rotor speed = $N$ rpm.
- Winding is **lap-type** with $A = P$ parallel paths.

### Step 1: Convert Speed to Revolutions per Second

$$
n = \dfrac{N}{60} \quad \text{[revolutions per second]}
$$

**Reasoning:** All EMF derivations are based on flux cut **per second**, so speed must be expressed in rps, not rpm.

### Step 2: Flux Cut by One Conductor per Revolution

For a multi-pole machine, the flux cut by a conductor in one complete revolution is:

$$
\Phi_{cut} = P \cdot \Phi \quad \text{[Webers]}
$$

**Reasoning:** In a 2-pole machine, one conductor passes through 1 North and 1 South pole in one revolution, cutting $2\Phi$ Webers. In a $P$-pole machine, it cuts $P$ pole-pair crossings, hence $P\Phi$ total.

### Step 3: Flux Cut per Conductor per Second

$$
\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = P\Phi \cdot n = P\Phi \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \quad \text{[Wb/s]}
$$

**Reasoning:** Multiplying the flux cut per revolution by the revolutions per second gives the time-rate of flux change.

### Step 4: EMF Induced per Conductor

By Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced in a single conductor is:

$$
e_{cond} = \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60} \quad \text{[Volts]}
$$

### Step 5: Total Number of Conductors in Series per Parallel Path

In a DC armature, the $Z$ conductors are distributed among $A$ parallel paths. Each path has $Z/A$ conductors connected in series.

$$
Z_{series} = \dfrac{Z}{A}
$$

### Step 6: Total EMF per Parallel Path (and hence the total generated EMF)

$$
E_g = e_{cond} \times Z_{series} = \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60} \times \dfrac{Z}{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}}
$$

### Step 7: Substitute Specific Winding Types

**For Lap Winding** ($A = P$):

$$
E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 P} = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60}
$$

**For Wave Winding** ($A = 2$):

$$
E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 \times 2} = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{120}
$$

### Step 8: Sensitivity Derivation

Since $E_g \propto N$ (with $\Phi, Z, P, A$ all being machine constants), we can write:

$$
E_g = K_v \cdot N
$$

Comparing with the general equation:

$$
K_v = \dfrac{P \Phi Z}{60 A} \quad \text{[V/rpm]}
$$

### Step 9: Conversion to SI Form (rad/s)

If speed is given in rad/s ($\omega = 2\pi N / 60$), the sensitivity in SI units is:

$$
K_\omega = \dfrac{E_g}{\omega} = \dfrac{K_v \cdot N}{2\pi N / 60} = \dfrac{60 \cdot K_v}{2\pi} = \dfrac{30 K_v}{\pi}
$$

Therefore:

$$
K_\omega = \dfrac{30 K_v}{\pi} \quad \text{[V·s/rad]}
$$

### Step 10: Terminal Voltage Under Load

When the tachogenerator supplies a load current $I_L$ to an external resistance $R_L$, the armature current $I_a = I_L$, and the terminal voltage is:

$$
V_t = E_g - I_a R_a
$$

where $R_a$ is the armature resistance (typically 1–100 $\Omega$ for small tachogenerators).

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A permanent-magnet DC tachogenerator has 4 poles, 500 armature conductors, and a flux per pole of 5 mWb. The armature is wave-wound. Calculate (a) the generated EMF at 1500 rpm, (b) the sensitivity in V/rpm, and (c) the terminal voltage when delivering 50 mA to a load, given armature resistance = 2 $\Omega$.

**Given:**
- $P = 4$
- $Z = 500$
- $\Phi = 5 \text{ mWb} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$
- $N = 1500$ rpm
- Wave-wound: $A = 2$
- $I_a = 50$ mA $= 0.05$ A
- $R_a = 2$ $\Omega$

**Solution:**

**(a) Generated EMF:**

$$
E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} = \dfrac{4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 \times 1500}{60 \times 2}
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
E_g = \dfrac{4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 \times 1500}{120}
$$

$$
E_g = \dfrac{4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 750000}{120}
$$

$$
E_g = \dfrac{15000}{120} = 125 \text{ V}
$$

**(b) Sensitivity:**

$$
K_v = \dfrac{E_g}{N} = \dfrac{125}{1500} = 0.0833 \text{ V/rpm} = 83.3 \text{ mV/rpm}
$$

**(c) Terminal Voltage under load:**

$$
V_t = E_g - I_a R_a = 125 - (0.05 \times 2) = 125 - 0.1 = 124.9 \text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** At 1500 rpm, the voltage drop across the armature (0.1 V) is negligible compared to $E_g$ (125 V), giving a very small loading error of **0.08%**. This demonstrates the inherent accuracy of well-designed tachogenerators.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | DC Tachogenerator | AC Synchronous Tachogenerator | AC Asynchronous (Drag-Cup) Tachogenerator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | DC voltage | AC voltage | AC voltage |
| Output vs Speed | $E \propto N$ | $E \propto N$ (freq $\propto N$) | $E \propto N$ (freq = constant) |
| Linearity | Good (PM type) | Poor at low speeds | Excellent |
| Ripple | Moderate (commutator) | High | Very Low |
| Brushes required | Yes | No (brushless rotor) | No (brushless rotor) |
| Maintenance | High (brush wear) | Low | Very Low |
| Temperature sensitivity | Medium | High (rotor magnet) | Low |
| Typical accuracy | ±1% to ±5% | ±2% to ±10% | ±0.1% to ±1% |
| Phase shift issue | None | None | Minor (quadrature error) |
| Cost | Medium | Low | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students often confuse the "synchronous" AC tachogenerator with the "asynchronous/induction" type. Remember: the **synchronous** type has a permanent magnet rotor (output frequency varies with speed), whereas the **asynchronous/induction** type has a drag-cup rotor and constant output frequency.
