The **piezoelectric effect** (discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie, 1880) is the core phenomenon behind most modern ultrasonic transducers:

1. **Direct Effect:** Mechanical stress applied to a crystal (e.g., Quartz, PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) generates a measurable voltage across its faces.
2. **Converse Effect:** An applied alternating voltage causes the crystal to mechanically deform (expand and contract) at the same frequency.
3. **Resonance:** When the AC drive frequency matches the *mechanical resonance* of the crystal, deformation amplitude is maximized — this is the operating frequency of the transducer.

**Why PZT dominates:** Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT) has a coupling coefficient $k \approx 0.5$–$0.6$, far higher than quartz ($k \approx 0.1$), giving superior energy conversion efficiency.

## 2.2 Magnetostrictive Transducers

Certain ferromagnetic materials (Nickel, Permendur, Alfer) change physical dimensions when placed in a magnetic field — this is the **magnetostrictive effect**. They are robust, generate high acoustic power, and are preferred for **low-frequency, high-power** SONAR (typically 10–50 kHz underwater).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** Magnetostrictive transducers cannot operate above ~100 kHz due to eddy-current losses and mechanical constraints. Piezoelectric devices dominate at higher frequencies.

## 2.3 SONAR Classification

| Type | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Active SONAR** | Transmits pulse + listens for echo | Obstacle avoidance, depth sounding |
| **Passive SONAR** | Only listens for ambient acoustic signatures | Submarine detection, marine biology |
| **Mono-static** | Single transducer for TX and RX | Compact sensors (e.g., HC-SR04) |
| **Bi-static** | Separate TX and RX transducers | Long-range military SONAR |

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Ultrasonic Propagation

- **Attenuation ($\alpha$):** Loss of intensity due to absorption, scattering, and geometric spreading. In air, attenuation rises sharply with frequency — this is why 40 kHz is the sweet spot for air-coupled sensors (range up to ~10 m).
- **Beam Spreading:** As wavefront expands, intensity drops as $1/r^2$ in the far field.
- **Temperature & Humidity:** Speed of sound in air varies as $c = 331.4 + 0.6T$ (m/s, where $T$ is in °C).
- **Reflection Coefficient (R):** At an interface, $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$, where $Z = \rho c$ is acoustic impedance.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = f\lambda$ | Wave equation | m/s |
| 2 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Distance from time-of-flight (echo-ranging) | m |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength | m |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4c}$ | Near-field (Fresnel) distance | m |
| 5 | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{1.22\lambda}{D}$ | Half-angle of main beam lobe | radians |
| 6 | $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | Attenuation (Beer-Lambert form) | W/m² |
| 7 | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Intensity reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Z = \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance | Rayl (kg/m²·s) |
| 9 | $\text{SNR} = \text{SL} - \text{NL} + \text{DI} - \text{TL}$ | Passive SONAR equation | dB |
| 10 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | Range resolution (pulse bandwidth $B$) | m |
| 11 | $v = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | Velocity from Doppler shift | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These equations are not academic exercises. They directly drive design choices in automotive parking sensors, drone altimeters, robotic obstacle-avoidance modules, medical imaging probes, and naval surveillance arrays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Distance Measurement from Time-of-Flight

**Statement:** For a mono-static ultrasonic sensor, the distance $d$ to a reflecting target is half the product of the speed of sound and the round-trip echo time.

**Step 1 — Define the round-trip time.**
Let the transducer emit a pulse at $t = 0$ and receive the echo at $t = t_e$ (the "echo time"). The pulse travels a total path length $L = c \cdot t_e$.

**Step 2 — Recognize that the path is round-trip.**
The pulse goes from transducer → target → transducer, covering the distance twice.

$$L = 2d$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $L$.**

$$2d = c \cdot t_e$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the target distance $d$.**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t_e}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Insert the temperature-corrected speed of sound** $c = 331.4 + 0.6T$ (with $T$ in °C, result in m/s):

$$d = \frac{(331.4 + 0.6T) \cdot t_e}{2}$$

> **Interpretation:** The factor of $1/2$ is non-negotiable in KTU derivations — examiners specifically look for this. Students commonly lose marks by forgetting it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Active SONAR Range Equation (Simplified)

The **active SONAR equation** expresses the received signal level (RL) in decibels in terms of transmitted level, target strength, propagation loss, and noise.

**Step 1 — Start with one-way propagation loss** (spherical spreading in a homogeneous medium):

$$\text{TL}_{\text{1-way}} = 20 \log_{10}(r) + \alpha r$$

where $r$ is the one-way range in meters and $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient in dB/m.

**Step 2 — For a mono-static system the pulse travels twice** (out and back), so total two-way transmission loss is:

$$\text{TL}_{\text{2-way}} = 2 \cdot \text{TL}_{\text{1-way}} = 40 \log_{10}(r) + 2\alpha r$$

**Step 3 — Define the source level SL** as the transmitted intensity referenced to $1\,\mu\text{Pa}$ at 1 m:

$$\text{SL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{\text{tx}}}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) \quad \text{(dB re } 1\,\mu\text{Pa at 1 m)}$$

**Step 4 — Define target strength TS** as the backscattered echo level referenced at 1 m from the target.

**Step 5 — Apply the SONAR equation:**

$$\text{RL} = \text{SL} + \text{TS} - \text{TL}_{\text{2-way}}$$

**Step 6 — Detection requires RL ≥ NL + DT**, where NL is noise level and DT is the detection threshold (a function of probability of detection $P_d$ and false alarm $P_{fa}$):

$$\text{SL} + \text{TS} - 40 \log_{10}(r) - 2\alpha r \geq \text{NL} + \text{DT}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for maximum detection range $r_{\max}$:**

$$r_{\max} = 10^{\,(\text{SL} + \text{TS} - \text{NL} - \text{DT}) / 40}$$

(assuming absorption is negligible compared to geometric spreading).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Near-Field (Fresnel) Distance

**Step 1 — The acoustic wave emerges from a circular piston of diameter $D$.** Within the near field, the wavefront oscillates between constructive and destructive interference due to differing path lengths from individual surface elements.

**Step 2 — The boundary is defined where the edge-to-center path difference equals $\lambda/2$:**

$$\sqrt{N^2 + (D/2)^2} - N = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the binomial approximation** $(1+x)^{1/2} \approx 1 + x/2$ for small $D/N$:

$$\frac{D^2}{8N} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the near-field distance $N$:**

$$N = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \frac{D^2 f}{4c}$$

> **Engineering use:** Designers use $N$ to decide where a target can be reliably placed. Targets placed within the near field produce highly variable readings.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Example — Worked Numerical Problem

**Problem:** An HC-SR04 sensor operates at $f = 40\,\text{kHz}$ in air at $T = 25\,°\text{C}$. An obstacle is detected with an echo time $t_e = 5.8\,\text{ms}$. Find (a) the wavelength, (b) the distance to the obstacle, and (c) the near-field distance for a transducer of diameter $D = 16\,\text{mm}$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Wavelength**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 25}{40 \times 10^3}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{346.4}{40000} = 8.66 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 8.66\,\text{mm}$$

**(b) Distance**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t_e}{2} = \frac{346.4 \cdot 5.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{2.009}{2} = 1.005\,\text{m}$$

**(c) Near-field distance**

$$N = \frac{D^2 f}{4c} = \frac{(0.016)^2 \cdot 40000}{4 \cdot 346.4}$$

$$N = \frac{0.000256 \cdot 40000}{1385.6} = \frac{10.24}{1385.6} \approx 7.4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 7.4\,\text{mm}$$

> **Interpretation:** The obstacle at 1 m lies *far* in the acoustic far field ($d \gg N$), so beam-spreading equations apply. At 7.4 mm, however, near-field effects dominate — this is why minimum-range "dead zones" exist in ultrasonic sensors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mono-static Ultrasonic Ranging System

The following diagram maps the **signal and data flow** of a typical mono-static ultrasonic ranging module (HC-SR04 / industrial equivalent) used in KTU robotics and automation projects.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU["Microcontroller Unit"] -->|TRIG pulse 10us| DRV["Driver Stage 40kHz"]
    DRV -->|High voltage AC| TX["Piezo Transmitter"]
    TX -->|Ultrasonic beam| MED["Propagation Medium Air or Water"]
    MED -->|Reflection from target| RX["Piezo Receiver"]
    RX -->|微弱 mV signal| AMP["Amplifier Stage 40dB"]
    AMP -->|Conditioned signal| BPF["Bandpass Filter 40kHz Q5"]
    BPF -->|Noise-free echo| COMP["Comparator Threshold"]
    COMP -->|Digital echo pulse| TMR["Echo Timer Capture"]
    TMR -->|Time of flight t e| CALC["Distance Calculator d = c t / 2"]
    CALC -->|Distance value cm| MCU
    MCU -->|Display or Motor control| OUT["Actuator or LCD Output"]
```

> **Reading guide for KTU students:** Follow the signal from MCU (left) through the analog signal chain (middle) back to the MCU (right). Note the *symmetry* of TX and RX — in mono-static designs both share the same piezo element via a TX/RX switch.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Active SONAR System (Underwater / Long-Range)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Transmit Chain
        A1["Waveform Generator LFM or CW Pulse"] --> A2["Power Amplifier kW class"]
        A2 --> A3["TR Switch"]
        A3 --> A4["Projector Transducer"]
    end
    A4 -->|Acoustic wave| B1["Underwater Channel"]
    B1 -->|Echo from target| B2["Hydrophone Array"]
    subgraph Receive Chain
        B2 --> A3
        A3 --> R1["Preamplifier"]
        R1 --> R2["Matched Filter"]
        R2 --> R3["Beamformer"]
        R3 --> R4["Detector CFAR"]
        R4 --> R5["Display Tracker"]
    end
```

> **Engineering note:** The **TR (Transmit/Receive) Switch** is critical — it protects the sensitive hydrophone from the kW-class transmit pulse. In mono-static air sensors this is implemented as a simple diode bridge or analog multiplexer.

---

## 4.3 Operating-Frequency Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start Application selection"] --> Q1{"Range needed"}
    Q1 -->|"Less than 1 m"| P1["Use 200 to 400 kHz short range precision"]
    Q1 -->|"1 to 10 m"| P2["Use 40 to 80 kHz air coupled standard"]
    Q1 -->|"Greater than 10 m underwater"| P3["Use 10 to 50 kHz SONAR"]
    Q1 -->|"Greater than 1 km"| P4["Use 1 to 10 kHz deep ocean SONAR"]
    P1 --> END["Select piezoelectric type"]
    P2 --> END
    P3 --> M1{"Power required"}
    M1 -->|"High power"| P5["Use magnetostrictive"]
    M1 -->|"Moderate"| END
    P4 --> P5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the piezoelectric effect and its role in ultrasonic transducers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The piezoelectric effect is the ability of certain crystals (Quartz, PZT, Lithium Niobate) to convert mechanical stress into electrical charge (direct effect) and conversely, electrical voltage into mechanical strain (converse effect) **[1 mark]**.
2. In ultrasonic transducers, an alternating voltage applied at the crystal's mechanical resonance frequency (typically 20 kHz–10 MHz) causes it to vibrate, generating an acoustic pressure wave **[1 mark]**.
3. Reverse operation allows the same crystal to act as a receiver, producing voltage when struck by returning echoes — giving a single piezo element dual TX/RX capability **[1 mark]**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between active and passive SONAR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Active SONAR | Passive SONAR |
|---|---|---|
| Transmission | Emits acoustic pulse | No transmission, only listens |
| Information | Range, bearing, velocity | Bearing and acoustic signature only |
| Power | Requires high TX power | Low power, stealthy |
| Example | Fish-finder, obstacle sensor | Submarine surveillance |

**[1 mark per correct pair, total 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the distance of an obstacle using the time-of-flight method in an ultrasonic ranging system. State any assumptions made. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An ultrasonic sensor operates at 50 kHz in air at 30 °C. A pulse is emitted and the echo returns in 6 ms. Calculate (i) the wavelength of the transmitted wave, (ii) the distance to the target, and (iii) the maximum unambiguous range if the receiver recovery time is 1 ms. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation [7 marks]**

1. State assumption: speed of sound $c$ is constant in a homogeneous medium between TX and target. **[1 mark]**
2. Define $t_e$ as the round-trip echo time. **[1 mark]**
3. Path length $L = c \cdot t_e$ **[1 mark]**
4. Recognize round trip: $L = 2d$ **[1 mark]**
5. Equate: $2d = c \cdot t_e$ **[1 mark]**
6. Solve: $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t_e}{2}$ **[1 mark]**
7. Temperature correction: substitute $c = 331.4 + 0.6T$ **[1 mark]**

**(b) Numerical [7 marks]**

(i) Wavelength

$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 30 = 349.4\,\text{m/s}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{349.4}{50 \times 10^3} = 6.988 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \approx 6.99\,\text{mm}$$

**[2 marks]**

(ii) Distance

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t_e}{2} = \frac{349.4 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.0964}{2} = 1.048\,\text{m}$$

**[2 marks]**

(iii) Maximum unambiguous range

The sensor needs $t_e + 1\,\text{ms}$ to be ready before the next pulse, so maximum unambiguous echo time = $6\,\text{ms}$ (we use echo time directly for this scenario):

$$r_{\max} = \frac{c \cdot t_{\max}}{2} = \frac{349.4 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.048\,\text{m}$$

**[3 marks]**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a mono-static ultrasonic ranging system. List the role of each block. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the SONAR equation for an active detection system. A SONAR system has source level SL = 220 dB, target strength TS = 15 dB, noise level NL = 70 dB, and detection threshold DT = 10 dB. Assuming spherical spreading and negligible absorption, compute the maximum detection range. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation [7 marks]**

Draw the block diagram (as in Section 4.1) covering: Microcontroller → Driver → Piezo Transmitter → Medium → Target → Piezo Receiver → Amplifier → Bandpass Filter → Comparator → Timer → Calculator. **[3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for block-wise role description]**

| Block | Role |
|---|---|
| Microcontroller | Initiates TRIG pulse, computes distance |
| Driver | Boosts 5 V logic to 20 Vpp AC at 40 kHz |
| Piezo TX | Converts electrical → acoustic |
| Piezo RX | Converts returning acoustic → voltage |
| Amplifier | Boosts µV-level echo to mV level |
| Bandpass Filter | Rejects ambient noise outside 40 kHz |
| Comparator | Produces clean digital echo edge |
| Timer | Captures echo time $t_e$ |

**(b) SONAR Equation & Numerical [7 marks]**

1. SONAR equation: $\text{RL} = \text{SL} + \text{TS} - \text{TL}_{2\text{-way}}$ **[1 mark]**
2. Two-way transmission loss (spherical): $\text{TL} = 40\log_{10}(r)$ **[1 mark]**
3. Detection condition: $\text{SL} + \text{TS} - 40\log_{10}(r) \geq \text{NL} + \text{DT}$ **[1 mark]**
4. Plug values: $220 + 15 - 40\log_{10}(r) = 70 + 10 = 80$ **[1 mark]**
5. $40\log_{10}(r) = 220 + 15 - 80 = 155$ **[1 mark]**
6. $\log_{10}(r) = \dfrac{155}{40} = 3.875$ **[1 mark]**
7. $r = 10^{3.875} \approx 7500\,\text{m} = 7.5\,\text{km}$ **[1 mark]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing**
> - **Forgetting the factor of 1/2:** The single most common mistake is writing $d = c \cdot t$ instead of $d = c \cdot t/2$. Examiners deduct 1 mark instantly.
> - **Not temperature-correcting $c$:** Writing $c = 330$ or $c = 340$ without specifying temperature loses a step mark in numericals.
> - **Confusing acoustic with electromagnetic waves:** SONAR uses *sound*, RADAR/LIDAR use EM. Don't mix terminology.
> - **Skipping units in numericals:** Every numerical answer must end with the correct unit (m, mm, kHz, dB).
> - **Block diagram without labels:** Drawing a box without naming it earns partial credit only. Use labeled rectangles, not vague "black boxes".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ultrasonic frequency band:** 20 kHz to 10 MHz; most sensors operate at **40 kHz** (air) or **10–200 kHz** (underwater).
- **Speed of sound (air, 20 °C):** $c = 343\,\text{m/s}$; **water:** $c = 1480\,\text{m/s}$.
- **Core equation:** $d = c \cdot t_e / 2$ — always remember the half.
- **Temperature correction:** $c = 331.4 + 0.6T$ (m/s, $T$ in °C).
- **Piezoelectric transducers** dominate the market (PZT preferred) due to high coupling coefficient and bidirectional TX/RX capability.
- **Magnetostrictive transducers** are used for high-power, low-frequency underwater SONAR.
- **Near-field distance** $N = D^2 f/(4c)$ — minimum reliable ranging distance.
- **Beam spreading:** Beyond $N$, intensity falls as $1/r^2$ — explains why range drops sharply with distance.
- **Reflection coefficient:** $R = ((Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1))^2$ — large impedance mismatch (e.g., air-to-steel) gives strong echo.
- **Active SONAR equation:** $\text{SL} + \text{TS} - 40\log_{10}(r) \geq \text{NL} + \text{DT}$.
- **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = c/(2B)$ — set by pulse bandwidth $B$.
- **Applications:** Automotive parking sensors, robotic obstacle avoidance, drone altimeters, medical ultrasonography, underwater mapping, naval surveillance.
- **Acoustic impedance mismatch** between air and water is why the same transducer cannot work efficiently in both media without redesign.
- **Minimum dead-zone** exists near the sensor due to ring-down of the piezo element — important in robotics for short-range mapping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RADAR and Doppler Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RADAR and Doppler Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 RADAR — Formal Definition

**RADAR (Radio Detection and Ranging)** is an electromagnetic-based sensing and ranging system that uses the principle of **reflection (echo)** of high-frequency radio waves to detect, locate (range, angular position), and determine the velocity of distant objects such as aircraft, ships, missiles, vehicles, weather formations, and terrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A radar system transmits a known waveform (pulsed or continuous wave) toward a target and analyzes the reflected signal to extract four primary target parameters: **Range (R), Velocity (v), Angular Position (θ, φ), and Radar Cross-Section (σ)**.

## 1.2 The Doppler Effect — Formal Definition

The **Doppler Effect** in electromagnetic systems is the apparent shift in the received signal frequency relative to the transmitted frequency, caused by the **relative radial motion** between the radar and the target. If the target moves **toward** the radar, frequency increases; if moving **away**, frequency decreases.

$$f_d = f_r - f_t = \frac{2 v_r f_t}{c}$$

where $f_d$ is the Doppler frequency shift, $v_r$ is the radial velocity of the target, $f_t$ is the transmitted frequency, and $c$ is the speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The "Whistle" Effect):**
> - Imagine standing on a footpath and a train is approaching you while blowing a whistle. The whistle sounds **higher pitched** (shorter wavelength) as the train comes near. As it passes and moves away, the pitch **drops** (longer wavelength). This is the Doppler Effect.
> - Now replace the train whistle with a **flashlight** and the sound waves with a **beam of radio waves**. The radar does exactly this: it shines invisible radio "light" at an object, listens for the echo, and measures the time-delay and frequency-shift to figure out **how far** and **how fast** the object is moving.

## 1.4 Why RADAR Uses Radio Waves

| Property | Why it matters for RADAR |
|---|---|
| **Speed c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Known, constant, used as a precise "ruler" via time-delay |
| **Low atmospheric attenuation** | Microwave band (1–100 GHz) penetrates rain, fog, clouds |
| **Reflectivity from metals** | Aircraft, ships, vehicles act as good reflectors |
| **All-weather, day-night** | Unlike optical/IR systems, RADAR works in darkness and storms |
| **Small wavelength** | Allows narrow beams → good angular resolution |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** RADAR works in the **microwave** region (300 MHz to 300 GHz) because microwaves are reflected well by metallic targets and suffer less atmospheric scattering than visible light.

## 1.5 Classification of RADAR Systems (Overview)

1. **CW (Continuous Wave) RADAR** — transmits continuously, measures Doppler shift only.
2. **Pulsed RADAR** — transmits short pulses, measures both range and Doppler.
3. **MTI (Moving Target Indication) RADAR** — filters out stationary clutter using Doppler.
4. **Pulse Doppler RADAR** — combines pulse ranging with Doppler processing.
5. **Tracking RADAR** — continuously follows a single target.
6. **SAR / ISAR** — Synthetic Aperture / Inverse SAR for high-resolution imaging.
7. **Phased Array RADAR** — electronic beam steering with no mechanical motion.
8. **Secondary Surveillance RADAR (SSR)** — interrogator-transponder cooperation (used in ATC).
9. **Over-the-Horizon RADAR (OTHR)** — uses ionospheric reflection for long ranges.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of RADAR — Step-by-Step Logic

The standard **Pulse RADAR** operates on five sequential stages:

1. **Waveform Generation (Transmitter):** A stable, high-power, coherent RF signal is generated (typically a magnetron or a solid-state oscillator) and modulated into **short pulses** of width $\tau$ (pulse duration) at a Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) of $f_p$.
2. **Antenna Radiation & Beam Shaping:** The RF pulses are radiated via a highly directional **parabolic dish** or **phased-array antenna** with a narrow beamwidth $\theta_B = k \lambda / D$, where $D$ is the antenna diameter.
3. **Propagation to Target:** The wave propagates at speed $c$. The one-way travel time to a target at range $R$ is $T_{ow} = R / c$.
4. **Reflection from Target:** The target intercepts a portion of the incident power and re-radiates it. The amount re-radiated is quantified by the **Radar Cross-Section (RCS, σ)** in square meters.
5. **Echo Reception & Processing:** The weak reflected echo is captured, amplified by a low-noise receiver, and processed to extract:
   - **Time delay** $\Delta t = 2R/c$ → range $R$
   - **Frequency shift** $f_d = 2v_r f_0/c$ → radial velocity
   - **Angle of arrival** (via antenna pointing or phased-array) → angular position

## 2.2 The RADAR Range Equation — The Heart of the System

The **RADAR Range Equation** links the **received echo power $P_r$** to the radar parameters and target range. It is derived from the **Friis transmission equation** applied twice (one-way for Tx-to-target, one-way for target-to-Rx), with the RCS appearing as a "reradiation gain" of the target.

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4 \, L}$$

where the symbols are tabulated below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $R^4$ dependence is the most critical KTU concept:** Doubling the target range reduces the received echo power by a factor of **16 (12 dB)**. This extreme fall-off is why radar transmitter powers must be enormous (MW-level) for long-range detection.

## 2.3 Maximum Detectable Range Equation

Setting $P_r = S_{min}$ (the Minimum Detectable Signal), and solving for the maximum range $R_{max}$:

$$R_{max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F \, (S/N)_{min} \, L} \right]^{1/4}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \, \Delta t}{2}$ | Range from round-trip delay $\Delta t$ | m, s |
| 2 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Doppler shift (CW / Pulse-Doppler RADAR) | Hz, m/s |
| 3 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 L}$ | RADAR range equation (echo power) | W |
| 4 | $R_{max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{min} L}\right]^{1/4}$ | Max detection range | m |
| 5 | $R_{max} \propto P_t^{1/4}$ | Power gain requires 16× power to double range | — |
| 6 | $R_{unamb} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_p}$ | Unambiguous range (Pulse RADAR) | m |
| 7 | $v_{unamb} = \dfrac{\lambda f_p}{2}$ | Unambiguous velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $\theta_B = \dfrac{k \lambda}{D}$ | Antenna 3-dB beamwidth | rad |
| 9 | $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{4\pi \eta A}{\lambda^2}$ | Antenna gain (aperture efficiency $\eta$) | numeric |
| 10 | $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | Effective aperture | m² |
| 11 | $S/N = \dfrac{P_r}{k T_0 B_n F}$ | Receiver SNR after LNA | numeric / dB |
| 12 | $\text{PAR} = \dfrac{\tau}{T_p} = \tau \, f_p$ | Pulse aspect ratio / duty cycle | numeric |
| 13 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \tau}{2}$ | Range resolution (pulse width $\tau$) | m |
| 14 | $v_{rel} = \dfrac{f_d \lambda}{2}$ | Radial velocity from Doppler | m/s |
| 15 | $B_n = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | Matched-filter bandwidth | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Radar Constants** — memorize these:
> - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of light)
> - $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant)
> - $T_0 = 290$ K (standard reference temperature)

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where RADAR is Used

- **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** — primary radar + SSR for aircraft separation.
- **Military Surveillance & Fire Control** — tracking incoming missiles, target acquisition.
- **Weather RADAR (Doppler Weather)** — detecting rain intensity, wind shear, tornadoes.
- **Maritime Navigation** — surface search, collision avoidance.
- **Automotive RADAR** — Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), AEB, blind-spot detection (77 GHz).
- **Police Speed Guns** — handheld CW Doppler radar at 24/34 GHz.
- **Space Debris Tracking** — very-long-range phased arrays (e.g., GRAVES).
- **Synthetic Aperture RADAR (SAR)** — used in Earth-observation satellites (e.g., NISAR).
- **Medical Doppler** — fetal heart-rate monitoring, blood-flow imaging.

## 2.6 Why $R^4$ ? — Physical Insight

The $R^4$ law emerges naturally from three sequential spreading effects:

1. **Tx-to-Target Spreading:** Power density at target $\propto P_t G / (4\pi R^2)$.
2. **Target Re-radiation:** Power scattered by target $\propto P_t G \sigma / (4\pi R^2)$.
3. **Target-to-Rx Spreading:** The reradiated spherical wave further spreads by $1/(4\pi R^2)$.

Multiplying: $P_r \propto \sigma / R^4$ → confirmed.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Why is the radar equation $R^4$ but communication is $R^2$?"  
> **Answer:** Communication is one-way (Tx → Rx), so only $1/(4\pi R^2)$ appears. RADAR is **two-way** (Tx → target → Rx), so the $1/(4\pi R^2)$ factor appears **twice**, giving $1/R^4$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Range from Time Delay

**Statement:** A pulse-echo radar measures a round-trip time delay $\Delta t$ between transmission and reception. Show that the target range is $R = c \Delta t / 2$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.** The electromagnetic wave travels from the radar to the target at range $R$ and back, so the total path length is $2R$.

**Step 2 — Use the speed-distance-time relation.** Since the wave propagates at speed $c$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= \frac{\text{Total path length}}{\text{Speed}} = \frac{2R}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R$.** Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **1/2** arises because $\Delta t$ is the *round-trip* delay; the one-way distance is half the total.

**Example (numerical):** If $\Delta t = 200\ \mu s$, then $R = (3 \times 10^8)(200 \times 10^{-6})/2 = 30{,}000$ m = 30 km.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RADAR Range Equation (Two-Way Power)

**Statement:** A monostatic radar (single antenna for Tx and Rx) transmits peak power $P_t$ with antenna gain $G$ at wavelength $\lambda$. The target has RCS $\sigma$ at range $R$. Find the received echo power $P_r$.

**Step 1 — Power density at the target.** The isotropic power density at distance $R$ from a transmit antenna of gain $G$ and power $P_t$ is

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_t &= \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Power intercepted and re-radiated by the target.** The RCS $\sigma$ (m²) acts as an "effective capture area" of the target. The total power re-radiated isotropically by the target is

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{scat}} &= S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Power density of the echo back at the radar.** This isotropically scattered spherical wave arrives at the radar aperture with density

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_r &= \frac{P_{\text{scat}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power captured by the receiving antenna.** The effective aperture of the Rx antenna is $A_e = G \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$. Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final RADAR range equation.** Including system losses $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4 \, L}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Detection Range

**Statement:** Derive $R_{max}$ in terms of radar, target, and receiver parameters, given the minimum detectable SNR.

**Step 1 — Set the received power equal to the Minimum Detectable Signal (MDS).** The receiver noise floor is $P_n = k T_0 B_n F$, where $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T_0 = 290$ K, $B_n$ is the noise bandwidth, and $F$ is the receiver noise figure. The MDS is

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{min} = (S/N)_{min} \cdot k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate $P_r$ to $S_{min}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R_{max}^4 \, L} &= (S/N)_{min} \, k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_{max}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max}^4 &= \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F \, (S/N)_{min} \, L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the 4th root.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F \, (S/N)_{min} \, L} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Doppler Frequency Shift

**Statement:** Show that the Doppler shift seen by a CW radar is $f_d = 2 v_r f_0 / c$ for a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ relative to a stationary radar.

**Step 1 — Frequency at the target.** The transmitted wave has frequency $f_0$. To the moving target, the apparent frequency is increased (for closing target) by the classical Doppler factor $v_r / c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f' &= f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency at the radar after reflection.** The target now acts as a moving source, re-radiating at $f'$. The radar (stationary) receives it shifted again by the same factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r &= f' \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right) = f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — For non-relativistic targets ($v_r \ll c$),** apply the binomial approximation $(1 + x)^2 \approx 1 + 2x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r &\approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right) = f_0 + \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Doppler shift definition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **2** appears because the Doppler shift is incurred **twice**: once on Tx-to-target, and once on target-to-Rx.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Unambiguous Range and Unambiguous Velocity

**Statement:** For a pulsed radar with PRF $f_p$ and wavelength $\lambda$, derive the maximum unambiguous range and maximum unambiguous velocity.

**Step 1 — Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI).** $T_p = 1 / f_p$.

**Step 2 — Maximum unambiguous range.** A returned echo from range $R$ arrives after time $2R/c$. To avoid aliasing, this must be less than $T_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2 R_{unamb}}{c} &< T_p = \frac{1}{f_p} \\
\Rightarrow \quad R_{unamb} &= \frac{c}{2 f_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum unambiguous velocity.** The Nyquist sampling rate for the Doppler shift is $f_p$. The maximum unambiguously detectable Doppler shift is $f_d = f_p / 2$. Using $v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{unamb} &= \frac{\lambda \, f_p}{4} \quad \text{(from $f_d = f_p/2$ with $v = f_d \lambda / 2$)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let us re-derive carefully. The Doppler frequency is sampled at PRF, so Nyquist says $f_d \le f_p / 2$. The radial velocity is $v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$, giving

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{unamb} &= \frac{\lambda \, f_p}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Trade-off:** Increasing PRF improves unambiguous **velocity** but reduces unambiguous **range**; decreasing PRF does the reverse. This is the famous **Range–Doppler ambiguity** in pulse-Doppler radar, resolved using staggered PRFs or multiple PRF techniques.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing CW vs Pulsed RADAR

| Parameter | CW RADAR | Pulsed RADAR |
|---|---|---|
| Waveform | Continuous sinusoid | Short pulses (width $\tau$) at PRF $f_p$ |
| Measures Doppler? | **Yes** (primary function) | **Yes** (in Pulse-Doppler mode) |
| Measures Range? | **No** (no time marker) | **Yes** (from $2R/c$) |
| Power efficiency | Low (must transmit continuously) | **High** (only transmits during pulse) |
| Range ambiguity | Not applicable | Yes, if $R > c/(2f_p)$ |
| Velocity ambiguity | Yes, if $v > \lambda f_p / 4$ | Yes, if PRF is too low for Doppler |
| Isolation Tx–Rx | Difficult (need separate antennas or circulator) | Easy (Tx off while Rx is on) |
| Typical use | Police speed gun, altimeter, proximity fuse | Surveillance, tracking, weather, ATC |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A pulsed radar operates at $f_0 = 10$ GHz with PRF $f_p = 1$ kHz, pulse width $\tau = 1\ \mu$s, peak power $P_t = 1$ MW, antenna gain $G = 40$ dB, and detects a target with RCS $\sigma = 1$ m². Find: (a) Unambiguous range, (b) Range resolution, (c) Doppler shift for $v_r = 300$ m/s, (d) Duty cycle.

**Solution (a) — Unambiguous range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{unamb} &= \frac{c}{2 f_p} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^3} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 150 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b) — Range resolution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R &= \frac{c \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 150 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (c) — Doppler shift:** First find $\lambda = c / f_0 = (3 \times 10^8) / (10 \times 10^9) = 0.03$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 300}{0.03} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (d) — Duty cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Duty} &= \tau \cdot f_p = (1 \times 10^{-6})(1 \times 10^3) = 1 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Pulsed RADAR System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Waveform Generator - Pulse Modulator] -->|Trigger pulse| nodeB[Stable Local Oscillator - f0]
    nodeB -->|CW RF| nodeD[Duplexer - T R Switch]
    nodeA -->|Modulating pulse| nodeD
    nodeD -->|High power Tx pulse| nodeC[High Power Amplifier - Magnetron or SSPA]
    nodeC -->|Radiated RF| nodeE[Antenna - Parabolic Dish or Phased Array]
    nodeE -->|Echo from target| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Weak echo| nodeF[Low Noise Amplifier - LNA]
    nodeF -->|Amplified echo| nodeG[Mixer - Coherent Detector]
    nodeB -->|LO reference| nodeG
    nodeG -->|I Q baseband| nodeH[Matched Filter - Pulse Compression]
    nodeH -->|Range bins| nodeI[Signal Processor - Range Doppler Map]
    nodeI -->|Detection threshold| nodeJ[Display - PPI Scope]
    nodeI -->|Tracking outputs| nodeK[Tracker - Kalman Filter]
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow Explanation:**

- The **Waveform Generator** produces a low-power trigger pulse that defines the pulse width $\tau$ and PRF $f_p$.
- The **Local Oscillator** generates a stable carrier at $f_0$ (X-band, e.g., 10 GHz).
- The trigger pulse turns on the **High-Power Amplifier** for $\tau$ seconds, producing a peak RF pulse (MW-level).
- The **Duplexer** switches the antenna from Tx to Rx. It protects the sensitive LNA during the high-power Tx phase using a PIN-diode limiter or a ferrite circulator.
- The **Antenna** radiates a narrow beam; for SAR or phased arrays, beam steering is electronic.
- The returning echo is amplified by the **LNA** (sets the noise figure $F$).
- The **Mixer** down-converts the RF echo to baseband using the same LO — preserving phase coherence so Doppler is measurable.
- The **Matched Filter** maximizes SNR and provides pulse compression (if chirp is used), improving range resolution to $\Delta R = c/(2B)$ instead of $c\tau/2$.
- The **Signal Processor** builds a Range–Doppler map via FFT across the PRI-stacked pulse train.
- The **Display** is a **Plan Position Indicator (PPI)** — a circular scope where range is radial distance and bearing is the angle.

---

## 4.2 CW Doppler RADAR Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[CW Oscillator - f0] -->|Transmit| nodeB[Antenna or Circulator]
    nodeB -->|Radiated CW| nodeC[Moving Target]
    nodeC -->|Doppler-shifted echo f0 + fd| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Echo| nodeD[Mixer with LO = f0]
    nodeD -->|Baseband fd| nodeE[Bandpass Filter around fd]
    nodeE -->|Velocity output| nodeF[Frequency Counter or FFT]
    nodeF -->|v_r| nodeG[Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a pure CW Doppler radar, the **range cannot be measured** because the transmit waveform has no time markers. Only the **velocity** is extracted by measuring $f_d$.

---

## 4.3 MTI (Moving Target Indication) RADAR Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Pulse Train Echoes from one Range Bin] --> nodeB[Coherent Oscillator Reference]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Phase Detector vs PRI]
    nodeC -->|PRI k vs PRI k+1| nodeD[Subtraction Canceller]
    nodeB -->|Reference phase| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Stationary clutter cancelled| nodeE[Moving Target Echo only]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Threshold Detector]
    nodeF -->|MTI output| nodeG[Display]
```

**Why this works:** Stationary clutter returns echoes of *constant phase* from one PRI to the next. Subtracting PRI $k+1$ from PRI $k$ yields **zero** for clutter. A moving target changes phase by $2\pi f_d / f_p$ per PRI, so the subtraction **preserves** it. The improvement factor $I$ is typically 20–50 dB.

---

## 4.4 Phased Array RADAR Beam Steering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Beam Steering Computer] -->|Phase shift commands| nodeB[Phase Shifter per Element]
    nodeB -->|Element 1 - phi1| nodeC[N-element Antenna Array]
    nodeB -->|Element 2 - phi2| nodeC
    nodeB -->|Element N - phiN| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Narrow beam pointed at angle theta| nodeD[Target in Space]
    nodeD -->|Echo| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Received N signals| nodeE[Beamforming Network - Combine]
    nodeE -->|Processed echo| nodeF[Receiver and Display]
```

The progressive phase shift $\Delta\phi = (2\pi d \sin\theta)/\lambda$ across elements causes constructive interference in the desired direction $\theta$, allowing **electronic beam steering** in microseconds — far faster than mechanical rotation.

---

## 4.5 RADAR Operating Frequency Bands (KTU Reference)

| Band | Frequency | Wavelength | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| HF | 3–30 MHz | 10–100 m | Over-the-horizon radar (OTHR) |
| VHF / UHF | 30 MHz–1 GHz | 0.3–10 m | Long-range early warning |
| L-band | 1–2 GHz | 15–30 cm | Air traffic control, long-range surveillance |
| S-band | 2–4 GHz | 7.5–15 cm | Airport surveillance, weather |
| C-band | 4–8 GHz | 3.75–7.5 cm | Fire-control, missile guidance |
| **X-band** | **8–12 GHz** | **2.5–3.75 cm** | **Police radar, military fire-control, marine** |
| Ku-band | 12–18 GHz | 1.67–2.5 cm | Satellite altimetry, automotive |
| K-band | 18–26 GHz | 1.1–1.67 cm | Police speed radar |
| **mm-wave (77 GHz)** | 76–81 GHz | ~4 mm | **Automotive ACC / AEB, imaging** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Why does automotive radar use 77 GHz?"  
> **Answer:** 77 GHz allows very small antennas (wavelength ~4 mm) and large available bandwidth (~5 GHz), giving high range resolution (cm-level) for distinguishing pedestrians from vehicles.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define RADAR. What is the principle used in radar for target detection?

**Model Answer:**
RADAR stands for **Radio Detection and Ranging**. It is an electromagnetic system that detects the presence, range, velocity, and angular position of distant objects using **reflected radio-frequency energy**. The principle is the **echo-location / pulse-reflection method**: the radar transmits a known waveform (pulsed or CW) toward the target; the target reflects (back-scatters) a portion of the incident wave; the radar receives this echo and from the **time delay** computes the range ($R = c\Delta t / 2$) and from the **Doppler frequency shift** computes the radial velocity ($v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$). Unlike passive sensors, radar provides its own illumination, making it an **active** sensor with all-weather, day-night capability. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024] State and explain the Doppler effect as applied to radar.

**Model Answer:**
The **Doppler Effect** is the apparent change in frequency of a received signal due to the relative motion between the transmitter and the target. In a monostatic radar, since the wave experiences a frequency shift both on the way to the target and on the way back, the total shift is

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

where $v_r$ is the radial component of target velocity (positive toward radar, negative away), $f_0$ is the transmitted frequency, and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. A **closing** target produces a **positive** Doppler shift (frequency increases), and a **receding** target produces a **negative** shift. By measuring $f_d$, the radar can determine the radial velocity of the target even when the target is at the same range as a stationary clutter source — this is the basis of **MTI (Moving Target Indication)**. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2** in $R = c\Delta t / 2$ (round-trip!) — loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 2** in the Doppler equation $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$ — very common.
> 3. **Mixing up** $P_r$ (received power) and $P_t$ (transmitted power) in the range equation.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency**: $\lambda$ must be in metres, $R$ in metres; never mix km with cm.
> 5. **Confusing $R^2$ (one-way) with $R^4$ (radar two-way)** — must be stated explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — RADAR Range Equation Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the RADAR range equation, explaining each term. Show that the maximum range of a radar is proportional to the **fourth root** of the transmitted power. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The RADAR range equation is derived in five steps.

**Step 1 — Power density at the target.** A transmit antenna of gain $G$ and input power $P_t$ produces a power density at range $R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_t = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2} \quad \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Power scattered by the target.** The target intercepts this power density and re-radiates it according to its **radar cross-section** $\sigma$ (units: m²). The total scattered power isotropically radiated is

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{scat}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Power density of the echo at the radar.** The scattered wave further spreads as a sphere, so at the radar's location the echo power density is

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_r = \frac{P_{\text{scat}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power captured by the receive antenna.** The effective aperture is $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$. Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Including system losses $L$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 \, L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum range and $P_t^{1/4}$ dependence:** Setting $P_r = S_{min} = (S/N)_{min} \, k T_0 B_n F$ and solving for $R_{max}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{min} L} \right]^{1/4} \\
R_{max} &\propto P_t^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

To double the maximum range, the peak transmit power must be increased by $2^4 = \mathbf{16}$ times (12 dB). This is a major constraint in radar design and motivates the use of high-gain antennas and low-noise receivers to achieve long ranges. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b)** A pulsed radar operates at **9 GHz** with peak transmitted power **500 kW**, antenna gain **30 dB**, pulse width **2 µs**, and receiver noise figure **4 dB**. The minimum detectable SNR is **13 dB**, system losses are **3 dB**, and the target RCS is **1 m²**. The matched-filter bandwidth equals $1/\tau$. Find the maximum detection range $R_{max}$. Take $T_0 = 290$ K. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert dB quantities to numeric.**

- Antenna gain: $G_{dB} = 30 \Rightarrow G = 10^{30/10} = 1000$
- Noise figure: $F_{dB} = 4 \Rightarrow F = 10^{0.4} \approx 2.512$
- SNR: $(S/N)_{dB} = 13 \Rightarrow (S/N)_{min} = 10^{1.3} \approx 19.95$
- Losses: $L_{dB} = 3 \Rightarrow L = 10^{0.3} \approx 1.995$

**Step 2 — Wavelength and bandwidth.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{9 \times 10^9} = 0.0333 \text{ m} \\
B_n &= \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the range equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max}^4 = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{min} L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma &= (5 \times 10^5)(1000)^2 (0.0333)^2 (1) \\
&= 5 \times 10^5 \times 10^6 \times 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 5.556 \times 10^8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(4\pi)^3 &= 1984.4 \\
k T_0 &= 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 = 4.0 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J} \\
k T_0 B_n F (S/N)_{min} L &= (4 \times 10^{-21})(5 \times 10^5)(2.512)(19.95)(1.995) \\
&\approx 2.0 \times 10^{-13}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max}^4 &= \frac{5.556 \times 10^8}{1984.4 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-13}} = \frac{5.556 \times 10^8}{3.97 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{18} \\
R_{max} &= (1.4 \times 10^{18})^{1/4} \approx 1.93 \times 10^4 \text{ m} \approx 19.3 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answer:** $R_{max} \approx 19.3$ km. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Doppler Effect and CW RADAR Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the Doppler shift equation for a CW radar, $f_d = 2 v_r f_0 / c$, and explain why a CW radar cannot measure target range. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Apparent frequency at the moving target.** A stationary radar transmits at frequency $f_0$. To a target approaching with radial velocity $v_r$, the apparent received frequency is increased (classical non-relativistic Doppler):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f' = f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Re-radiation by the target.** The target re-radiates the wave as if it were a moving source at frequency $f'$. The stationary radar receives this re-radiated wave with an additional Doppler upshift:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r = f' \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right) = f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Non-relativistic approximation.** For $v_r \ll c$, expand using $(1+x)^2 \approx 1 + 2x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right) = f_0 + \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Doppler shift definition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor **2** accounts for the **two** Doppler shifts (Tx→target, target→Rx).

**Why CW RADAR cannot measure range:** A continuous wave has **no time marker** — the radar cannot determine when a particular wavefront was transmitted. Without a time reference, the round-trip delay $\Delta t = 2R/c$ is unobservable. The radar can only detect a frequency shift $f_d$ but cannot associate it with a particular range. To measure range, a **time marker** (a pulse, a chirp, or a frequency-modulated carrier — FMCW) is required. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b)** A police Doppler radar operates at **34 GHz** (K-band). A car is approaching the radar at **108 km/h**. Calculate: (i) the Doppler frequency shift, and (ii) the wavelength and time-delay for a target at 200 m. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
- $v_r = 108$ km/h = $108 \times (1000/3600) = 30$ m/s
- $f_0 = 34$ GHz = $34 \times 10^9$ Hz
- $R = 200$ m

**Step 2 — Wavelength.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{34 \times 10^9} = 8.82 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 8.82 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — (i) Doppler shift.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 30}{8.82 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 6803 \text{ Hz} \approx 6.8 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Alternatively, $f_d = 2 v_r f_0 / c = (2)(30)(34 \times 10^9) / (3 \times 10^8) = 6800$ Hz ✓

**Step 4 — (ii) Time delay for $R = 200$ m.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t = \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{2 \times 200}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.33\ \mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answer:** $f_d \approx 6.8$ kHz, $\lambda \approx 8.82$ mm, $\Delta t = 1.33$ µs. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Checklist — RADAR and Doppler Effect**

- **RADAR = Radio Detection and Ranging.** Active sensor using **reflected** RF energy.
- **Range equation:** $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 L}$ — note the **$R^4$** (two-way).
- **$R^4$ vs $R^2$:** Communication is one-way ($R^2$); radar is two-way ($R^4$).
- **$R_{max} \propto P_t^{1/4}$:** Doubling range requires **16×** power.
- **Doppler shift:** $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ — factor of **2** for two-way path.
- **Range from time-delay:** $R = c \Delta t / 2$ — factor of **2** for round trip.
- **Unambiguous range:** $R_{unamb} = c / (2 f_p)$ (pulse radar).
- **Unambiguous velocity:** $v_{unamb} = \lambda f_p / 4$ (Nyquist).
- **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = c \tau / 2$ — smaller pulse width gives finer resolution.
- **CW RADAR:** measures **velocity only** (no range — no time marker).
- **Pulsed RADAR:** measures **range** (and Doppler if coherent).
- **MTI RADAR:** uses pulse-to-pulse **subtraction** to cancel stationary clutter, passing only moving targets.
- **Antenna beamwidth:** $\theta_B \approx k \lambda / D$ (smaller $\lambda$ or larger $D$ → sharper beam).
- **Antenna gain:** $G = 4\pi \eta A / \lambda^2$ (smaller $\lambda$ → higher gain for same dish).
- **RCS $\sigma$:** depends on target shape, size, material, and aspect angle; typical fighter ≈ 1 m², large ship ≈ 10⁴ m², stealth aircraft ≈ 0.001 m².
- **Automotive radar:** 77 GHz mm-wave (small antenna, high resolution).
- **Weather Doppler:** X-band/S-band radars measure precipitation velocity from $f_d$.
- **Important constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T_0 = 290$ K.
- **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** Forgetting the **factor 2** in either range or Doppler — instant 1–2 mark loss.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — RADAR and Doppler Effect**
