# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation of Key Equations

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylinder Thrust Force ($F = p \cdot A$)

**Statement:** The theoretical thrust force developed by a hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder equals the supply pressure multiplied by the effective piston area on the pressurized side.

**Given:**
- Piston diameter $D$ (m)
- Rod diameter $d$ (m)
- Supply pressure $p$ (Pa)

**Step 1 — Identify the effective area on the cap (blank) end:**
The full circular piston area is exposed to pressure.

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the effective area on the rod end:**
The rod occupies a portion of the piston face, reducing the effective area.

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's second law to the piston (quasi-static, ignoring friction and inertia):**
Sum of forces on the piston in the extension direction = load force $F$.

$$\sum F_{ext} = p \cdot A_{cap} - p \cdot A_{rod} - F = 0$$

Wait — this is the case when **both sides are pressurized**. For **single-rod cylinder extending under pressure on cap side only**, the rod side discharges to tank at $p \approx 0$:

$$F_{ext} = p \cdot A_{cap} - 0 = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Final result (extension):**

$$F_{extend} = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 5 — For retraction (pressure applied to rod end, cap end to tank):**

$$F_{retract} = p \cdot \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}$$

**Step 6 — Force ratio (important design metric):**

$$\frac{F_{retract}}{F_{extend}} = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{2}$$

**Example numerical check (KTU-style):** A cylinder with $D = 63$ mm, $d = 45$ mm, $p = 100$ bar = $10 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi (0.063)^{2}}{4} = 3.117 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$F_{extend} = (10 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (3.117 \times 10^{-3}) = 31170 \text{ N} \approx 31.17 \text{ kN}$$

$$\frac{F_{retract}}{F_{extend}} = 1 - \left(\frac{45}{63}\right)^{2} = 1 - 0.5102 = 0.4898 \approx 49\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A double-acting single-rod cylinder produces only ~**50% retract force** compared to extend force (for $d/D \approx 0.7$). This asymmetry matters in motion-control design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Piston Velocity and Cylinder Flow Rate ($v = Q/A$)

**Statement:** The piston velocity is the volumetric flow rate supplied to the cylinder divided by the effective piston area.

**Step 1 — Consider the control volume of fluid entering the cylinder cap end.**
In time $dt$, a volume $dV = Q \cdot dt$ enters.
This volume must equal the volume swept by the piston: $dV = A \cdot dx$, where $dx$ is piston displacement.

$$Q \cdot dt = A \cdot dx$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $dt$:**

$$Q = A \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = A \cdot v$$

**Step 3 — Solve for velocity:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Extend to retract motion (rod end pressurized):**

$$v_{retract} = \frac{Q}{A_{rod}} = \frac{Q}{\frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}}$$

**Step 5 — Time to complete a stroke of length $L$:**

$$t_{stroke} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \cdot A}{Q}$$

**Example:** A cylinder with $D = 100$ mm, $L = 0.5$ m, supplied by a pump delivering $Q = 15$ L/min = $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.1)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$v = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0318 \text{ m/s} = 31.8 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$t_{stroke} = \frac{0.5}{0.0318} = 15.7 \text{ s}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydraulic / Pneumatic Power ($P = p \cdot Q$)

**Statement:** The power transmitted by a fluid-power system is the product of pressure and volumetric flow rate.

**Step 1 — Start from first principles.**
Work done by pressure force on a moving piston:

$$dW = F \cdot dx = (p \cdot A) \cdot dx = p \cdot (A \cdot dx) = p \cdot dV$$

**Step 2 — Power is the rate of doing work:**

$$P = \frac{dW}{dt} = p \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} = p \cdot Q$$

**Step 3 — Converting to common industrial units:**
If $p$ is in **bar** and $Q$ is in **L/min**, then:

$$P \text{ (kW)} = \frac{p \text{ (bar)} \times Q \text{ (L/min)}}{600}$$

(The 600 comes from: $1$ bar = $10^{5}$ Pa, $1$ L/min = $10^{-3}/60$ m³/s, so $1 \text{ bar} \times 1 \text{ L/min} = 10^{5} \times 1.667 \times 10^{-5} = 1.667$ W = $1/600$ kW.)

**Step 4 — Verify with example:**
$p = 200$ bar, $Q = 50$ L/min.

$$P = \frac{200 \times 50}{600} = 16.67 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical output power for a cylinder:**

$$P_{mech} = F \cdot v = (p \cdot A) \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{A}\right) = p \cdot Q$$

This confirms the **fundamental identity:** *fluid power in* = *mechanical power out* (in the ideal lossless case).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hydraulic Cylinder Transfer Function (Block-Diagram Level)

This is the **most important derivation for Module 2 of PEMET414**, as it directly gives you the block-diagram model.

**Step 1 — Write the force balance on the piston + load (Newton's 2nd Law):**

$$M \ddot{x} + B \dot{x} + K x + F_{L} = A_{p} p_{L} - F_{fric}$$

where $M$ = total moving mass, $B$ = viscous damping coefficient, $K$ = load spring constant, $F_{L}$ = external load disturbance, $p_{L}$ = load pressure (pressure difference across piston), $A_{p}$ = piston area, $F_{fric}$ = friction force.

**Step 2 — Write the flow continuity equation for the cylinder chamber:**
Fluid compressibility in the chamber:

$$Q_{L} = A_{p} \dot{x} + \frac{V_{t}}{4 \beta_{e}} \dot{p}_{L} + C_{tc} p_{L}$$

where $Q_{L}$ = load flow (into the chamber), $V_{t}$ = total trapped volume (line + chamber), $\beta_{e}$ = effective bulk modulus, $C_{tc}$ = total leakage coefficient.

**Step 3 — Linearize for small perturbations about an operating point.**
(We use standard servo-hydraulics linearization; the result is a 3rd-order system.)

**Step 4 — Take the Laplace Transform (zero initial conditions):**

$$(Ms^{2} + Bs + K) X(s) = A_{p} P_{L}(s) - F_{L}(s)$$

$$Q_{L}(s) = A_{p} s X(s) + \left( \frac{V_{t}}{4 \beta_{e}} s + C_{tc} \right) P_{L}(s)$$

**Step 5 — Define the hydraulic natural frequency:**

$$\omega_{h} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \beta_{e} A_{p}^{2}}{V_{t} M}}$$

**Step 6 — Define the hydraulic damping ratio:**

$$\zeta_{h} = \frac{C_{tc}}{2 A_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{\beta_{e} M}{V_{t}}} + \frac{B}{2 A_{p}} \sqrt{\frac{V_{t}}{\beta_{e} M}}$$

**Step 7 — Combine to get the cylinder transfer function (position output for valve flow input):**

$$G_{cyl}(s) = \frac{X(s)}{Q_{L}(s)} = \frac{1/A_{p}}{s \left( \dfrac{s^{2}}{\omega_{h}^{2}} + \dfrac{2 \zeta_{h}}{\omega_{h}} s + 1 \right)}$$

**Step 8 — Interpret the result for block-diagram use:**
- The cylinder is an **integrator** (the $1/s$ factor) — position is the integral of velocity.
- The quadratic term in the denominator introduces **resonance** and **phase lag**.
- Typical $\omega_{h}$ values: **30 rad/s to 500 rad/s** (industrial cylinders).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Pneumatic Cylinder Force–Velocity Tradeoff (Compressed Air Energy)

**Step 1 — For a pneumatic cylinder, the supply pressure is not constant during stroke** (unlike hydraulics). It decays as the regulator-reservoir releases air.

**Step 2 — Use the isothermal expansion equation (slow stroke, n = 1):**

$$p_{1} V_{1} = p_{2} V_{2}$$

For a piston starting at position $x_{0}$ and moving to $x$:

$$p_{s} \cdot (V_{0} + A x_{0}) = p \cdot (V_{0} + A x)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for instantaneous pressure $p(x)$:**

$$p(x) = p_{s} \cdot \frac{V_{0} + A x_{0}}{V_{0} + A x}$$

**Step 4 — The instantaneous force on the piston:**

$$F(x) = p(x) \cdot A = p_{s} A \cdot \frac{V_{0} + A x_{0}}{V_{0} + A x}$$

**Step 5 — As $x \to \infty$ (long stroke), $F(x) \to 0$** — pneumatic force is **stroke-limited**! This is the *fundamental limitation* of single-acting pneumatic cylinders, and the reason double-acting cylinders with re-pressurization are preferred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Pneumatic cylinders are ideal for *short-stroke, high-speed* operations (pick-and-place, clamping, sorting). For *long-stroke, constant-force* applications, hydraulics wins by a wide margin.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Block-Diagram Level)

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Open-Loop Hydraulic Cylinder System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EM1["ELECTRIC MOTOR"]
    P1["HYDRAULIC PUMP"]
    RSV["RESERVOIR AND FILTER"]
    DV["DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE 4 3"]
    CYL["HYDRAULIC CYLINDER DOUBLE ACTING"]
    LOAD["MECHANICAL LOAD"]
    TANK["TANK RETURN LINE"]

    EM1 --> P1
    P1 --> RSV
    RSV --> DV
    DV --> CYL
    CYL --> LOAD
    CYL --> TANK
    DV --> TANK

    P1 -- PRESSURIZED OIL --> DV
    DV -- METERED FLOW --> CYL
    CYL -- FORCE AND VELOCITY --> LOAD
```

**Reading the diagram:** The motor drives the pump, which draws oil from the reservoir and delivers pressurized oil to the directional control valve. The valve directs flow into the cylinder chamber, pushing the piston to move the load. Exhaust oil returns to the tank.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Hydraulic Position Servo

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["REFERENCE POSITION INPUT"]
    SUM["SUMMING JUNCTION MINUS"]
    AMP["SERVO AMPLIFIER GAIN KA"]
    SV["SERVO VALVE TRANSFER FUNCTION KQ S"]
    QBLK["HYDRAULIC CYLINDER TRANSFER FUNCTION G CYL S"]
    LOAD["LOAD MECHANICAL"]
    FB["POSITION SENSOR LVDT OR ENCODER"]
    ROUT["OUTPUT POSITION Y S"]

    R --> SUM
    SUM --> AMP
    AMP --> SV
    SV --> QBLK
    QBLK --> LOAD
    LOAD --> ROUT
    ROUT --> FB
    FB --> SUM

    FB -- FEEDBACK SIGNAL --> SUM
    SV -- METERED FLOW --> QBLK
    QBLK -- PISTON MOTION --> LOAD
```

**Reading the diagram:** The reference input $r$ is compared with the feedback $y_{fb}$ at the summing junction (error $e = r - y_{fb}$). The error is amplified, drives the servo valve (which controls flow), which drives the cylinder, which moves the load. The position sensor closes the loop. The *closed-loop transfer function* is:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{amp} \cdot G_{valve} \cdot G_{cyl}}{1 + G_{amp} \cdot G_{valve} \cdot G_{cyl} \cdot H_{fb}}$$

This is the standard *single-input single-output (SISO)* control structure.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pneumatic System (with FRL Unit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ATM["ATMOSPHERIC AIR"]
    COMP["AIR COMPRESSOR"]
    RCVR["RECEIVER TANK"]
    FRL["FRL UNIT FILTER REGULATOR LUBRICATOR"]
    DPV["DIRECTIONAL VALVE 5 2"]
    PCYL["PNEUMATIC CYLINDER DOUBLE ACTING"]
    PLOAD["PNEUMATIC LOAD"]
    EXH["EXHAUST TO ATMOSPHERE"]
    CTRL["CONTROLLER PLC OR ELECTRONIC"]

    ATM --> COMP
    COMP --> RCVR
    RCVR --> FRL
    FRL --> DPV
    DPV --> PCYL
    PCYL --> PLOAD
    PCYL --> EXH
    DPV --> EXH
    CTRL --> DPV

    RCVR -- COMPRESSED AIR 6 TO 8 BAR --> FRL
    FRL -- CLEAN REGULATED LUBRICATED AIR --> DPV
    DPV -- METERED AIR --> PCYL
    PCYL -- LINEAR MOTION --> PLOAD
```

**Reading the diagram:** Atmospheric air is compressed, stored in a receiver tank, conditioned (filtered, pressure-regulated, lubricated) by the FRL unit, then routed by the directional control valve to the pneumatic cylinder. The PLC or electronic controller commands the valve position. Exhaust air is vented (with mufflers for noise reduction).

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Functional Decomposition (Common to Both)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ENERGY INPUT ELECTRICAL"]
    B["PRIME MOVER MOTOR OR COMPRESSOR"]
    C["FLUID POWER GENERATION PUMP OR RECEIVER"]
    D["FLUID CONDITIONING FRL OR FILTER AND ACCUMULATOR"]
    E["DISTRIBUTION AND CONTROL VALVES"]
    F["ACTUATOR CYLINDER OR MOTOR"]
    G["MECHANICAL OUTPUT FORCE VELOCITY OR TORQUE SPEED"]
    H["SENSORS AND FEEDBACK LVDT ENCODER PRESSURE GAUGE"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    F --> H
    H --> E

    F -- ENERGY CONVERSION --> G
    H -- FEEDBACK SIGNAL --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the *universal block diagram* for any fluid-power actuator system. It shows five forward blocks (energy source → prime mover → fluid power → conditioning → actuator) and one feedback path. The dashed feedback arrow represents the **closed-loop control** path that turns an open-loop system into a precise servo system.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Hydraulic Motor (Rotary Actuator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PRESSURIZED OIL INLET"]
    MV["MOTOR VALVE PLATE"]
    HM["HYDRAULIC MOTOR GEAR OR PISTON OR VANE"]
    SHAFT["OUTPUT SHAFT TORQUE T SPEED OMEGA"]
    DRAIN["CASE DRAIN RETURN TO TANK"]

    P1 --> MV
    MV --> HM
    HM --> SHAFT
    HM --> DRAIN

    P1 -- HIGH PRESSURE --> MV
    MV -- METERED OIL --> HM
    HM -- ROTARY MOTION --> SHAFT
```

**Reading the diagram:** Pressurized oil enters through the inlet port, the valve plate distributes it to the motor's working chambers, the motor converts pressure × flow into torque × angular velocity at the output shaft. Case drain prevents internal leakage from mixing with the return line.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEMET414 Module 2]**

*With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a hydraulic actuator system. List the major functional blocks.*

**Model Answer (Stepwise, 3-Mark Weight):**

A hydraulic actuator system converts electrical energy into linear or rotary mechanical motion using pressurized oil as the working medium. At the block-diagram level, the system consists of the following five functional blocks:

**(i) Energy Source Block:** An electric motor that supplies mechanical power to the pump. **(1 mark)**

**(ii) Prime Mover / Pump Block:** A hydraulic pump (gear, vane, or piston type) that converts mechanical power into hydraulic power (pressure × flow). The output pressure is typically 100–350 bar. **(1 mark)**

**(iii) Conditioning and Distribution Block:** Includes the reservoir, filter, pressure-relief valve, and directional control valve. The reservoir stores and cools oil; the filter removes contaminants; the relief valve limits maximum pressure; the directional valve routes flow to the cylinder. **(0.5 mark)**

**(iv) Actuator Block:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder (for linear motion) or hydraulic motor (for rotary motion) that converts hydraulic power back to mechanical power. **(0.5 mark)**

**(v) Feedback Block (for servo systems):** Position sensor (LVDT or encoder) and pressure transducer provide feedback signals to close the control loop.

**Block Diagram (must be drawn):**

```
[Motor] → [Pump] → [Filter & Relief Valve] → [Directional Valve] → [Cylinder] → [Load]
                                                                                  ↑
                                                                            [Position Sensor]
```

---

### **Question A2** (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEMET414 Module 2]**

*Compare hydraulic and pneumatic actuators based on: (i) working fluid, (ii) operating pressure, (iii) force density, and (iv) compressibility effect.*

**Model Answer (Tabular Form, 3-Mark Weight):**

| Parameter | Hydraulic Actuator | Pneumatic Actuator |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **(i) Working fluid** | Incompressible oil (ISO VG 32/46) **(0.75)** | Compressible air (filtered, dried) **(0.75)** |
| **(ii) Operating pressure** | 100 – 350 bar (high) **(0.75)** | 4 – 8 bar (low, ~6 bar standard) **(0.75)** |
| **(iii) Force density** | Very high (up to 35 N/mm²) | Low to moderate (~0.6 N/mm²) |
| **(iv) Compressibility** | Negligible (β ≈ 1.5–2.0 GPa); nearly incompressible **(0.75)** | High (air follows ideal gas law $pV = mRT$); affects stiffness and position accuracy **(0.75)** |
| Applications | Heavy machinery, presses, machine tools | Assembly lines, pick-and-place, clamping |

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A** (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEMET414 Module 2]**

*Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a closed-loop hydraulic position servo system. Derive the transfer function of the hydraulic cylinder and obtain the expression for the hydraulic natural frequency.*

#### **Part (a) — Block Diagram and Working [7 Marks]**

A closed-loop hydraulic position servo system is used to precisely control the position of a load. The block diagram is:

```
                +    e
   r   ──────►(−)─────────►[Ka]──►[Servo Valve Kq/s]──►[Cylinder Gcyl(s)]──►[Load]──► y
              ▲                                                                      │
              │                                                                      │
              └──────────────[Position Sensor H(s)]◄─────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Working Principle [3 marks]:**

(i) The reference position $r(t)$ is commanded by the user (e.g., from a PLC or joystick). **(0.5)**

(ii) The summing junction compares the reference $r$ with the measured position $y$ (via LVDT/encoder) and generates an error signal $e = r - y$. **(0.5)**

(iii) The error $e$ is amplified by the servo amplifier (gain $K_a$) and sent to the servo valve. **(0.5)**

(iv) The servo valve regulates the flow $Q_L$ to the hydraulic cylinder proportionally to the control current/voltage. **(0.5)**

(v) The cylinder converts the metered flow into piston displacement $x$ (and force $F$). The piston moves the mechanical load. **(0.5)**

(vi) The position sensor measures $x$ and feeds it back; the loop continues until $e \approx 0$, i.e., $y \approx r$. **(0.5)**

**Key Components Identified [2 marks]:**

- Servo valve (e.g., Moog, Bosch Rexroth) — the heart of the servo, with spool position proportional to input current. **(0.5)**
- Double-acting hydraulic cylinder (single-rod type, with position sensor). **(0.5)**
- Position sensor (LVDT for short stroke, optical encoder for long stroke). **(0.5)**
- Servo amplifier (current driver for the torque motor of the servo valve). **(0.5)**

**Advantages [2 marks]:**

- High positioning accuracy (down to **±0.01 mm** with precision servos). **(0.5)**
- High stiffness (oil incompressibility). **(0.5)**
- High force/weight ratio. **(0.5)**
- Fast response (bandwidth up to **50–100 Hz**). **(0.5)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Transfer Function Derivation and Natural Frequency [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Force balance on piston + load (Newton's 2nd Law): [2 marks]**

$$M \ddot{x} + B \dot{x} + K x = A_p p_L - F_L$$

where $M$ = total moving mass (piston + load), $B$ = viscous damping, $K$ = load stiffness, $A_p$ = piston area, $p_L$ = load pressure = $p_1 - p_2$ (pressure difference across piston), $F_L$ = external load disturbance.

**Step 2 — Flow continuity equation: [2 marks]**

$$Q_L = A_p \dot{x} + \frac{V_t}{4 \beta_e} \dot{p}_L + C_{tc} p_L$$

where $Q_L$ = load flow, $V_t$ = total trapped volume (line volume + chamber volume), $\beta_e$ = effective bulk modulus (≈ 0.7 × $\beta$ oil, accounting for entrained air), $C_{tc}$ = total leakage coefficient.

**Step 3 — Laplace transform (zero initial conditions): [1 mark]**

$$(Ms^{2} + Bs + K) X(s) = A_p P_L(s) - F_L(s)$$

$$Q_L(s) = A_p s X(s) + \left( \frac{V_t}{4 \beta_e} s + C_{tc} \right) P_L(s)$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $P_L(s)$ and solve for $X(s)/Q_L(s)$: [1 mark]**

After algebraic manipulation (express $P_L$ from flow eq, substitute in force eq), and ignoring $F_L$ for the transfer function:

$$\frac{X(s)}{Q_L(s)} = \frac{1/A_p}{s \left( \dfrac{s^{2}}{\omega_{h}^{2}} + \dfrac{2 \zeta_{h}}{\omega_{h}} s + 1 \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic natural frequency: [1 mark]**

$$\boxed{\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{4 \beta_e A_p^{2}}{V_t M}} \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Numerical Example:** $A_p = 7.85 \times 10^{-3}$ m² (100 mm bore), $V_t = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, $\beta_e = 1.0 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $M = 50$ kg.

$$\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times (10^{9}) \times (7.85 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{(2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 50}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 10^{9} \times 6.16 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1}}$$

$$= \sqrt{2.464 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1570 \text{ rad/s} \approx 250 \text{ Hz}$$

---

### **QUESTION B** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEMET414 Module 2]**

*With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the working of a pneumatic actuator system used in industrial automation. Derive the expression for the air consumption of a double-acting pneumatic cylinder.*

#### **Part (a) — Pneumatic System Block Diagram and Working [7 Marks]**

A pneumatic actuator system in industrial automation typically has the following block diagram:

```
[Atmosphere] → [Compressor] → [Receiver Tank] → [FRL Unit] → [Directional Valve 5/2] → [Cylinder] → [Load]
                                                                                                            ↑
                                                                                              [Limit Switches/Sensors]
                                                                                                            ↓
                                                                                            [PLC / Electronic Controller]
```

**Working Principle [3 marks]:**

(i) Atmospheric air is drawn in by a compressor (typically reciprocating or screw type) and compressed to a standard industrial pressure of **6–8 bar (0.6–0.8 MPa)**. **(0.5)**

(ii) The compressed air is stored in a receiver tank, which smooths pulsations and provides a buffer for peak demand. **(0.5)**

(iii) The air passes through an **FRL unit** — Filter (removes moisture and dust), Regulator (maintains constant downstream pressure), Lubricator (injects oil mist for valve sealing). **(0.5)**

(iv) A **5/2 or 3/2 directional control valve** (solenoid or pneumatically actuated) routes air to either the cap end or rod end of the cylinder, controlling direction. **(0.5)**

(v) The **double-acting pneumatic cylinder** converts air pressure into linear motion, moving the mechanical load (e.g., a gripper, clamp, or stopper). **(0.5)**

(vi) Limit switches or position sensors signal the end of stroke; the PLC reverses the valve to retract the cylinder, completing one cycle. **(0.5)**

**Why Pneumatics is Preferred in Automation [2 marks]:**

- **Clean and safe** — air leakage does not contaminate the workplace (unlike oil leaks). **(0.5)**
- **Fast response** — small pneumatic cylinders respond in **5–30 ms**, ideal for high-speed pick-and-place. **(0.5)**
- **Simple and low-cost** — fewer components, easier maintenance than hydraulics. **(0.5)**
- **Intrinsically safe** — no fire hazard; safe in explosive environments (with proper certification). **(0.5)**

**Components Identification [2 marks]:**

- Compressor (e.g., 7.5 kW screw compressor). **(0.5)**
- FRL unit (Filter + Regulator + Lubricator combination). **(0.5)**
- Solenoid-operated directional valve (e.g., 5/2 way, 24 VDC coil). **(0.5)**
- Magnetic reed / pneumatic position sensors on the cylinder. **(0.5)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Derivation of Air Consumption [7 Marks]**

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the free air consumption of a double-acting pneumatic cylinder per minute.

**Step 1 — Identify the two volumes per stroke: [1 mark]**

For a double-acting single-rod cylinder with bore $D$ and rod diameter $d$:

- **Cap-end (extension) volume swept:** $V_{cap} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot L$
- **Rod-end (retraction) volume swept:** $V_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} \cdot L$

where $L$ = stroke length.

**Step 2 — Add both volumes (one complete cycle = extend + retract): [1 mark]**

$$V_{cycle} = V_{cap} + V_{rod} = \frac{\pi L}{4} \left[ D^{2} + (D^{2} - d^{2}) \right] = \frac{\pi L}{4} (2D^{2} - d^{2})$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by number of cycles per minute $N$: [1 mark]**

$$V_{disp} = N \cdot V_{cycle} = \frac{\pi L N}{4} (2D^{2} - d^{2})$$

This is the **displaced volume per minute** at cylinder (working) pressure.

**Step 4 — Convert to free air delivery (FAD) at atmospheric pressure: [2 marks]**

By Boyle's law (isothermal, $p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_2$):

$$p_{atm} \cdot Q_{FAD} = p_{work} \cdot V_{disp}$$

$$Q_{FAD} = \frac{p_{work}}{p_{atm}} \cdot V_{disp}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{Q_{FAD} = \frac{\pi L N}{4} (2D^{2} - d^{2}) \cdot \frac{p_{work}}{p_{atm}} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{min (free air)}}$$

**Step 5 — Account for volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ (valve leakage, seal leakage, dead volume): [1 mark]**

$$Q_{actual} = \frac{Q_{FAD}}{\eta_v} \quad \text{where } \eta_v \approx 0.85 \text{ to } 0.95 \text{ for industrial cylinders}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example [1 mark]:**

Given: $D = 50$ mm, $d = 25$ mm, $L = 100$ mm, $N = 30$ cycles/min, $p_{work} = 6$ bar (gauge) = 7 bar absolute, $p_{atm} = 1$ bar absolute.

$$V_{cycle} = \frac{\pi (0.1)}{4} (2 \times 0.05^{2} - 0.025^{2}) = \frac{0.0314}{4} (0.005 - 0.000625)$$

$$= 0.00785 \times 0.004375 = 3.43 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3} = 0.0343 \text{ L}$$

$$V_{disp} = 30 \times 0.0343 = 1.03 \text{ L/min at 7 bar abs}$$

$$Q_{FAD} = \frac{7}{1} \times 1.03 = 7.21 \text{ L/min free air}$$

**Practical use:** This free air delivery must be supplied by the compressor. Compressor sizing: $Q_{comp} \geq Q_{FAD} / \eta_{comp}$, where $\eta_{comp} \approx 0.7$ for a typical screw compressor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the rod-side volume** — Many students compute only $V_{cap}$ and forget the retract volume. This underestimates air consumption by **~25–40%** depending on $d/D$ ratio. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure** — Industrial pressure regulators show **gauge pressure** (6 bar gauge = 7 bar absolute). Using gauge pressure in Boyle's law gives wrong FAD. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Not drawing the block diagram** — In Part B questions, even a hand-drawn rough block diagram is mandatory. Without it, full marks are not awarded. **Loss: up to 4 marks.**
>
> 4. **Confusing hydraulic and pneumatic formulas** — Students often write $F = pA$ for both, but for **pneumatic long strokes**, the force is *not constant* (compressibility). State the assumption clearly. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Missing the $1/s$ integrator in the cylinder transfer function** — The cylinder is an integrator (position = integral of flow/area). Forgetting this loses a mark in derivation.
>
> 6. **Wrong bulk modulus** — Use $\beta_e$ (effective, accounting for entrained air) ≈ 70% of pure oil's $\beta$, **not** the full 1.5–2.0 GPa. **Loss: 1 mark in numerical problems.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Hydraulic & Pneumatic Actuators: Block-Diagram-Level Master Checklist**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ Hydraulic actuator: uses **incompressible oil** at **100–350 bar** for **high-force, high-stiffness** applications.
- ☐ Pneumatic actuator: uses **compressible air** at **4–8 bar** for **clean, fast, safe** applications.
- ☐ Block-diagram level = abstracting the system into **transfer functions** in a **signal/energy flow graph**.

**The Five Universal Functional Blocks (memorize the order):**
1. ☐ **Energy Source** (Electric motor / Air supply)
2. ☐ **Prime Mover** (Pump / Compressor)
3. ☐ **Conditioning** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator for pneumatics; filter + reservoir + relief valve for hydraulics)
4. ☐ **Control & Distribution** (Directional valves, servo valves, proportional valves)
5. ☐ **Actuator** (Cylinder for linear, Motor for rotary) + **Feedback Sensors** for closed loop

**Must-Know Formulas (Top 7 for KTU ESE):**
- ☐ $F = pA$ (force from pressure)
- ☐ $v = Q/A$ (velocity from flow)
- ☐ $P = pQ$ (fluid power)
- ☐ $P = Fv$ (mechanical power, linear)
- ☐ $P = T\omega$ (mechanical power, rotary)
- ☐ $\omega_h = \sqrt{4\beta_e A_p^2 / (V_t M)}$ (hydraulic natural frequency)
- ☐ $G_{cyl}(s) = \dfrac{1/A_p}{s(s^2/\omega_h^2 + 2\zeta_h s/\omega_h + 1)}$ (cylinder transfer function)

**Key Conceptual Distinctions:**
- ☐ Hydraulics → incompressible → constant force/velocity → **integrator + resonant mode**.
- ☐ Pneumatics → compressible → force decays with stroke → **nonlinear, stroke-limited**.
- ☐ Single-rod cylinder: $F_{extend}/F_{retract} = 1/(1 - (d/D)^2)$ — retract force is always smaller.
- ☐ Closed-loop = **servo system**; open-loop = **on-off or proportional** without position feedback.

**Design Selection Rule of Thumb (for exam answers):**
- ☐ Force > 50 kN **or** pressure accuracy < 1% **→ use hydraulics**.
- ☐ Force < 10 kN **and** clean environment **and** high cycle rate **→ use pneumatics**.
- ☐ Mixed (e.g., machine tool) **→ use both**: hydraulics for axis drives, pneumatics for tool changers and clamps.

**KTU 2024 Frequently-Tested Topics:**
- ☐ Drawing and labeling the block diagram of a **closed-loop hydraulic position servo**.
- ☐ Deriving the **cylinder transfer function** and identifying $\omega_h$ and $\zeta_h$.
- ☐ Computing **air consumption** of a pneumatic cylinder (in $\text{Nm}^3/\text{min}$ or L/min free air).
- ☐ Numerical problems on $F = pA$ and $v = Q/A$ for given cylinder dimensions.
- ☐ Comparing hydraulic vs pneumatic on at least 4 parameters (table format).

**Final Memory Aid:**
> "**Hydraulics = Heavy, Hard, Incompressible. Pneumatics = Light, Quick, Compressible. Both share the same block-diagram structure: Source → Prime Mover → Condition → Control → Actuate → Sense → Feedback.**"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Actuator?

An **actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into a physical motion — typically linear displacement or rotational torque. In an *intelligent system*, the actuator is the **final execution element** that delivers usable work to the external environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> An actuator is the output transducer of an automated/robotic system that produces mechanical work in response to a control command, forming the *muscular system* of any intelligent machine.

## 1.2 The Two Energy Domains Covered in This Topic

| Domain | Working Fluid | Typical Pressure | Energy Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic** | Incompressible oil (Mineral/Synthetic) | 50 – 700 bar | Electric motor → Pump |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressible air / inert gas | 4 – 10 bar (industrial) | Compressor + Reservoir |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydraulic actuator = "the bodybuilder"** 🏋️
> Uses a *thick, oily fluid* that cannot be squished (incompressible). Just like a bodybuilder can lift enormous weights slowly and steadily with controlled precision, a hydraulic actuator produces **massive force at controlled, slow speeds**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pneumatic actuator = "the sprinter"** 🏃
> Uses *thin, light air* that can be squished (compressible). Like a sprinter who moves light weights incredibly fast with snappy, jerky motion, a pneumatic actuator produces **fast, light, repeatable motion** but with limited force.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Hydraulic operating pressure range:** $P_{hyd} = 50$ to $700 \text{ bar}$ ($5$ to $70 \text{ MPa}$)
- **Pneumatic operating pressure range:** $P_{pneu} = 4$ to $10 \text{ bar}$ (typical industrial supply = **6 bar**)
- **Bulk modulus of hydraulic oil:** $\beta \approx 1.5$ to $2.0 \text{ GPa}$ (defines incompressibility)
- **Speed of sound in air (compressibility limit):** $c \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$
- **Power conversion:** $1 \text{ HP} = 745.7 \text{ W}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics

The fundamental force–pressure relationship that governs **every fluid-power actuator** is:

$$F = P \times A$$

Where:
- $F$ = Thrust force developed (N)
- $P$ = Gauge pressure of the fluid (Pa or N/m²)
- $A$ = Effective piston area (m²)

For a **double-acting cylinder** of bore diameter $D$ and rod diameter $d$:

$$F_{extend} = P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$F_{retract} = P \times \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

### Why This Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because pneumatic air is **highly compressible** (low bulk modulus), the instantaneous force $F$ varies significantly with piston position and load. Hydraulic oil, being nearly **incompressible**, delivers an almost **constant force** throughout the stroke — a key advantage in precision positioning.

## 2.2 The Three Core "Why's" Behind Actuator Selection

1. **Force Magnitude** → Hydraulic wins for high-load applications (e.g., injection moulding clamp force).
2. **Speed & Cleanliness** → Pneumatic wins for fast, repetitive, food/pharma-grade motion.
3. **Cost & Simplicity** → Pneumatic wins for low-budget, low-maintenance setups.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Basic force-pressure relation | $F$ [N], $P$ [Pa], $A$ [m²] |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Piston area (full bore) | m² |
| 3 | $A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | Annular (rod-side) area | m² |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Piston velocity from flow rate $Q$ | m/s |
| 5 | $P_{hyd} = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure (in static oil column) | Pa |
| 6 | $W = F \cdot s = P \cdot A \cdot s = P \cdot V$ | Work done by actuator | J |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\beta = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$ | Bulk modulus (incompressibility) | Pa |
| 9 | $Q = C_d A_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Flow through valve orifice | m³/s |
| 10 | $\text{Reynolds no.: } Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | Determines flow regime in pipes | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU numericals:**
> Hydraulic systems typically deliver **25× the force** of an equivalent-sized pneumatic system at industrial supply pressures (200 bar vs 6 bar → ratio ≈ 33, minus losses).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic systems** dominate: aircraft control surfaces, excavators, hydraulic presses, ABS brakes, machine tools, die-casting.
- **Pneumatic systems** dominate: pick-and-place robots in packaging, dental drills, paint sprayers, mine-pneumatic tools, semiconductor cleanrooms.
- **Hybrid systems** (electro-hydrostatic actuators — EHA) are the modern trend in aerospace (e.g., Airbus A380).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Advantages & Disadvantages

## 3.1 HYDRAULIC ACTUATORS

### 3.1.1 Advantages (Engineering Strengths)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks students to list **at least 5 advantages and 5 disadvantages** of each actuator. Memorize the bullets below — they appear in Part A (3-mark) questions.

**A1. Very High Force/Power Density**
Hydraulic oil at 200 bar applied to a 100 mm bore produces:

$$F = P \times A = 200 \times 10^5 \times \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4}$$

$$F = 200 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F = 1.5708 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 157 \text{ kN}$$

This compact package can lift ~16 tonnes — far exceeding pneumatic capability.

**A2. Precise Position & Speed Control**
Closed-loop servo valves achieve micron-level positioning accuracy (used in CNC, robotic arms).

**A3. Self-Lubricating**
Oil lubricates moving parts, reducing wear and extending service life to **> 10⁷ cycles**.

**A4. Smooth, Vibration-Free Operation**
Incompressibility eliminates jerkiness; ideal for **die-casting** and **injection moulding**.

**A5. Constant Force Throughout Stroke**
Because fluid is incompressible, force does not vary with displacement.

**A6. Overload Protection**
Relief valves automatically bleed excess pressure → inherent safety against mechanical overload.

**A7. Multi-Axis Control**
Single power-pack can drive many actuators through valves → centralized control.

**A8. Load-Holding Capability**
Directional control valves can *lock* a cylinder in position without consuming energy (statically stable).

### 3.1.2 Disadvantages (Engineering Limitations)

**D1. Leakage**
Even micro-leaks cause **dripping oil**, contamination, fire hazards, and environmental pollution.

**D2. High Initial & Maintenance Cost**
Precision pumps, valves, seals, and filtration are expensive.

**D3. Requires Auxiliary Equipment**
Needs reservoir, filter, cooler, pump, pressure regulator — bulky installation.

**D4. Noise**
Pumps and valves produce **75 – 95 dB** noise, requiring acoustic enclosures.

**D5. Fire & Environmental Hazard**
Mineral oil is flammable; spills contaminate soil and water.

**D6. Temperature Sensitivity**
Viscosity varies with temperature → affects performance (cold sluggish, hot thin).

**D7. Slow Response at High Loads**
Inertia of fluid limits dynamic bandwidth (~10–50 Hz typical).

**D8. Contamination Sensitivity**
Particles > 5 µm can damage servo valves → mandatory filtration.

---

## 3.2 PNEUMATIC ACTUATORS

### 3.2.1 Advantages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pneumatic = **"SIMPLE-CLEAN-CHEAP-FAST-SAFE"** — a great KTU mnemonic.

**P1. Simple Design & Low Cost**
Compressors, hoses, and cylinders are cheap; ideal for mass production lines.

**P2. Clean & Contamination-Free**
Air is harmless, leak-tolerant, and doesn't pollute → **food, pharma, semiconductor** use.

**P3. Explosion-Proof (Intrinsically Safe)**
No sparks, no flammable fluid → safe in **coal mines, oil refineries, paint booths**.

**P4. Fast Response & High Speed**
Pneumatic pistons can reach 1–3 m/s; ideal for **pick-and-place robots**.

**P5. Lightweight & Compact (for small forces)**
No heavy pumps; air reservoir on compressor side only.

**P6. Overload-Tolerant**
Compressible air cushions shock loads — components survive stalling.

**P7. Easy Storage & Distribution**
Air can be stored in tanks and piped across factories.

**P8. Wide Temperature Range**
Operates from $-30°\text{C}$ to $+200°\text{C}$ without degradation.

### 3.2.2 Disadvantages

**Q1. Low Force Output**
At 6 bar with 100 mm bore:

$$F = 6 \times 10^5 \times \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 4712 \text{ N} \approx 4.7 \text{ kN}$$

That is **~33× less** than the equivalent hydraulic cylinder. Hence unsuitable for heavy loads.

**Q2. Compressibility → Poor Positioning Accuracy**
Air compresses under load → spring-like behaviour; position varies with load. Open-loop accuracy ≈ ±1 mm; closed-loop rarely better than ±0.1 mm.

**Q3. Noise**
Compressed air exhaust reaches **90 – 110 dB**; needs silencers.

**Q4. Energy Inefficient**
Compressing air wastes ~80% of input electrical energy as heat.

**Q5. Requires Clean, Dry Air**
Moisture causes rust; oil aerosols contaminate products → mandatory FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator).

**Q6. Cannot Hold Load Statically**
Air leaks → load drifts; requires external mechanical brake or check-valve circuits.

**Q7. Lubrication Issues**
Air doesn't lubricate — components need external greasing.

**Q8. Limited Stroke**
Practical strokes rarely exceed 2 m without buckling.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU-High-Yield)

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) | — |
| Typical pressure | 50 – 700 bar | 4 – 10 bar | Hydraulic |
| Force output | Very high | Low | Hydraulic |
| Speed | Moderate (≤ 1 m/s) | High (≤ 3 m/s) | Pneumatic |
| Positioning accuracy | High (μm) | Low (mm) | Hydraulic |
| Cleanliness | Dirty (oil leaks) | Clean | Pneumatic |
| Fire safety | Risk | Intrinsically safe | Pneumatic |
| Cost | High | Low | Pneumatic |
| Maintenance | Complex | Simple | Pneumatic |
| Noise | Moderate (75–95 dB) | High (90–110 dB) | Hydraulic |
| Energy efficiency | ~70 – 80% | ~15 – 25% | Hydraulic |
| Load holding | Excellent | Poor | Hydraulic |
| Stiffness (load resistance) | Very high | Low | Hydraulic |
| Shock tolerance | Poor | Excellent | Pneumatic |
| Temperature sensitivity | High | Low | Pneumatic |
| Typical applications | Presses, excavators, CNC | Packaging, robotics, tools | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Hydraulic Actuation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Electric Motor] --> A2[Hydraulic Pump]
    A2 --> A3[Pressure Relief Valve]
    A3 --> A4[Filter Unit]
    A4 --> A5[Directional Control Valve]
    A5 -->|High Pressure Oil| A6[Double Acting Cylinder]
    A5 -->|Return Oil| A7[Reservoir Tank]
    A7 -->|Suction Line| A2
    A6 -->|Mechanical Motion| A8[Load / Workpiece]
    A9[Pressure Gauge] -.Monitor.-> A5
    A10[Controller Signal] -.Command.-> A5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Electric motor → Pump pressurises oil → Relief valve limits max pressure → Filter cleans oil → DCV (directional control valve) directs flow to cylinder → Cylinder pushes load → Return oil back to tank.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Pneumatic Actuation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B2[Air Compressor]
    B2 --> B3[After Cooler]
    B3 --> B4[Air Receiver Tank]
    B4 --> B5[FRL: Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    B5 --> B6[3 by 2 Directional Control Valve]
    B6 -->|Pressurized Air| B7[Single / Double Acting Cylinder]
    B6 -->|Exhaust Air| B8[Silencer Muffler]
    B7 -->|Linear Motion| B9[Load]
    B10[Solenoid Coil] -.Electric Signal.-> B6
    B11[Pressure Switch] -.Feedback.-> B4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Ambient air → Compressor → Cooler → Storage tank → FRL (cleans, regulates pressure, lubricates) → Solenoid DCV → Cylinder → Load. Exhaust air silenced before release.

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree (KTU Exam Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0{Required Force Output}
    C0 -->|Greater than 50 kN| C1[Choose HYDRAULIC]
    C0 -->|Less than 5 kN| C2{Environment}
    C1 --> C1A[Servo valve for precision]
    C1 --> C1B[Proportional valve for moderate control]
    C2 -->|Clean / Flammable| C3[Choose PNEUMATIC]
    C2 -->|Heavy industrial| C1
    C3 --> C3A[Single acting for simple push]
    C3 --> C3B[Double acting for push pull]
    C3 --> C3C[Rotary vane motor for rotary]
```

## 4.4 Performance Trade-Off Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    D1[Force Output] ---|Hydraulic: Very High| D1A[HIGH]
    D1 ---|Pneumatic: Low| D1B[LOW]
    D2[Speed of Operation] ---|Hydraulic: Moderate| D2A[MED]
    D2 ---|Pneumatic: High| D2B[HIGH]
    D3[Precision Positioning] ---|Hydraulic: Excellent| D3A[EXCEL]
    D3 ---|Pneumatic: Poor| D3B[POOR]
    D4[Cost of Ownership] ---|Hydraulic: High| D4A[HIGH]
    D4 ---|Pneumatic: Low| D4B[LOW]
    D5[Cleanliness Safety] ---|Hydraulic: Moderate| D5A[MED]
    D5 ---|Pneumatic: Excellent| D5B[EXCEL]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks)

### **Q1. List any three advantages and three disadvantages of hydraulic actuators.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. High force and power density — pressures up to 700 bar give large thrust from compact cylinders.
2. Precise position and speed control using servo/proportional valves.
3. Self-lubricating oil ensures long service life and smooth motion.
4. Constant force throughout stroke (incompressible fluid).
5. Statically load-holding — no energy required to hold position.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Leakage of oil causes contamination and fire hazard.
2. High initial cost (pump, valves, filtration, hoses).
3. Requires bulky auxiliary equipment (reservoir, cooler, filter).
4. Contamination-sensitive — particles damage servo valves.
5. Noise during operation (75–95 dB).

*(Any 3 from each category fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. Why are pneumatic actuators preferred in food-processing industries?**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample]`

**Model Answer:**
Pneumatic actuators use **compressed air** as the working medium, which is:
1. **Clean and non-contaminating** — essential for food/pharma hygiene.
2. **Non-flammable** — safe near edible products.
3. **Cheap and easy to maintain** — cost-effective for repetitive tasks.
4. **Fast-acting** — ideal for high-speed packaging lines.
5. **Suitable for explosive/flammable atmospheres** (no electrical sparks needed at the actuator).

Hence pneumatic is preferred in food, dairy, and pharmaceutical industries.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for extend and retract forces.
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has two ports — port A (cap end) and port B (rod end). Pressurised oil entering port A pushes the piston outward (extension); oil entering port B pushes it inward (retraction).

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Port A - Cap End] -->|Pressurised Oil| B[Piston with Rod]
    B -->|Extends| C[Load]
    D[Port B - Rod End] -->|Pressurised Oil for retract| B
    E[Tank Return] -->|During Extension| D
```

**Derivation of Extend Force:**
Let $D$ = bore diameter, $d$ = rod diameter, $P$ = supply pressure.

The full piston face is exposed to pressure during extension.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{cap} &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\
F_{extend} &= P \times A_{cap} \\
F_{extend} &= P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation of Retract Force:**
During retraction, the effective area is reduced by the rod cross-section.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{ann} &= A_{cap} - A_{rod} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\
A_{ann} &= \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} \\
F_{retract} &= P \times A_{ann} \\
F_{retract} &= P \times \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example (often asked):**
For $D = 50 \text{ mm}$, $d = 25 \text{ mm}$, $P = 100 \text{ bar} = 10^7 \text{ Pa}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{cap} &= \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
F_{extend} &= 10^7 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} = 19630 \text{ N} \approx 19.6 \text{ kN} \\
A_{ann} &= \frac{\pi ((0.050)^2 - (0.025)^2)}{4} = 1.473 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
F_{retract} &= 10^7 \times 1.473 \times 10^{-3} = 14730 \text{ N} \approx 14.7 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b)** Compare hydraulic and pneumatic actuators based on force, speed, accuracy, cost, and application. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer Table:**

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| **Force** | Very high (up to 700 bar) | Low (6–10 bar typical) |
| **Speed** | Moderate (≤ 1 m/s) | High (≤ 3 m/s) |
| **Accuracy** | Excellent (μm with servo valves) | Poor (mm range) |
| **Cost** | High initial + maintenance | Low initial + maintenance |
| **Cleanliness** | Oil leaks | Clean air |
| **Fire safety** | Risk | Intrinsically safe |
| **Applications** | Excavators, presses, CNC, ABS brakes | Packaging, food, pharma, tools |

**Conclusion:** Hydraulic is preferred for heavy-duty precision work; pneumatic is preferred for fast, clean, low-force repetitive tasks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the components of a pneumatic system and state the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[Air Intake Filter] --> a2[Compressor]
    a2 --> a3[After Cooler]
    a3 --> a4[Receiver Tank]
    a4 --> a5[FRL Unit]
    a5 --> a6[Directional Control Valve]
    a6 --> a7[Actuator / Cylinder]
    a7 -->|Exhaust| a8[Silencer]
```

**Functions:**
1. **Air Intake Filter:** Removes dust and moisture from atmospheric air entering compressor.
2. **Compressor:** Converts mechanical energy into pneumatic energy by compressing air.
3. **After Cooler:** Cools hot compressed air to remove condensate and prevent downstream damage.
4. **Receiver Tank:** Stores compressed air to meet peak demand and stabilise pressure.
5. **FRL Unit:** Filter cleans air, Regulator sets working pressure, Lubricator adds oil mist for moving parts.
6. **Directional Control Valve:** Routes air to actuator ports based on control signal.
7. **Actuator/Cylinder:** Converts pneumatic energy into mechanical work.
8. **Silencer:** Reduces exhaust noise to acceptable levels (< 75 dB).

---

**(b)** Discuss any seven advantages and seven disadvantages of pneumatic actuators. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Seven advantages):**
1. Simple in design, low cost.
2. Clean, non-contaminating working medium.
3. Safe in explosive/flammable environments.
4. High operating speed.
5. Lightweight and compact.
6. Overload-tolerant (air cushions shock).
7. Wide operating temperature range.

**Model Answer (Seven disadvantages):**
1. Low force output due to low pressure.
2. Poor positioning accuracy (compressibility).
3. Cannot hold load statically without brake.
4. Requires clean, dry air (mandatory FRL).
5. High noise from exhaust.
6. Energy inefficient (~20% overall).
7. Limited stroke length; needs lubrication.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the bore-side vs rod-side areas** — students often write the *same* force for extension and retraction. Always subtract rod area for $F_{retract}$.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — $P$ must be in **Pa** (not bar) when using SI units. Conversion: 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa.
> 3. **Writing "hydraulic actuators are noisy"** as a disadvantage — this is **wrong**; pneumatics are noisier. Hydraulic is the *quieter* one.
> 4. **Listing "leakage" as a disadvantage for pneumatics** — air leaks don't pollute; only hydraulic oil leaks are problematic.
> 5. **Missing neat diagrams** in 7-mark sub-questions — minimum 1 block diagram is mandatory to score full marks.
> 6. **Failing to state applications** — KTU explicitly tests application knowledge for component identification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Actuator = energy converter that produces mechanical work from fluid/electrical signals.
- ✅ **Hydraulic = incompressible oil → high force, high accuracy, low compressibility.**
- ✅ **Pneumatic = compressible air → low force, high speed, low accuracy.**
- ✅ **Master formula:** $F = P \cdot A = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ (extension) and $F = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ (retraction).
- ✅ **Industrial hydraulic pressure:** 100 – 350 bar; **Industrial pneumatic pressure:** 6 bar (standard).
- ✅ **Hydraulic force : Pneumatic force ratio ≈ 25 – 35× at industrial pressures.**
- ✅ **Hydraulic noise: 75 – 95 dB | Pneumatic noise: 90 – 110 dB** (pneumatic is noisier).
- ✅ **Pneumatic = intrinsically safe** (no sparks, no flammable fluid).
- ✅ **Hydraulic is statically stable** (holds load without energy); **Pneumatic drifts** when supply is cut.
- ✅ **Hydraulic needs FRL; Pneumatic needs FRL + Silencer.**
- ✅ **Bulk modulus of oil ≈ 1.5 GPa** → incompressibility definition.
- ✅ **Applications snapshot:** Hydraulic → excavators, presses, CNC, ABS brakes, injection moulding, aircraft.
- ✅ **Applications snapshot:** Pneumatic → packaging, food, pharma, semiconductor, paint spray, dental drills, mine tools.
- ✅ **Energy efficiency:** Hydraulic ≈ 70–80% | Pneumatic ≈ 15–25% → pneumatic is wasteful.
- ✅ **Mnemonic — Hydraulic: HHHH-LLL** (High force, High accuracy, Heavy cost, Hold load, Leak-prone).
- ✅ **Mnemonic — Pneumatic: SCFS** (Simple, Clean, Fast, Safe).
- ✅ **Always draw block diagrams** for system explanation questions (Part B).
- ✅ **Always convert bar → Pa** before calculation: $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

---

*End of Notes — Module 2 / Hydraulic & Pneumatic Actuators*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Actuators: Electric Motors - DC Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric Actuators: Electric Motors - DC Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC Motor (Direct Current Motor):** A DC motor is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms direct current (DC) electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy (torque and angular displacement) based on the principle of interaction between a current-carrying conductor and a magnetic field, governed by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force Equation**.

In the context of **PEMET414 – Components of Intelligent Systems**, the DC motor is classified as a **Class-A Electro-Mechanical Actuator** that forms the *primary actuation stage* in robotic manipulators, CNC feed drives, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and prosthetic limb systems. It serves as the *muscle* of any intelligent mechatronic subsystem by converting controller-generated PWM signals into precise angular motion.

## 1.2 The KTU Working Definition of an Electric Actuator

> [!NOTE]
> **Electric Actuator:** A device that converts electrical energy (voltage/current signals) into a precisely controlled mechanical output (linear displacement, angular rotation, or force/torque). In intelligent systems, the actuator is the *final control element* in the signal chain: **Controller → Driver (Power Electronics) → Actuator → Mechanical Load**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Water Wheel

Imagine a **water wheel** in an old mill. The water (analogous to *electric current*) flows onto paddles (analogous to *armature conductors*) attached to a circular wheel (the *rotor*). The wheel sits inside a strong magnetic field created by stationary magnets on the sides (the *field poles*). As water strikes the paddles, the wheel rotates, grinding grain (doing *mechanical work*).

In a DC motor:
- The **stationary magnets** (field poles) produce a constant magnetic flux $\Phi$.
- The **current-carrying armature conductors** experience a force $F = BIL$ (Lorentz force).
- The **commutator and brush assembly** acts like a clever *traffic controller* that reverses the current direction every half rotation, ensuring the torque is always unidirectional — the wheel *always spins the same way*.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants in DC Motor Analysis:**
> - **Back-EMF Constant** $K_e$ = **(V·s/rad)** — relates flux to induced voltage
> - **Motor Torque Constant** $K_t$ = **(N·m/A)** — relates current to mechanical torque
> - **In SI units:** $K_e = K_t$ (numerically equal)
> - **Standard supply voltages:** 12 V, 24 V, 48 V, 110 V, 220 V DC
> - **Pole configurations:** $P = 2$ (most common in small actuators), 4, 6

## 1.5 Role of DC Motor in Intelligent Systems (Contextual Hook)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DC Motors for Intelligent Systems?**
> - **High starting torque** — critical for robotic joint initialization
> - **Excellent speed controllability** over a wide range (0 to base speed) using simple **PWM duty-cycle modulation**
> - **Linear torque-speed characteristics** that simplify PID controller design
> - **Bidirectional control** possible with an **H-Bridge driver** (e.g., L298N, BTS7960)
> - **Compact size-to-power ratio** for embedded applications

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a DC Motor

The DC motor consists of two principal subassemblies:

### A. Stator (Stationary Part) — *The Magnetic Frame*
- **Yoke:** Outer cylindrical cast-iron/steel frame that provides mechanical support and carries the magnetic flux.
- **Pole Core & Pole Shoe:** Laminated silicon-steel projections that hold the field windings and spread the flux uniformly across the air gap.
- **Field Windings:** Copper coils wound around the pole cores. Energized by DC excitation to produce the main working flux $\Phi$.

### B. Rotor (Rotating Part) — *The Armature*
- **Armature Core:** Slotted cylindrical laminated silicon-steel structure.
- **Armature Winding:** Closed-loop copper conductors embedded in the slots, where EMF is induced and torque-producing current flows.
- **Commutator:** A split-ring assembly of copper segments insulated by mica; mechanically rectifies the induced AC into unidirectional brush-side voltage.
- **Shaft & Bearings:** Mechanical support for the rotating assembly.

### C. Brushes
Carbon or graphite blocks sliding on the commutator; they provide the *electrical interface* between the stationary external circuit and the rotating armature.

## 2.2 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind the Rotation

The operation rests on **two fundamental electromagnetic laws**:

> **Law 1 — Lorentz Force:** A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force.
> $$\vec{F} = I \cdot (\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$$

> **Law 2 — Faraday's Induction:** A conductor moving in a magnetic field has an EMF induced across it.
> $$e = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

**Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic):**

1. **Step 1 — Flux Generation:** DC excitation of the field winding creates a uniform radial magnetic field $\Phi$ in the air gap.
2. **Step 2 — Current Injection:** External DC supply feeds current $I_a$ into the armature winding through the brush-commutator assembly.
3. **Step 3 — Force Production:** Each conductor on the armature periphery experiences a tangential Lorentz force $F = BIL$ perpendicular to both current and flux.
4. **Step 4 — Torque Generation:** These tangential forces act at radius $r$ (rotor radius), summing to produce a net electromagnetic torque $T_e$.
5. **Step 5 — Rotation:** The torque overcomes load friction and inertia, accelerating the rotor to a steady-state speed $N$.
6. **Step 6 — Back-EMF Opposition:** As the conductor cuts flux, a counter-EMF $E_b$ is induced (Lenz's Law) that opposes the applied voltage.
7. **Step 7 — Commutation:** The commutator periodically reverses the current in each conductor as it crosses the brush axis, ensuring the torque always acts in the *same rotational direction*.

## 2.3 Types of DC Motors (Field Excitation Classification)

| Motor Type | Field Connection | Flux Behavior | Speed Regulation | Typical Intelligent System Use |
|------------|----------------|---------------|------------------|--------------------------------|
| **DC Shunt** | Field winding **parallel** to armature | Nearly constant $\Phi$ | Excellent (good) | CNC feed drives, conveyor belts, recorders |
| **DC Series** | Field winding **series** with armature | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (variable) | Poor (high speed at no load) | Traction systems, cranes, hoists, robotic grippers (high-torque, low-speed) |
| **DC Compound (Cumulative)** | Both shunt + series aiding | Partially variable | Moderate | Presses, elevators, heavy-load actuators |
| **DC Compound (Differential)** | Shunt + series opposing | Reduced with load | Poor (rising speed) | Rarely used; special applications |
| **PMDC (Permanent Magnet)** | Permanent magnets replace field | Fixed $\Phi$ | Excellent | Small DC servo motors, toy robots, fans |
| **Brushless DC (BLDC)** | Electronic commutation | Trapezoidal $\Phi$ | Excellent | Drones, EV traction, hard drives, robotic joints |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (The "Exam-Saver" Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are mathematically rigorous and verified. Use $\vert$ instead of \vert in exam scripts to denote absolute value where needed.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | **Back-EMF (Induced EMF)** | $E_b = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}$ | $P$ = poles, $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $N$ = speed (rpm), $Z$ = conductors, $A$ = parallel paths | Volts (V) |
| 2 | **Armature EMF Constant** | $E_b = K_e\,\Phi\,N$ | $K_e$ = EMF constant = $\dfrac{P\,Z}{60\,A}$ | V per (Wb·rpm) |
| 3 | **Electromagnetic Torque** | $T_e = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi\,A}$ | $T_e$ = developed torque, $I_a$ = armature current | N·m |
| 4 | **Torque in SI form** | $T_e = K_t\,\Phi\,I_a$ | $K_t$ = torque constant | N·m |
| 5 | **Armature Voltage Equation (KVL)** | $V = E_b + I_a\,R_a$ | $V$ = supply voltage, $R_a$ = armature resistance | V |
| 6 | **No-Load Speed** | $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_e\,\Phi}$ | At $T = 0$ (ideal) | rpm |
| 7 | **Mechanical Power Developed** | $P_m = E_b\,I_a = T_e\,\omega$ | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | W |
| 8 | **Input Electrical Power** | $P_{in} = V\,I_a$ | (neglecting field circuit) | W |
| 9 | **Angular Velocity** | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi\,N}{60}$ | rad/s conversion from rpm | rad/s |
| 10 | **Speed Regulation** | $\%\,SR = \dfrac{N_0 - N_{fl}}{N_{fl}} \times 100$ | $N_{fl}$ = full-load speed | % |
| 11 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T_e\,\omega}{V\,I_a}$ | Less losses: copper + iron + friction + windage | % |
| 12 | **Shunt Motor Field Current** | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | $R_{sh}$ = shunt field resistance | A |
| 13 | **Series Motor Flux Relation** | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (pre-saturation) | Below magnetic saturation | Wb |
| 14 | **Series Motor Speed** | $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T_e}}$ (after saturation) | Used in traction design | rpm |
| 15 | **Shunt Motor Speed** | $N \approx \text{constant}$ (small drop) | Nearly constant flux | rpm |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where This Knowledge is Applied

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application Matrix:**
> - **Robotics:** DC servo motors in joint actuators with optical encoders for closed-loop position control.
> - **Automotive:** PMDC motors in windshield wipers, power windows, seat adjusters.
> - **Aerospace:** BLDC motors in flight control surface actuators, drone propulsion.
> - **Medical:** Miniature DC motors in insulin pumps, surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci system).
> - **Manufacturing:** Compound DC motors in rolling mills, printing presses.
> - **Consumer Electronics:** PMDC motors in hard disk drives, CD/DVD drives, cooling fans.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — The Back-EMF Equation ($E_b$)

> **Goal:** To derive the relationship between the induced EMF, the speed, and the flux of a DC motor.

**Step 1: Flux Cut Per Revolution by One Conductor**

Consider one armature conductor. In one complete revolution, it sweeps through an area equal to the *circumferential area* of the rotor.

Area swept per revolution $= \pi D L$

where $D$ is the armature diameter and $L$ is the axial length of the conductor.

**Step 2: Flux Density at the Conductor's Location**

The total flux per pole is $\Phi$ Wb, distributed over the pole pitch. The flux density is:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{\text{area per pole}}$$

For simplicity, the average flux density in the air gap gives the EMF per conductor as:

$$\text{EMF per conductor per revolution} = B \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

**Step 3: Total Flux Cut Per Revolution (All Conductors)**

For a wave-wound armature, the number of parallel paths $A = 2$; for a lap-wound armature, $A = P$. In general, the EMF is generated across the **conductors in series per parallel path**:

$$\text{Conductors in series per path} = \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 4: EMF Generated in One Parallel Path Per Revolution**

$$\text{EMF per path} = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot B \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

**Step 5: Relate to Flux per Pole $\Phi$**

Since $B \cdot (\text{pole area}) = \Phi$ and the pole arc roughly equals the pole pitch $Y = \dfrac{\pi D}{P}$, we get:

$$B \cdot \pi D \cdot L = P \cdot \Phi$$

**Step 6: Substituting Back**

$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot P \cdot \Phi \quad \text{(per revolution)}$$

**Step 7: Convert Revolutions to Seconds**

If the rotor makes $N$ revolutions per minute, then per second it makes $\dfrac{N}{60}$ revolutions.

$$\boxed{\,E_b = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}\,} \quad \text{(Volts)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — The Electromagnetic Torque Equation ($T_e$)

> **Goal:** To derive the mechanical torque developed by the armature.

**Step 1: Force on a Single Conductor**

From the Lorentz force law:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$

where $I$ is the current in one conductor.

**Step 2: Current Per Conductor**

For an armature carrying total current $I_a$ distributed across $A$ parallel paths:

$$I_{\text{conductor}} = \frac{I_a}{A}$$

**Step 3: Force Per Conductor**

$$F_{\text{per conductor}} = B \cdot L \cdot \frac{I_a}{A}$$

**Step 4: Torque Per Conductor**

The conductor is located at radius $r = \dfrac{D}{2}$ from the shaft axis:

$$T_{\text{per conductor}} = F \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{B \cdot L \cdot I_a \cdot D}{2\,A}$$

**Step 5: Total Torque (All $Z$ Conductors)**

$$T_e = Z \cdot \frac{B \cdot L \cdot I_a \cdot D}{2\,A}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $B \cdot \pi D \cdot L = P\,\Phi$**

$$T_e = \frac{Z \cdot P \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}{2\pi A}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_e = \frac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi\,A}\,} \quad \text{(N·m)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Power Equivalence Proof ($P_m = E_b I_a = T_e \omega$)

**Step 1: Start with Back-EMF**

$$E_b = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}$$

**Step 2: Multiply by $I_a$**

$$E_b \cdot I_a = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z\,I_a}{60\,A}$$

**Step 3: Start with Torque Expression**

$$T_e = \frac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi A}$$

**Step 4: Multiply by Angular Velocity**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$T_e \cdot \omega = \frac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi A} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z\,I_a}{60\,A}$$

**Step 5: Compare Both Expressions**

Both expressions yield the **identical mathematical form**, hence:

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{mech}} = E_b \cdot I_a = T_e \cdot \omega\,}$$

This is a **conservation of energy** statement — electrical power converted to mechanical power.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem Statement:** A **4-pole, 220 V DC shunt motor** has **540 lap-connected conductors**. The armature resistance is $R_a = 0.1\;\Omega$ and the field resistance is $R_{sh} = 110\;\Omega$. The flux per pole is $\Phi = 30\;\text{mWb} = 0.03\;\text{Wb}$. The motor draws a line current of $52\;\text{A}$. Calculate: **(a)** the back-EMF, **(b)** the armature current, **(c)** the developed torque, and **(d)** the speed in rpm.

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$, $V = 220\;\text{V}$, $Z = 540$
- Lap winding: $A = P = 4$
- $R_a = 0.1\;\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110\;\Omega$, $\Phi = 0.03\;\text{Wb}$, $I_L = 52\;\text{A}$

**Solution (a): Field Current & Armature Current**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2\;\text{A}$$

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 52 - 2 = 50\;\text{A}$$

**Solution (b): Back-EMF**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (50)(0.1) = 220 - 5 = 215\;\text{V}$$

**Solution (c): Speed in rpm**

$$E_b = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}$$

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P\,\Phi\,Z} = \frac{215 \times 60 \times 4}{4 \times 0.03 \times 540}$$

$$N = \frac{51{,}600}{64.8} = 796.296\;\text{rpm} \approx 796\;\text{rpm}$$

**Solution (d): Developed Torque**

$$T_e = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega} = \frac{215 \times 50}{\frac{2\pi \times 796}{60}} = \frac{10{,}750}{83.36} = 128.96\;\text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_e \approx 129\;\text{N·m}\,}$$

**Cross-Check (Direct Formula):**

$$T_e = \frac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.03 \times 540 \times 50}{2\pi \times 4} = \frac{3240}{25.133} = 128.93\;\text{N·m} \;\checkmark$$

## 3.5 Speed-Torque Characteristic Derivations (Per Motor Type)

### Shunt Motor
Since $\Phi$ is essentially constant (because $V$ is constant and $R_{sh}$ is constant):

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e \Phi} \approx \text{constant} - \frac{R_a}{K_e^2 \Phi^2} T_e$$

This is a **gently drooping straight line** with negative slope $\dfrac{R_a}{(K_e \Phi)^2}$.

### Series Motor (Below Saturation)
Since $\Phi = k \cdot I_a$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{K_e k I_a} = \frac{V}{K_e k I_a} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{K_e k}$$

Combined with $T_e = K_t k I_a^2$, eliminating $I_a$:

$$\boxed{\,N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_e}}\,}$$

This is a **hyperbolic (inverse-square-root) curve** — high torque at low speed, runaway speed at light loads.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — DC Motor in an Intelligent Control System

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a closed-loop DC motor position/speed control system as used in robotic joints, CNC servos, and AGV drives.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Input: Desired Position or Speed] --> B[Microcontroller or DSP Controller]
    B --> C[PWM Signal Generator]
    C --> D[H-Bridge Motor Driver L298N or BTS7960]
    D --> E[DC Motor Actuator]
    E --> F[Mechanical Load: Robot Joint, Wheel, Conveyor]
    E --> G[Rotary Encoder or Tachogenerator]
    G --> H[Feedback Signal: Actual Position or Speed]
    H --> I[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    I --> B
    J[DC Power Supply: 12V or 24V Battery] --> D
    J --> B
    K[Operator Interface: Keypad or HMI] --> B

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Functional Block — Internal Construction of a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Supply V] --> B[Brush Assembly]
    B --> C[Commutator Segments]
    C --> D[Armature Winding Z Conductors]
    D --> E[Air Gap Magnetic Field Phi]
    E --> F[Field Winding Shunt or Series]
    F --> A
```

## 4.4 State Diagram — DC Motor Operation Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> Accelerating: Apply Voltage V
    Accelerating --> Running: Reaches Steady State
    Running --> Loaded: External Torque Applied
    Running --> Unloaded: Load Removed
    Loaded --> Running: Torque Balanced
    Unloaded --> Running: Speed Stabilized
    Running --> Braking: Reverse Voltage or Regenerative
    Braking --> Standby: Speed = 0
    Standby --> [*]

    note right of Accelerating: Current is high Eb grows as N increases
    note right of Running: Eb approx V minus Ia Ra
    note right of Braking: Motor acts as generator
```
