# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Modeled after the KTU 2024 Part A pattern — concise, definition/derivation/explanation-based, 2–3 sentence answers expected.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Back-EMF in a DC Motor. Why is it called a "back" EMF?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Back-EMF, denoted $E_b$, is the **counter-electromotive force induced in the armature conductors** as they rotate through the magnetic field produced by the field poles. It is governed by Faraday's law:

$$E_b = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}$$

It is called "back" EMF because, by **Lenz's Law**, its polarity is such that it **opposes the applied supply voltage**, effectively acting like a *brake* on the current flow. The voltage equation of the armature circuit is therefore:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

This counter-EMF is what naturally limits the armature current and stabilizes the motor at a steady-state speed.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between DC Shunt and DC Series Motors (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Field Connection** | Field winding in **parallel** with armature | Field winding in **series** with armature |
| **Flux** | $\Phi$ is **constant** (independent of load) | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (varies with load) |
| **Speed Regulation** | **Good** — nearly constant speed | **Poor** — speed varies widely with load |
| **Starting Torque** | **Moderate** (1.5× to 2× full-load) | **Very high** (5× to 8× full-load) |
| **Application** | Lathes, fans, CNC, conveyors | Cranes, hoists, electric traction |
| **No-Load Behavior** | Safe — speed remains within limits | **Dangerous** — tends to run away |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 ESE pattern for Part B provides an **internal choice** within a module. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — both carry 14 marks and have sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a DC motor**. Derive the **back-EMF equation**.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A **220 V, 4-pole DC shunt motor** has **500 lap-wound conductors** and a **field winding resistance of 110 Ω**. The armature resistance is **0.2 Ω**. The flux per pole is **25 mWb**. On full load, the motor draws an armature current of **40 A**. Calculate:
> (i) The back-EMF
> (ii) The speed in rpm
> (iii) The developed torque
> (iv) The shaft torque if mechanical and iron losses total 600 W.

---

#### 📝 **Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Construction (4 Marks):**

A DC motor consists of two main parts:

1. **Stator (Yoke + Poles + Field Windings):**
   - **Yoke:** Cast-iron/steel frame; provides mechanical protection and a low-reluctance flux path.
   - **Pole Core & Shoe:** Laminated silicon-steel; carries the field winding and shapes the flux distribution.
   - **Field Winding:** Copper coils on pole cores; carries the excitation current to produce $\Phi$.

2. **Rotor (Armature Core + Winding + Commutator + Shaft):**
   - **Armature Core:** Slotted, laminated silicon-steel cylinder.
   - **Armature Winding:** Insulated copper conductors placed in slots; carries the working current $I_a$.
   - **Commutator:** Hard-drawn copper segments insulated by mica; acts as a mechanical rectifier.
   - **Brushes:** Carbon/graphite blocks; provide sliding electrical contact to the commutator.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**

When DC supply is given to the armature, current $I_a$ flows through the conductors. The field winding produces a uniform radial flux $\Phi$. Each conductor experiences a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$ tangential to the rotor. This produces a torque $T_e = F \times r$ per conductor. The commutator reverses the current direction in each conductor as it passes the brush axis, ensuring **unidirectional rotation**.

**Derivation of Back-EMF (1 Mark):**

$$E_b = \frac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A} \quad \text{Volts}$$

---

#### 📝 **Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $P = 4$, $Z = 500$, $R_{sh} = 110\;\Omega$, $R_a = 0.2\;\Omega$, $\Phi = 0.025$ Wb, $I_a = 40$ A, lap winding so $A = P = 4$.

**(i) Back-EMF:**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (40)(0.2) = 220 - 8 = 212\;\text{V}$$

**(ii) Speed in rpm:**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P\,\Phi\,Z} = \frac{212 \times 60 \times 4}{4 \times 0.025 \times 500} = \frac{50{,}880}{50} = 1017.6\;\text{rpm}$$

**(iii) Developed (Electromagnetic) Torque:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1017.6}{60} = 106.58\;\text{rad/s}$$

$$T_e = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega} = \frac{212 \times 40}{106.58} = 79.57\;\text{N·m}$$

**(iv) Shaft Torque (after subtracting mechanical + iron losses of 600 W):**

$$P_{\text{loss\_mech+iron}} = 600\;\text{W}$$

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = T_e \cdot \omega - P_{\text{loss}} = (79.57)(106.58) - 600 = 8481 - 600 = 7881\;\text{W}$$

$$T_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\omega} = \frac{7881}{106.58} = 73.94\;\text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{\text{shaft}} \approx 73.94\;\text{N·m}\,}$$

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **classification of DC motors** based on field excitation with neat circuit diagrams. Compare the **speed-torque characteristics** of shunt, series, and compound motors.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A **PMDC motor** has the following nameplate data: **24 V, 10 A, 3000 rpm**. The armature resistance is **0.5 Ω**. Determine:
> (i) The back-EMF constant $K_e$ and torque constant $K_t$
> (ii) The no-load speed and current
> (iii) The starting current at the instant of starting (assume $E_b = 0$)
> (iv) The developed torque at full load

---

#### 📝 **Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Classification of DC Motors (3 Marks):**

DC motors are classified into **four** major types based on field winding connection:

1. **DC Shunt Motor:** Field winding connected in **parallel** with the armature.
2. **DC Series Motor:** Field winding connected in **series** with the armature.
3. **Cumulative Compound Motor:** Both shunt and series fields are present, with series aiding the shunt flux.
4. **Differential Compound Motor:** Series field *opposes* the shunt flux (rarely used).

**Circuit Diagram Description (1 Mark):** Show $V$ → armature ($R_a$, $E_b$), with field winding $R_{sh}$ in parallel (shunt case); $R_{se}$ in series with armature (series case).

**Speed-Torque Characteristics Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Motor Type | Speed Characteristic | Torque Characteristic | Graph Shape |
|------------|----------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Shunt** | Nearly constant (slight linear drop) | Linearly increasing with $I_a$ | **Gently drooping straight line** |
| **Series** | Inversely proportional to $\sqrt{T_e}$ | Quadratically increasing with $I_a$ | **Hyperbolic curve** |
| **Cumulative Compound** | Moderate droop (between shunt and series) | Better starting than shunt, less runaway than series | **Intermediate droop** |
| **Differential Compound** | Rising with load (unstable) | Decreasing with load | **Rising curve** (unstable) |

**Key Insight:** Shunt motors offer **best speed regulation**; series motors offer **best starting torque**; cumulative compound offers a **balanced compromise** — preferred in robotics and elevator applications.

---

#### 📝 **Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $V = 24$ V, $I_{rated} = 10$ A, $N_{rated} = 3000$ rpm, $R_a = 0.5\;\Omega$.

**Step 1: Back-EMF at rated load**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 24 - (10)(0.5) = 24 - 5 = 19\;\text{V}$$

**(i) Back-EMF Constant $K_e$ and Torque Constant $K_t$**

$$K_e = \frac{E_b}{N} = \frac{19}{3000} = 6.333 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V/rpm}$$

Or in SI units (using $\omega$):

$$\omega_{rated} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 314.16\;\text{rad/s}$$

$$K_e' = \frac{E_b}{\omega} = \frac{19}{314.16} = 0.06048\;\text{V·s/rad}$$

By SI convention, $K_t = K_e'$ numerically:

$$\boxed{\,K_e' = K_t = 0.0605\;\text{V·s/rad = N·m/A}\,}$$

**(ii) No-Load Speed and Current**

Assuming no-load friction is negligible and $I_{a0} \approx 0$:

$$E_{b0} \approx V = 24\;\text{V}$$

$$N_0 = \frac{E_{b0}}{K_e} = \frac{24}{6.333 \times 10^{-3}} = 3789.5\;\text{rpm}$$

For a more realistic estimate, assume $I_{a0} = 0.5$ A (to overcome friction):

$$E_{b0} = 24 - (0.5)(0.5) = 23.75\;\text{V}$$

$$N_0 = \frac{23.75}{6.333 \times 10^{-3}} = 3750\;\text{rpm (approx)}$$

**(iii) Starting Current (at $t = 0$, $E_b = 0$, rotor stationary):**

$$I_{\text{start}} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{24}{0.5} = 48\;\text{A}$$

This is **4.8× the rated current** — illustrating why a *soft-starter* or *PWM ramp* is essential in intelligent system design.

**(iv) Developed Torque at Full Load:**

$$T_e = K_t \cdot I_a = 0.06048 \times 10 = 0.6048\;\text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_e \approx 0.605\;\text{N·m}\,}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $A$ (parallel paths) with $P$ (poles):** For **lap winding** $A = P$; for **wave winding** $A = 2$. Many students write $A = 2$ for *all* DC motors — this is wrong and costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract $I_{sh}$ from $I_L$ in shunt motors:** $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$, NOT $I_L$. A classic trap.
>
> 3. **Mixing $N$ in rpm and $\omega$ in rad/s:** When using $P = T\omega$, you MUST use $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$. Many students directly use $N$ and lose the unit conversion mark.
>
> 4. **Sign convention for Back-EMF:** The KVL equation is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (not $V + E_b$). Drawing the wrong polarity direction will lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Series motor speed at no-load:** Students often say "speed decreases" — actually, speed **increases dangerously** (tendency to run away). Examiners penalize this.
>
> 6. **Units in final answer:** Always mention units (N·m for torque, rpm for speed, W for power, V for EMF). An answer without units is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for DC Motors:**

- **Operating Principle:** Lorentz force on current-carrying conductors in a magnetic field produces torque.
- **Key Equations:**
  - $E_b = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}$
  - $T_e = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi A}$
  - $V = E_b + I_a R_a$
  - $P_{\text{mech}} = E_b I_a = T_e \omega$
- **Back-EMF** is the heart of self-regulation: as speed rises, $E_b$ rises, reducing $I_a$ and limiting torque.
- **Types:** Shunt (constant flux), Series (variable flux), Compound (mixed), PMDC, BLDC.
- **Shunt Motor:** $\Phi = $ const → speed ≈ const (best regulation).
- **Series Motor:** $\Phi \propto I_a$ → high starting torque, but **dangerous at no load**.
- **Compound (Cumulative):** Combines benefits of both — used in elevators, presses.
- **Speed Control Methods:** (1) Field flux variation (above base speed), (2) Armature voltage variation (below base speed), (3) Series resistance insertion.
- **Power Flow:** Electrical input ($VI_a$) → Copper loss ($I_a^2 R_a$) → Mechanical developed power ($E_b I_a$) → Iron + friction losses → Shaft output power.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{V I_a} \times 100\%$ — typically 75–90% for medium-sized motors.
- **In Intelligent Systems:** DC motors are *interfaced* via **H-bridge drivers** with **PWM control**, often paired with **optical encoders** for closed-loop PID control.
- **Modern Trend:** BLDC motors are replacing brushed DC motors in drones, EVs, and robotic joints due to higher efficiency and no brush wear.

---

> **END OF NOTES — KTU PEMET414 | Module 2 | Electric Actuators: DC Motors**

topics.title: Stepper Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stepper Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepper Motor (Definition as per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A *stepper motor* is a **brushless, synchronous electric motor** that converts a series of discrete electrical pulses (input commands) into **discrete angular mechanical movements** (steps). Each input pulse advances the rotor by a precise, fixed angular increment called the **step angle (β)**, without requiring any feedback sensor for position control. It is therefore a quintessential **open-loop actuator** in intelligent motion-control systems.

Key identifiers of a stepper motor within the KTU intelligent-systems context:
- **Electromechanical transducer** (electrical pulse → mechanical rotation).
- Operates in **discrete incremental steps** — not continuous rotation.
- Synchronism is maintained between stator field rotation and rotor position.
- Position is **digitally determined** by the number of pulses applied.
- Speed is **proportional to pulse frequency**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Numbered Revolving Door":**
> Imagine a hotel revolving door with **4 glass panels** locked in place. Every time a bellhop (the controller) presses a button, the door unlocks, rotates by exactly **one panel-width (90°)**, and locks again. The door NEVER slips, NEVER misses, NEVER rotates halfway. If the bellhop presses the button 5 times, the door rotates **5 × 90° = 450°**. A stepper motor behaves identically: every electrical pulse is a "button press," and the rotor (the door) rotates by exactly one **step angle (β)** per pulse, then locks (holds) at that position.

- The **step angle (β)** is the "panel width" of the door.
- The **number of pulses** is how many times the bellhop pressed.
- The **locking action** after each step is called **holding torque**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Industry Constants / Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Holding Torque (Tₕ):** The maximum torque the motor can exert while stationary and energized; units: **N·m** (Newton-meter).
> - **Step Angle (β):** Common values are **1.8°** (most popular, 200 steps/rev), **0.9°** (400 steps/rev), **7.5°** (48 steps/rev), **15°** (24 steps/rev).
> - **Step Resolution (Steps per Revolution):** N = **360° / β**.
> - **Pull-out Torque:** Max torque at a given pulse rate before losing synchronism.
> - **Pull-in Torque:** Max torque at which the motor can start/stop without losing step.

## 1.4 Why a Stepper Motor is "Intelligent"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Components of Intelligent Systems Perspective:**
> A stepper motor is a *smart actuator* because it embeds intelligence through:
> 1. **Digital Command Interpretation:** Pulses = digital instructions.
> 2. **Self-Locking Geometry:** Detent torque (PM & hybrid types) holds position even after power-off (in PM type).
> 3. **No External Feedback Required:** Position is *implicit* in pulse count, simplifying controller design.
> 4. **Microcontroller/Microprocessor Compatibility:** Easily driven by Arduino, PLC, DSP, FPGA — making it the **workhorse of robotics, CNC, and 3D printers** — all intelligent-system domains.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — "How Does It Think?"

A stepper motor is governed by the **fundamental electromagnetic principle**: *"A ferromagnetic rotor always tries to align itself with the minimum reluctance path of the stator magnetic field."* When the stator winding is energized, it creates a magnetic flux; the rotor (with permanent magnets or salient poles) is mechanically pulled/torqued to align with this flux. Sequentially switching stator phases rotates the stator field, and the rotor faithfully follows.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Follow-the-Leader" Principle:**
> 1. Controller sends a **pulse** to phase A → stator field points North at A.
> 2. Rotor North-pole swings to align with phase A's South (minimum reluctance).
> 3. Controller de-energizes A and energizes B → rotor swings to phase B's position.
> 4. This sequence repeats: A → B → C → D → A… producing a discrete **step-by-step rotation**.

## 2.2 Classification of Stepper Motors (KTU Syllabus — Must Memorize)

| **Type** | **Full Form** | **Construction** | **Step Angle (Typical)** | **Holding at Rest** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **VR** | Variable Reluctance | Soft-iron toothed rotor, no magnets | **7.5° – 15°** | No detent (unenergized) |
| **PM** | Permanent Magnet | Permanent-magnet rotor (axially or radially magnetized) | **45° – 90°** | Yes (detent torque) |
| **HB / Hybrid** | Hybrid (PM + VR combined) | Toothed rotor with axially magnetized PM between two sections, polarized opposite | **0.9° – 5°** (typically **1.8°**) | Yes (high detent) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Stepper Motor is the INDUSTRY DOMINANT type** (used in 99% of 3D printers, CNC, robotics). KTU examiners love asking comparative questions on VR vs PM vs Hybrid — know the construction diagram and the reason for the small step angle in hybrid type (stator AND rotor are both toothed → small pitch gives fine resolution).

## 2.3 Step Angle — The Core Formula

The **step angle (β)** is the angular displacement produced per pulse:

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot \left(\frac{N_r}{2}\right)} \quad \text{(for Hybrid / PM multi-stack types)}$$

A simpler, universally-used formula:

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r} \quad \text{or} \quad N_{steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{\beta}$$

Where:
- **m** = number of stator phase pairs (stacks)
- **Nᵣ** = number of rotor teeth
- **Nₛ** = number of stator teeth
- **β** = step angle in degrees

For a typical **1.8° hybrid stepper**:
- N = 360°/1.8° = **200 steps per revolution**.
- With **microstepping (1/16)**: effective resolution = 3200 steps/rev → 0.1125°/step.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formula Bank for KTU Board Exams:**

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step Angle | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_{steps}}$ | degrees (°) | N_steps = steps per revolution |
| 2 | Steps per Revolution | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$ | dimensionless | Inverse of step angle |
| 3 | Rotor Speed | $n = f \cdot \beta$ | rev/s | f = pulse rate in pulses/sec |
| 4 | Rotor Speed (RPM) | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{360°}$ | RPM | Convert rev/s to rev/min |
| 5 | Shaft Frequency | $f_{shaft} = \dfrac{f}{N_{steps}}$ | rev/s | Mechanical rotation frequency |
| 6 | Holding Torque | $T_h$ (manufacturer spec) | N·m | Torque when energized, stationary |
| 7 | Resolution (Hybrid) | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot (N_r/2)}$ | degrees | N_s stator teeth, N_r rotor teeth |
| 8 | Number of Stack Pairs | $m$ | — | Multi-stack hybrid: m = 2 to 5 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **3D Printers (Prusa, Ender, Bambu):** NEMA 17 hybrid steppers for X/Y/Z axis motion.
- **CNC Routers / Lathes:** NEMA 23 / NEMA 34 for high-torque linear actuation.
- **Robotic Arms / Pick-and-Place:** Wrist & finger joint actuation.
- **Camera Gimbals:** Smooth, vibration-free positioning.
- **Disk Drives & Printers:** Head carriage positioning (historical).
- **Medical Equipment:** Syringe pumps, automated analyzers (precision + safety).

## 2.6 Modes of Excitation (Driven by Stepper Driver ICs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Canonical Excitation Modes (High-Yield for KTU 14-Mark Question):**
>
> 1. **Wave / One-Phase-On (Full Step):** Only one phase energized at a time. Sequence: A → B → C → D. Lowest torque, lowest heat.
> 2. **Full-Step / Two-Phase-On:** Two adjacent phases energized simultaneously. Sequence: AB → BC → CD → DA. **Highest torque**.
> 3. **Half-Step (8-step sequence):** Alternates wave and two-phase-on. Doubles resolution. Sequence: A → AB → B → BC → C → CD → D → DA.
> 4. **Microstepping (1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32):** Phases driven by sinusoidal currents. Smoothest motion, finest resolution.

## 2.7 The Stepper as an Open-Loop Intelligent Component

- **Open-loop:** No encoder, no feedback.
- **Position = f(number of pulses).** Loss of synchronism (load > pull-out torque) is **NOT detected** — this is the **Achilles' heel** of stepper systems and a common KTU question.
- For high-reliability systems, a hybrid open-loop + periodic-error-correction scheme is used.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Pulse Rate and Shaft Speed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Premise:** Each electrical pulse causes the rotor to rotate by exactly **one step angle (β)**.

**Step 1 — Define the input:**
Let **f** be the pulse rate (in pulses per second, Hz). This means the controller delivers **f** pulses every second.

**Step 2 — Pulses per revolution:**
To complete **one full revolution (360°)**, the controller must deliver:

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360°}{\beta}$$

**Step 3 — Time to complete one revolution:**
Since pulses arrive at rate f, the time to deliver N_steps pulses is:

$$T_{rev} = \frac{N_{steps}}{f} = \frac{360°}{f \cdot \beta}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to rev/s (mechanical frequency):**

$$f_{shaft} = \frac{1}{T_{rev}} = \frac{f \cdot \beta}{360°} \quad [\text{rev/s}]$$

**Step 5 — Convert to RPM (revolutions per minute):**

$$N_{rpm} = f_{shaft} \times 60 = \frac{f \cdot \beta \cdot 60}{360°} = \frac{f \cdot \beta}{6°}$$

Final boxed formula:

$$\boxed{N_{rpm} = \frac{f \cdot \beta}{6°}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (Typical 7-mark KTU style):**
> A hybrid stepper motor has 200 rotor teeth and 4 stator phase pairs (stacks). The controller delivers pulses at 2000 Hz. Calculate:
> (a) Step angle β in degrees.
> (b) Shaft speed in RPM.
> (c) Number of steps required to rotate the shaft by 75°.

---

### (a) Step Angle Calculation

**Given:** N_r = 200 rotor teeth, m = 4 stacks.

Using the hybrid stepper formula:

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{4 \times 200} = \frac{360°}{800}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = 0.45°}$$

---

### (b) Shaft Speed in RPM

**Given:** f = 2000 Hz, β = 0.45°.

Using the derived formula:

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{f \cdot \beta}{6°}$$

Substituting:

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{2000 \times 0.45}{6}$$

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{900}{6}$$

$$\boxed{N_{rpm} = 150 \text{ RPM}}$$

---

### (c) Number of Steps for 75° Rotation

**Given:** Required shaft rotation = 75°.

$$N_{steps} = \frac{\text{Angle desired}}{\beta} = \frac{75°}{0.45°}$$

$$\boxed{N_{steps} = 166.67 \approx 167 \text{ pulses}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** 75° is not an integer multiple of 0.45°, so we round up to 167 pulses. The actual angle after 167 pulses is 167 × 0.45° = 75.15° — a small quantization error of **0.15°** inherent to stepper systems. Microstepping can drastically reduce this error.

---

## 3.3 Torque–Speed Characteristic (Qualitative Derivation)

A stepper motor's **torque decreases as pulse rate increases** — the opposite of a DC motor. Why?

**Reasoning chain:**
1. At low f, each phase is energized for a long duration → rotor has time to settle → full holding torque available.
2. As f increases, the phase current does not have time to reach its steady-state value (due to winding inductance L).
3. Effective current I(t) = I_ss × (1 − e^(−t/τ)), where τ = L/R.
4. Smaller I → smaller magnetic flux → smaller torque.
5. Therefore: **T_available ∝ I(ω) → T_max ∝ 1/ω** (approximately, in the mid-speed region).
6. Beyond the **pull-out curve**, the motor stalls and loses synchronism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Stepper is a slow-burn workhorse — give it time, and it gives you torque; rush it, and torque fades."*

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For 14-Mark "Compare" Questions)

| **Parameter** | **VR Stepper** | **PM Stepper** | **Hybrid Stepper** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotor | Soft iron, toothed | Permanent magnet | Toothed + axially magnetized PM |
| Detent Torque (unenergized) | **Zero** | Moderate | **High** |
| Step Angle | Large (7.5°–15°) | Large (45°–90°) | Very small (0.9°–5°) |
| Holding Torque (energized) | Low | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Inertia | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | **High** |
| Typical Use | Cheap instrumentation | Automotive gauges | **CNC, 3D printers, robotics** |
| Resolution | Low | Low | **High** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Stepper Motor Subsystem (Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Type:** Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (mapping signal & power interactions between the controller, driver, and the stepper motor, per KTU Section 4 fallback rule).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU["Microcontroller or PLC Pulse Generator"]
    DIR["Direction Logic Pin HIGH or LOW"]
    EN["Enable Signal Pin"]
    CLK["Clock Pulse Train Pin f in Hz"]
    DRV["Stepper Driver IC e.g. A4988 or DRV8825 or L298N"]
    HBR["H Bridge MOSFETs Phase A and B Current Chopping"]
    MOT["Stepper Motor NEMA 17 Hybrid 1.8 degree"]
    SHAFT["Mechanical Shaft and Load Coupling"]
    FB["Open Loop NO Feedback Sensor Position implicit in pulse count"]

    MCU --> CLK
    MCU --> DIR
    MCU --> EN
    CLK --> DRV
    DIR --> DRV
    EN --> DRV
    DRV --> HBR
    HBR --> MOT
    MOT --> SHAFT
    MOT -.implicit position.-> FB
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The MCU (microcontroller) emits a digital pulse train. The driver IC interprets the pulses and switches the H-bridge MOSFETs. The H-bridge sequentially energizes the two-phase windings of the stepper. The rotor steps. **No physical feedback** — the position is inferred from the pulse count (open-loop).

---

## 4.2 Internal Construction of a Hybrid Stepper (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STATOR["STATOR with 8 salient teeth per stack, two coils A and B"]
        A1["Stator Tooth 1 North"]
        A2["Stator Tooth 2 South"]
        B1["Stator Tooth 3 North"]
        B2["Stator Tooth 4 South"]
    end

    subgraph ROTOR["ROTOR two toothed cups separated by axially magnetized permanent magnet"]
        R1["Cup 1 Polarized South teeth"]
        PM["Axial Permanent Magnet Disc N on one side and S on the other"]
        R2["Cup 2 Polarized North teeth"]
    end

    SHAFT["Output Shaft Coupled to Rotor"]

    R1 --> SHAFT
    R2 --> SHAFT
    R1 --- PM
    PM --- R2

    A1 -. magnetic flux lines .-> R1
    A2 -. magnetic flux lines .-> R1
    B1 -. magnetic flux lines .-> R2
    B2 -. magnetic flux lines .-> R2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why hybrid gives small step angle:** The two rotor cups are offset by **half a tooth pitch**. With many fine teeth (e.g., 50 teeth per cup), the angular pitch is 360°/50 = 7.2°, but the effective step is **half of that = 3.6°**, and with multi-tooth stator phases, it can be reduced to **1.8°** — hence the popularity of hybrid steppers.

---

## 4.3 Half-Step Excitation Sequence (Timing Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Step 1 Phase A ON"]
    P2["Step 2 A and B ON"]
    P3["Step 3 Phase B ON"]
    P4["Step 4 B and C ON"]
    P5["Step 5 Phase C ON"]
    P6["Step 6 C and D ON"]
    P7["Step 7 Phase D ON"]
    P8["Step 8 D and A ON"]
    RET["Returns to Step 1"]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> RET
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Drawing this 8-step half-step sequence in the exam fetches full marks on the excitation-mode question. Note that **single-phase steps (1, 3, 5, 7)** give lower torque; **two-phase steps (2, 4, 6, 8)** give higher torque. The alternation is what makes the motion smooth and doubles the resolution.

---

## 4.4 Torque vs. Pulse-Rate Curve (Qualitative Schematic via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Low Pulse Rate Region Torque = Holding Torque Maximum"]
    L2["Mid Pulse Rate Region Torque decreases approximately linearly"]
    L3["High Pulse Rate Region Torque approaches Zero Pull out curve"]
    L4["Beyond Pull Out Torque Motor stalls loses synchronism"]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Practice]**

**Q1. Define a stepper motor. Why is it called a "digital-to-analog" converter of motion?**
*[3 Marks — Remember / Understand Level]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A stepper motor is a **brushless, synchronous electromechanical actuator** that converts a train of **discrete digital electrical pulses** into **discrete angular mechanical steps** (each of fixed angle β). It is called a "digital-to-analog converter of motion" because the input is a digital signal (counted pulses) and the output is an analog quantity (continuous angular position, position = N_pulses × β). The motor advances by a fixed step angle for every pulse, producing an incremental mechanical rotation. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. List any three advantages and one disadvantage of a stepper motor.**
*[3 Marks — Understand Level]*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Open-loop position control — no encoder/feedback required. **[1 mark]**
2. Excellent low-speed torque (holding torque even at standstill). **[1 mark]**
3. Direct digital compatibility with microcontrollers/PLCs. **[1 mark]**
*(any 3 of: precise positioning, low cost, brushless/long life, compatible with digital logic)*

**Disadvantage:**
1. Low efficiency, prone to resonance, loss of synchronism at high loads without feedback. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Provided

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *Excitation Modes & Operation*

**(a)** With neat phase sequence diagrams, explain the **wave (one-phase-on)** and **two-phase-on (full-step)** excitation modes of a 4-phase stepper motor. Compare their torques. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A hybrid stepper motor has **N_s = 50 stator teeth per stack** and **N_r = 50 rotor teeth per cup**, with **2 phase pairs (m = 2)**. If the pulse rate is **1500 Hz**, find the **step angle** and the **shaft speed in RPM**. Also compute the **number of pulses required for a 144° rotation**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### ✅ Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Wave / One-Phase-On (Full Step) Mode:** Only one stator phase is energized at a time. The rotor aligns with the energized phase and holds there until the next pulse.

**Phase Sequence:** A → B → C → D → A … (cyclic)

**Torque:** Lower than two-phase-on. Because only one phase produces flux, magnetic coupling is weaker.

**Two-Phase-On (Full Step) Mode:** Two adjacent stator phases are energized simultaneously. The rotor aligns at the **midpoint** between the two phase poles.

**Phase Sequence:** AB → BC → CD → DA → AB … (cyclic)

**Torque:** **Higher (≈ 1.4× wave mode).** Two phases contribute flux → stronger magnetic field → greater holding torque.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **Wave Mode** | **Two-Phase-On** |
|---|---|---|
| Phases ON at a time | 1 | 2 |
| Power consumption | Lower | **Higher** |
| Holding torque | Lower | **Higher (≈ 1.4×)** |
| Rotor rest position | Aligned with phase | **Midway between two phases** |
| Heat dissipation | Less | More |

**[Full 7 marks awarded for: diagrams (3 marks), correct sequence explanation (2 marks), comparison table (2 marks)]**

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Given:** N_s = 50, N_r = 50, m = 2, f = 1500 Hz.

**Step 1 — Step angle formula for hybrid stepper:**

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{2 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{100}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = 3.6°}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft speed in RPM:**

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{f \cdot \beta}{6°} = \frac{1500 \times 3.6}{6}$$

$$\boxed{N_{rpm} = 900 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Step 3 — Pulses for 144° rotation:**

$$N_{pulses} = \frac{144°}{\beta} = \frac{144°}{3.6°} = 40 \text{ pulses}$$

$$\boxed{N_{pulses} = 40}$$

**Verification:** 40 × 3.6° = 144° ✓

**[Full 7 marks awarded: 2 for step angle, 2 for RPM, 2 for pulse count, 1 for verification]**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Construction & Comparison*

**(a)** With the help of a neat construction diagram, explain the working of a **Hybrid Stepper Motor**. Why does it combine the advantages of PM and VR types? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare **Variable Reluctance (VR)**, **Permanent Magnet (PM)**, and **Hybrid (HB)** stepper motors based on **construction, step angle, holding torque, detent torque, and typical applications**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### ✅ Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) Solution:

A **Hybrid Stepper Motor** has two main parts:
- **Stator:** Made of laminated silicon steel with **salient poles (teeth)**, carrying two phase windings (A and B) wound on alternate teeth.
- **Rotor:** Consists of **two toothed cups** separated by an **axially magnetized permanent magnet disc**. The two cups are **offset by half a tooth pitch** so that one cup's North tooth aligns with the other cup's South tooth.

**Working Principle:**
1. When Phase A is energized, the stator field exerts a torque on the rotor. The rotor shifts by **one tooth pitch / 2**.
2. The permanent magnet polarizes the two rotor cups in opposite directions, doubling the effective flux linkage.
3. When Phase A is de-energized and Phase B is energized, the rotor advances by another half-pitch.

**Why it combines PM + VR advantages:**
- The **axially magnetized PM** (from PM type) provides strong **detent torque** and **holding torque**.
- The **toothed construction of both rotor and stator** (from VR type) gives **fine step resolution** (small step angle).
- Result: **Small step angle + high torque** — the best of both worlds.

**[7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for working, 2 for "why hybrid" explanation]**

#### Part (b) Solution:

| **Parameter** | **VR Stepper** | **PM Stepper** | **Hybrid Stepper** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotor** | Soft iron, toothed (no magnet) | Permanent magnet (cylindrical) | Toothed rotor + axial PM |
| **Step Angle** | 7.5° – 15° (large) | 45° – 90° (very large) | **0.9° – 5° (small)** |
| **Holding Torque** | Low | Moderate | **High** |
| **Detent Torque (unenergized)** | **Zero** | Moderate | **High** |
| **Resolution** | Low | Lowest | **Highest** |
| **Construction Complexity** | Simple | Moderate | **Complex** |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | **Highest** |
| **Typical Applications** | Cheap instruments, clocks | Automotive dashboards, toys | **3D printers, CNC, robotics** |

**[7 marks: 1 mark per row × 7 rows = 7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways KTU Students Lose Marks in Stepper Motor Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Step Angle formulas:** Students often write $\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r}$ (VR formula) when the motor is a **hybrid** — for hybrid, you **must** include the multiplier **m** (phase pairs) and use the full formula $\beta = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$. **[−2 to −3 marks]**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the ×60 conversion to RPM:** Writing shaft speed in rev/s instead of RPM. Always check if the question wants RPM. **[−1 to −2 marks]**
>
> 3. **Drawing excitation sequence as a table only, no diagram:** KTU examiners explicitly want a "neat phase sequence diagram." Use **Mermaid-style or hand-drawn square-wave timing diagrams** with phases A, B, C, D as four horizontal lines. **[−2 marks]**
>
> 4. **Not stating the unit of holding torque:** N·m, not just "torque." Units matter. **[−0.5 mark per question]**
>
> 5. **Oversimplifying "Why Hybrid"** in part (a) comparisons: Just saying "it combines both" is not enough — you must mention the **mechanism** (axially magnetized PM gives detent torque + toothed construction gives fine step). **[−1 to −2 marks]**
>
> 6. **Ignoring the open-loop limitation:** A stepper does not have feedback. Loss of step is silent and undetected. Examiners love testing this "intelligent system" awareness. **[−1 mark]**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This to Your Wall):**
>
> ✅ A stepper motor is a **brushless synchronous motor** that moves in **discrete steps**.
> ✅ Each pulse = one step of angle **β**.
> ✅ Position is controlled by **counting pulses** (no encoder needed).
> ✅ Speed is proportional to **pulse frequency (f)**.
> ✅ **Three types:** VR (no detent), PM (large step, has detent), **Hybrid (1.8° standard, industry favorite)**.
> ✅ Hybrid combines PM (detent) + VR (small step) advantages.
> ✅ **Key formula:** $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ (Hybrid), $N_{steps} = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$, $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{6°}$.
> ✅ **Excitation modes:** Wave (1-phase, low torque), Full-step (2-phase, high torque), Half-step (mixed, doubles resolution), Microstepping (smoothest).
> ✅ **Torque falls with speed** (opposite of DC motor) — because winding inductance limits current at high f.
> ✅ **Holding torque** = torque at standstill when energized.
> ✅ **Pull-out torque** = max torque at given speed before losing synchronism.
> ✅ **Open-loop Achilles' heel:** Step loss is silent — no error feedback.
> ✅ **Applications:** 3D printers, CNC machines, robotics, camera gimbals, syringe pumps, plotters.
> ✅ **Standard NEMA sizes:** NEMA 17 (1.8°, ~0.4 N·m), NEMA 23 (bigger), NEMA 34 (industrial).
> ✅ **Driver ICs:** A4988, DRV8825, TMC2208/2209 (silent stepper tech).
> ✅ **Stepper + Microcontroller = "intelligent" open-loop actuator** in the KTU 2024 syllabus context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servo Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Servo Motors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Servo Motor** is a rotary or linear actuator that enables precise control of angular or linear position, velocity, and acceleration in a closed-loop control system. Unlike ordinary induction or DC motors, a servo motor incorporates a built-in **position feedback sensor** (typically an encoder, resolver, or potentiometer) and a dedicated **servo controller/driver** that continuously compares the commanded reference input with the actual output and corrects any deviation (error) in real-time. In the context of **Components of Intelligent Systems (PEMET414)**, servo motors are classified as the **actuation subsystem** of a mechatronic/intelligent machine. They convert low-power control signals (typically $\pm 10\text{ V}$, $4$–$20\text{ mA}$, or PWM pulses) into high-power mechanical motion with high precision, repeatability, and bandwidth. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A servo motor is **NOT just a motor** — it is a complete *closed-loop position/velocity control system* consisting of (i) a motor, (ii) a feedback sensor, (iii) an error detector, and (iv) a power amplifier. The KTU board examiner frequently tests whether students understand the closed-loop nature of the system. **Physical Constants and Standard Metrics:** | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---| | Position accuracy (industrial servo) | $\pm 0.01°$ to $\pm 0.001°$ | | Servo bandwidth (typical) | **$100$ Hz to $1$ kHz** | | Control signal (analog) | $\pm 10\text{ V}$ DC | | Control signal (digital) | PWM $50\text{ Hz}$, pulse width $1$–$2\text{ ms}$ | | Encoder resolution | $10\text{-bit}$ ($1024$ ppr) to $23\text{-bit}$ ($8.4$M ppr) | | Torque range | $0.5\text{ N·m}$ to $500\text{ N·m}$ | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are driving a car on a narrow lane, and you have a co-passenger who constantly watches the lane markings and tells you: *"Steer left 2 degrees... a bit more... perfect, hold."* The co-passenger is acting as a **feedback system**. The car is the **motor**, the steering wheel is the **actuator**, the road is the **desired position**, and the co-passenger's corrections form the **error-correction loop**. A **servo motor** works exactly like this. Send a command (e.g., "rotate to 45°"), and the servo: 1. Checks the current position via a feedback sensor. 2. Computes the **error** = (desired position) − (actual position). 3. Drives the motor in the correct direction to reduce the error. 4. Stops and locks when the error becomes **zero**. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Takeaway:** A regular motor just spins when you give it power. A **servo motor spins to a SPECIFIC position** (or speed) and *stays there* — it obeys commands like a loyal, precise robotic muscle. **Real-world examples of servo motor applications:** - Robotic arm joints in industrial assembly lines - CNC machine tool axis drives - Camera gimbal stabilizers in drones - Antenna positioning in radar systems - Pick-and-place machines in SMT electronics manufacturing - Solar tracker panels - Prosthetic limbs and humanoid robots # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Types of Servo Motors (KTU-Focused Classification) Servo motors are broadly classified into the following categories, each of which has its own working principle, transfer function, and characteristic equation. ### 2.1.1 DC Servo Motor A DC servo motor is essentially a **specially wound DC motor** (often with low inertia armatures, low armature inductance, and reduced friction) designed for high-speed, high-precision servo applications. The field winding may be **separately excited** (most common in servo drives) or **permanent magnet** (in small/medium-power servos). **Working Principle:** - The reference voltage $V_r$ is applied as the command signal. - The actual position is converted to a proportional voltage $V_c$ by a **potentiometer/encoder**. - An **error detector** computes $e = V_r - V_c$. - The error voltage, after amplification, is applied to the **armature terminals** of the DC motor. - The motor rotates until the error is zero. **Field Control vs. Armature Control:** - **Armature-controlled DC servo motor:** Field flux $\phi$ is held **constant**, and the armature voltage $v_a$ is varied to control torque/speed. - **Field-controlled DC servo motor:** Armature current is held **constant**, and the field voltage $v_f$ is varied. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Favourite:** The transfer function of an **armature-controlled** DC servo motor is **always** asked. Memorise it as: $$\frac{\theta_o(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s\left[ s(JL_a + RB_m) + (JR + B L_a) \right] + K_m K_b \cdot \text{(feedback terms)}}$$ The simplified second-order form used in KTU problems is: $$\frac{\theta_o(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s\left( \tau_a \tau_m s^2 + (\tau_a + \tau_m)s + 1 \right)}$$ where $\tau_a = L_a/R_a$ is the **electrical time constant** and $\tau_m = J R_a / (K_m K_b)$ is the **mechanical time constant**. ### 2.1.2 AC Servo Motor (Two-Phase Induction Motor) An AC servo motor is a **two-phase induction motor** with a **high-resistance rotor** (typically a squirrel-cage rotor with very high rotor resistance, or a drag-cup rotor) designed to operate smoothly from **zero speed** to a high synchronous speed. **Working Principle:** - It has two stator windings: a **reference winding** (fixed-amplitude AC supply) and a **control winding** (variable-amplitude, variable-phase AC command signal). - The two windings produce a **rotating magnetic field** whose magnitude and direction depend on the **phase difference** and the **amplitude ratio** between the two voltages. - The motor produces torque proportional to $\sin(2\alpha)$, where $\alpha$ is the phase difference between the two supply voltages. - The high rotor resistance ensures a **linear torque-speed characteristic** (no dead zone), which is essential for stable servo operation. ### 2.1.3 Brushless DC (BLDC) Servo Motor A BLDC servo motor replaces the mechanical commutator and brushes of a conventional DC servo with **electronic commutation** using Hall-effect sensors or back-EMF detection. It offers: - Higher efficiency (no brush friction) - Higher torque-to-weight ratio - Lower maintenance - Better thermal dissipation ### 2.1.4 Stepper Motor (Open-Loop Servo Substitute) Strictly, a stepper motor is **open-loop**. However, modern intelligent systems often pair stepper motors with encoders to form **closed-loop stepper servos** (also called "step-servos"). It is included here because the KTU 2024 syllabus often groups it under the servo motor umbrella. ## 2.2 Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop Servo System A typical **position-control servo system** consists of the following cascaded blocks: 1. **Command/Reference input** $R(s)$ — desired angular position. 2. **Error detector (Comparator)** — computes $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$. 3. **Servo amplifier** — gain $K_A$. 4. **Power amplifier / Motor driver** — gain $K_m$. 5. **Motor + load (Plant)** — transfer function $G_m(s)$. 6. **Feedback sensor (Potentiometer / Encoder)** — gain $K_t$. 7. **Output** $C(s)$ — actual angular position. The **closed-loop transfer function** is: $$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$ For a **unity feedback** system ($H(s)=1$): $$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Formula** | **Symbol Description** | **Unit** | **Application** | |---|---|---|---| | $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$ | Error signal in Laplace domain | V (or rad) | Error detector | | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | dimensionless | Position/velocity control | | $T_m = \dfrac{JR_a}{K_m K_b}$ | Mechanical time constant | s | DC servo motor dynamics | | $T_a = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ | Electrical (armature) time constant | s | DC servo motor dynamics | | $\dfrac{\theta_o(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s(T_a T_m s^2 + (T_a + T_m)s + 1)}$ | Armature-controlled DC servo TF | rad/V | KTU board exam standard | | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s\to 0} s\,E(s)$ | Steady-state error | depends on input | Type-0,1,2 system analysis | | $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} s\,G(s)H(s)$ | Static velocity error constant | s$^{-1}$ | Ramp input response | | $K_p = \lim_{s\to 0} G(s)H(s)$ | Static position error constant | dimensionless | Step input response | | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Torque developed by motor | N·m | DC motor torque equation | | $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF | V | DC motor voltage equation | | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power output | W | Motor power rating | | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b}$ | Speed equation of DC servo | rad/s | Speed control | | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ | Natural frequency of second-order system | rad/s | Servo loop stability | | $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | Servo response shape | | $M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage overshoot | % | Transient response | | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | Settling time (2% criterion) | s | Speed of response | | $\dfrac{\theta_o(s)}{\theta_i(s)} = \dfrac{K_m K_A}{s(L_a Js^2 + (L_a B + R_a J)s + R_a B + K_m K_b) + K_m K_A K_t}$ | Armature-controlled DC servo with position feedback | rad/rad | **Most asked KTU TF** | | $\tau \propto \sin(2\alpha)$ | AC servo motor torque-phase relation | N·m | AC servo phase control | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU 2024 Board Tip:** The full closed-loop transfer function of an armature-controlled DC servo motor **with position feedback** is the **single most tested formula** in this module. It is worth **7 marks** on its own in a typical Part B (14-mark) question. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Robotics:** Each joint of a 6-DOF robotic arm uses a servo motor with a closed-loop position controller. Modern collaborative robots (cobots) use brushless AC servos with $23$-bit encoders. - **CNC Machining:** X, Y, Z axis drives use **servo motors** (not stepper) when sub-micron precision and high feed rates are required. - **Aerospace:** Flight control surfaces (ailerons, elevators, rudders) are driven by **electro-hydraulic servos** (EHSV) — the muscle of fly-by-wire systems. - **Semiconductor manufacturing:** Wafer-handling robots use **direct-drive torque motors** (a type of servo) to avoid backlash entirely. - **Medical:** Surgical robots (da Vinci) use **harmonic-drive servo motors** for tremor-free, precision instrument positioning. - **Renewable energy:** Solar trackers use **servo motors** to follow the sun's path, increasing PV panel yield by **up to 25%**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function of Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor The KTU 2024 board exam **almost always** asks for the derivation of the transfer function $\theta_o(s)/V_a(s)$ of an armature-controlled DC servo motor. We derive it here in complete, step-by-step detail. ### 3.1.1 Governing Physical Equations The DC servo motor is described by **three coupled equations** — electrical, mechanical, and electromechanical coupling. **Step 1: Write the armature circuit KVL (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law).** The applied armature voltage $V_a$ drives a current $i_a$ through the armature resistance $R_a$ and inductance $L_a$, and is opposed by the back-EMF $e_b$: $$V_a = i_a R_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + e_b$$ **Step 2: Write the back-EMF equation.** The back-EMF $e_b$ is proportional to the angular velocity $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta_o}{dt}$: $$e_b = K_b \cdot \omega = K_b \cdot \frac{d\theta_o}{dt}$$ where $K_b$ is the **back-EMF constant** (units: V·s/rad). **Step 3: Write the torque equation of the rotating system.** The motor develops a torque $T_m$ proportional to the armature current $i_a$. This torque drives the load inertia $J$ against viscous friction $B$: $$T_m = J \frac{d^2\theta_o}{dt^2} + B \frac{d\theta_o}{dt}$$ The developed torque is: $$T_m = K_t \cdot i_a$$ where $K_t$ is the **torque constant** (units: N·m/A). For SI units, $K_t = K_b$. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Laplace Transform **Step 4: Take the Laplace transform of all three equations assuming zero initial conditions.** For the armature circuit: $$V_a(s) = I_a(s) R_a + s L_a I_a(s) + E_b(s)$$ $$V_a(s) = I_a(s) (R_a + s L_a) + E_b(s)$$ For the back-EMF equation: $$E_b(s) = K_b \, s \, \theta_o(s)$$ For the mechanical equation: $$K_t I_a(s) = J s^2 \theta_o(s) + B s \theta_o(s) = s \theta_o(s) (Js + B)$$ ### 3.1.3 Eliminate the Intermediate Variables **Step 5: Solve for $I_a(s)$ from the mechanical equation.** $$I_a(s) = \frac{s \theta_o(s) (Js + B)}{K_t}$$ **Step 6: Substitute $I_a(s)$ and $E_b(s)$ into the armature KVL.** $$V_a(s) = \left[ \frac{s \theta_o(s) (Js + B)}{K_t} \right] (R_a + s L_a) + K_b \, s \, \theta_o(s)$$ **Step 7: Group $\theta_o(s)$ terms on one side.** $$V_a(s) = s \theta_o(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + s L_a)(Js + B)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$ **Step 8: Factor out the common $s$ and simplify the bracket.** $$V_a(s) = s \theta_o(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + s L_a)(Js + B) + K_b K_t}{K_t} \right]$$ **Step 9: Expand the product $(R_a + sL_a)(Js + B)$.** $$(R_a + sL_a)(Js + B) = R_a J s + R_a B + s L_a J s + s L_a B$$ $$= R_a B + (R_a J + L_a B) s + L_a J s^2$$ **Step 10: Substitute back and form the full transfer function.** $$V_a(s) = \frac{s \theta_o(s)}{K_t} \left[ L_a J s^2 + (R_a J + L_a B) s + R_a B + K_b K_t \right]$$ **Step 11: Rearrange to isolate $\theta_o(s)/V_a(s)$.** $$\frac{\theta_o(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ L_a J s^2 + (R_a J + L_a B) s + (R_a B + K_b K_t) \right]}$$ This is the **general, exact transfer function** of an armature-controlled DC servo motor. ### 3.1.4 Introduce the Time-Constant Form **Step 12: Define the electrical and mechanical time constants.** $$\tau_a = \frac{L_a}{R_a} \quad \text{(armature electrical time constant)}$$ $$\tau_m = \frac{R_a J}{K_b K_t} \quad \text{(mechanical time constant)}$$ And the motor gain: $$K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_b K_t}$$ **Step 13: Substitute and simplify (dividing numerator and denominator by $R_a B + K_b K_t$).** $$\frac{\theta_o(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s \left[ \tau_a \tau_m s^2 + (\tau_a + \tau_m) s + 1 \right]}$$ This is the **standard KTU exam form** of the transfer function. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does the form $1/s$ appear?** The $1/s$ term in the denominator indicates a **type-1 system** — the motor is an integrating element between voltage and position. A constant voltage produces a *linearly increasing* position (constant velocity). This is the foundation of why servos can track ramp inputs with finite error. ## 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Position Servo Transfer Function For a complete position-control servo loop, the motor is enclosed in feedback with a position sensor (gain $K_t'$) and a controller/amplifier (gain $K_A$). **Step 14: Forward path transfer function.** $$G(s) = K_A \cdot K_m \cdot \frac{1}{s \left[ \tau_a \tau_m s^2 + (\tau_a + \tau_m) s + 1 \right]}$$ **Step 15: Feedback path.** $$H(s) = K_t'$$ **Step 16: Apply the standard closed-loop formula.** $$T(s) = \frac{\theta_o(s)}{\theta_i(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$ **Step 17: Substitute and simplify.** $$\frac{\theta_o(s)}{\theta_i(s)} = \frac{K_A K_m}{s \left[ \tau_a \tau_m s^2 + (\tau_a + \tau_m) s + 1 \right] + K_A K_m K_t'}$$ This is the standard KTU exam result for a position servo system. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison of Servo Motor Types | **Parameter** | **DC Servo** | **AC Servo (2-phase)** | **Brushless DC Servo** | **Stepper (open-loop)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Power range | $5\text{ W}$–$10\text{ kW}$ | $0.5\text{ W}$–$500\text{ W}$ | $10\text{ W}$–$50\text{ kW}$ | $1\text{ W}$–$5\text{ kW}$ | | Peak torque | High | Moderate | Very high | Moderate | | Speed range | Up to $6000\text{ rpm}$ | Up to $5000\text{ rpm}$ | Up to $20000\text{ rpm}$ | Up to $3000\text{ rpm}$ | | Position accuracy | $\pm 0.001°$ | $\pm 0.5°$ | $\pm 0.0001°$ (with $23$-bit encoder) | $\pm 0.1°$ (open-loop) | | Maintenance | High (brush wear) | Low | Very low | Low | | Cost | Moderate | Moderate | High | Low | | Torque-speed linearity | Excellent (linear) | Good (with high-R rotor) | Excellent | Non-linear (decreases with speed) | | Heat dissipation | Poor (rotor) | Moderate | Excellent (stator) | Poor | | Feedback sensor | Encoder / Tachogenerator | Resolver / Encoder | Hall sensors + Encoder | None (open-loop) | | KTU exam focus | **Highest** | High | Moderate | Low (open-loop only) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Servo Motor System ```mermaid flowchart LR cmdA[Reference Input
R] --> compA[Error Detector
Comparator] fbA[Feedback Sensor
Encoder or Potentiometer] --> compA compA --> ampA[Servo Amplifier
Gain K_A] ampA --> pwrA[Power Amplifier
Motor Driver] pwrA --> motorA[DC Servo Motor
Plant G_m s] motorA --> loadA[Mechanical Load
J and B] loadA --> outA[Output Shaft
Angular Position theta] outA --> fbA ``` > **Caption:** Figure 1 — Classical closed-loop position servo system block diagram. The error detector subtracts the feedback signal from the reference input, the amplifier boosts the error, the power stage drives the motor, and the output is fed back through the sensor to close the loop. ## 4.2 Internal Block Diagram of Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor ```mermaid flowchart LR va[Va s
Armature Voltage] --> r1[Sum Node 1] r1 --> ra[R_a
Armature Resistance] ra --> la[L_a
Armature Inductance] la --> iaout[Ia s
Armature Current] iaout --> ktblk[K_t
Torque Constant] ktblk --> sumT[Sum Node T] sumT --> mechA[J s squared
Load Inertia] mechA --> mechB[B s
Viscous Friction] mechB --> thetOut[theta_o s
Angular Position] thetOut --> kb[K_b
Back EMF Constant] kb --> fb1[Feedback to Sum Node 1] thetOut --> outLabel[Output Position Signal] ``` > **Caption:** Figure 2 — Internal functional architecture of an armature-controlled DC servo motor. Three coupled sub-systems — electrical (armature circuit), electromechanical (back-EMF and torque conversion), and mechanical (rotor inertia and friction) — interact to produce the standard second-order, type-1 servo transfer function. ## 4.3 AC Servo Motor Two-Phase Winding Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB acRef[Fixed AC Supply
Reference Winding] --> refW[Stator Reference Winding
Phase alpha = 0] acCtrl[Variable AC Supply
Control Signal] --> ctrlW[Stator Control Winding
Phase alpha Variable] refW --> rmf[Rotating Magnetic Field
Magnitude and Direction Set by Phase Difference] ctrlW --> rmf rmf --> rotor[High-Resistance Rotor
Drag Cup or Squirrel Cage] rotor --> torque[Torque T proportional to sin 2 alpha] torque --> output[Mechanical Output Shaft] ``` > **Caption:** Figure 3 — Functional architecture of a two-phase AC servo motor. The two stator windings — one fixed (reference) and one variable (control) — produce a rotating magnetic field whose magnitude and direction are controlled by the amplitude and phase of the control winding signal. ## 4.4 Servo Motor Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD startQ{Need closed-loop
precision positioning?} startQ -- Yes --> powerQ{Power level?} startQ -- No --> stepper[Use Stepper Motor] powerQ -- Less than 1 kW --> dcQ{Need brushless operation?} powerQ -- Greater than 1 kW --> acSrv[Use AC Servo Motor] dcQ -- Yes --> bldc[Use Brushless DC Servo] dcQ -- No --> dcSrv[Use Brushed DC Servo] ``` > **Caption:** Figure 4 — Decision tree for selecting the appropriate servo motor type based on application requirements. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define a servo motor. How is it different from a conventional DC motor?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A servo motor is a rotary actuator that, together with a feedback sensor and a controller, forms a **closed-loop position, velocity, or torque control system**. It is designed to drive a mechanical load to a specific commanded position, speed, or torque and to maintain that state with high accuracy. | **Conventional DC Motor** | **Servo Motor** | |---|---| | Open-loop; spins when energised | Closed-loop; rotates to a *specific* position | | No built-in feedback sensor | Built-in encoder / resolver / potentiometer | | Optimised for continuous rotation | Optimised for start-stop, high-acceleration positioning | | Position cannot be controlled precisely | Position accuracy up to $\pm 0.001°$ | > **Examiner Note:** Award full marks only if the student explicitly mentions the *closed-loop* nature. Half-mark deduction if the student merely says "servo is a precise motor" without mentioning feedback. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **List the main components of a closed-loop servo system and state the function of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The main components of a closed-loop servo system are: 1. **Command/Reference input** — Desired position, velocity, or torque signal. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **Error detector (Comparator)** — Computes the error $e = R - C$ between the command and the actual output. *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Servo amplifier** — Amplifies the low-power error signal to a level sufficient to drive the power stage. *(0.5 mark)* 4. **Motor (DC/AC/BLDC)** — The plant that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **Feedback sensor** — Measures the actual output (position, speed, or torque) and converts it into a proportional electrical signal. *(0.5 mark)* 6. **Mechanical load** — The application being controlled (robot joint, antenna, machine tool axis, etc.). *(0.5 mark)* ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop DC servo position control system. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive the transfer function of an armature-controlled DC servo motor, expressing the result in terms of the electrical and mechanical time constants. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Block Diagram & Working (7 marks)** The closed-loop DC servo position control system consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Figure 1 in Section 4): 1. **Reference input $\theta_i(s)$** — The desired angular position is set by the user as a command signal. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **Error detector** — A differential amplifier or summing junction computes the error signal $E(s) = \theta_i(s) - \theta_o(s)$. *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Pre-amplifier / Servo amplifier** — The error voltage (typically in millivolts) is amplified to a few volts. Gain $= K_A$. *(0.5 mark)* 4. **Power amplifier** — Boosts the signal to a level sufficient to drive the motor armature (tens to hundreds of volts, several amps). *(0.5 mark)* 5. **DC servo motor** — The motor armature receives the amplified error voltage and develops a torque proportional to the current. It rotates the load to reduce the error. *(0.5 mark)* 6. **Feedback sensor** — A potentiometer, encoder, or resolver mounted on the output shaft produces a feedback voltage proportional to $\theta_o(s)$. *(0.5 mark)* 7. **Output** — The actual angular position $\theta_o(s)$ of the load. *(0.5 mark)* **Working:** When a command $\theta_i$ is applied, the comparator computes the error $e = \theta_i - \theta_o$. The amplifier boosts $e$ and applies it to the motor. The motor develops a torque that rotates the shaft in the direction that reduces $e$. As $\theta_o$ approaches $\theta_i$, the error shrinks, the motor slows down, and the system settles at $\theta_o = \theta_i$. The feedback loop thus *automatically* nullifies the error. *(3.5 marks for working principle, neat block diagram, and labelling)* **Part (b) — Derivation of the Transfer Function (7 marks)** **Step 1: Armature circuit equation.** *(1 mark)* $$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$ **Step 2: Back-EMF equation.** *(0.5 mark)* $$e_b(t) = K_b \frac{d\theta_o(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 3: Torque equation of the motor-load system.** *(0.5 mark)* $$T_m(t) = J \frac{d^2\theta_o(t)}{dt^2} + B \frac{d\theta_o(t)}{dt}$$ $$T_m(t) = K_t i_a(t)$$ **Step 4: Take Laplace transforms with zero initial conditions.** *(0.5 mark)* $$V_a(s) = (R_a + sL_a) I_a(s) + K_b s \theta_o(s)$$ $$K_t I_a(s) = (Js + B) s \theta_o(s)$$ **Step 5: Solve for $I_a(s)$ and substitute into the voltage equation.** *(1.5 marks)* $$I_a(s) = \frac{s \theta_o(s) (Js + B)}{K_t}$$ $$V_a(s) = \frac{s \theta_o(s) (Js + B)}{K_t}(R_a + sL_a) + K_b s \theta_o(s)$$ $$V_a(s) = s \theta_o(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + sL_a)(Js + B) + K_b K_t}{K_t} \right]$$ **Step 6: Expand the bracket.** *(1 mark)* $$V_a(s) = \frac{s \theta_o(s)}{K_t} \left[ L_a J s^2 + (R_a J + L_a B) s + R_a B + K_b K_t \right]$$ **Step 7: Form the transfer function.** *(0.5 mark)* $$\frac{\theta_o(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ L_a J s^2 + (R_a J + L_a B) s + (R_a B + K_b K_t) \right]}$$ **Step 8: Introduce the time constants.** *(1.5 marks)* $$\tau_a = \frac{L_a}{R_a}, \quad \tau_m = \frac{R_a J}{K_b K_t}, \quad K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_b K_t}$$ Dividing the denominator by $R_a B + K_b K_t$ and substituting: $$\boxed{\frac{\theta_o(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s \left[ \tau_a \tau_m s^2 + (\tau_a + \tau_m) s + 1 \right]}}$$ This is the **standard time-constant form** of the armature-controlled DC servo motor transfer function. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a two-phase AC servo motor. Discuss the role of high rotor resistance in achieving linear torque-speed characteristics. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A position control servo system has an open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(0.1s+1)(0.05s+1)}$. Determine: (i) the type of the system, (ii) the static error constants $K_p$, $K_v$, and $K_{a}$, (iii) the steady-state error for a unit-step input, and (iv) the steady-state error for a unit-ramp input. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Two-Phase AC Servo Motor (7 marks)** **Construction (2 marks):** - **Stator:** Two windings — a *reference winding* and a *control winding* — physically displaced by $90°$ in space. - **Rotor:** A high-resistance squirrel-cage rotor or a thin drag-cup rotor mounted on a low-inertia shaft. **Working (3 marks):** The reference winding is supplied with a **fixed-amplitude AC voltage** $V_{ref}$ at line frequency $\omega_s$. The control winding is supplied with a **variable-amplitude AC voltage** $V_c$ of the *same frequency* but with a *phase angle difference* $\alpha$ relative to $V_{ref}$. The two windings produce a **rotating magnetic field** in the air gap. The magnitude of this field is: $$B_{net} = B_{ref} + B_c e^{j\alpha}$$ The **direction of rotation** can be reversed by reversing the polarity of the control winding (changing $\alpha$ from $+90°$ to $-90°$). The **speed of rotation** depends on the amplitude $V_c$. The torque developed is: $$T \propto V_{ref} \cdot V_c \cdot \sin(2\alpha)$$ When the error is zero, the control voltage is also zero, the magnetic field becomes purely pulsating (not rotating), and the motor produces no torque. This is the **ideal servo behaviour** — the motor naturally produces zero torque at the desired position. **Role of High Rotor Resistance (2 marks):** A conventional induction motor has a low-resistance rotor, which gives a *non-linear* torque-speed curve with a "dead zone" near zero speed — unsuitable for servo use. A high rotor resistance shifts the **maximum torque point** to *zero slip* ($s = 1$, i.e., zero speed), making the torque-speed characteristic **linear** and the **starting torque equal to the maximum torque**. This linearity is critical for stable closed-loop servo operation. **Part (b) — Steady-State Error Analysis (7 marks)** **Given:** $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(0.1s+1)(0.05s+1)}$ **(i) System type:** The number of pure integrators ($1/s$ factors) in the open-loop transfer function. Here, there is **one** pole at the origin, so the system is a **Type-1 system**. *(1 mark)* **(ii) Static error constants:** *(3 marks)* $$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10}{s(0.1s+1)(0.05s+1)} = \infty$$ $$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10}{(0.1s+1)(0.05s+1)} = \frac{10}{1 \cdot 1} = 10 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ $$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2\,G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10 s}{(0.1s+1)(0.05s+1)} = 0$$ **(iii) Steady-state error for unit-step input $R(s) = 1/s$:** *(1.5 marks)* For a Type-1 system with a step input: $$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$ **(iv) Steady-state error for unit-ramp input $R(s) = 1/s^2$:** *(1.5 marks)* For a Type-1 system with a ramp input: $$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \text{ rad}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark-Loss Zones):** > 1. **Forgetting the $s$ in the denominator:** Many students write the transfer function of the DC servo motor as $\dfrac{K_m}{\tau_a \tau_m s^2 + (\tau_a + \tau_m)s + 1}$ — **omitting the leading $1/s$**. This costs **2 full marks**. The motor is a *double integrator in series* with the second-order block: voltage $\to$ velocity $\to$ position. Always include the $1/s$. > 2. **Confusing $K_t$ and $K_b$:** The torque constant $K_t$ and the back-EMF constant $K_b$ are *numerically equal* in SI units ($K_t = K_b$ in N·m/A = V·s/rad), but they have *different physical meanings* and *different units* in the dimensional sense. A common KTU question asks students to state this explicitly. Write: *"In SI units, $K_t = K_b$ numerically, but $K_t$ converts current to torque and $K_b$ converts velocity to voltage."* > 3. **Ignoring the $R_a B$ term:** In the denominator, students often write only $K_b K_t$ and forget the $R_a B$ viscous-friction term. Include the *full* denominator: $R_a B + K_b K_t$. > 4. **Confusing system type with order:** The *order* is the highest power of $s$ in the closed-loop denominator. The *type* is the number of pure integrators in the *open-loop* transfer function. They are different. > 5. **Steady-state error sign:** Some students write the ramp error as $1/(1+K_v)$ instead of $1/K_v$. For a Type-1 system, it is $1/K_v$, **not** $1/(1+K_v)$. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — Print This Page Before the Exam** **Core Definitions:** - A **servo motor** = motor + feedback sensor + error detector + amplifier forming a *closed-loop* system. - A **conventional motor** is *open-loop*; a **servo motor** is *closed-loop*. **The "Three Equations" of a DC Servo Motor (must-know):** - **Electrical (armature):** $V_a = R_a i_a + L_a \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + e_b$ - **Electromagnetic (back-EMF):** $e_b = K_b \omega$ - **Mechanical (rotor):** $T_m = J \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ and $T_m = K_t i_a$ **The Master Transfer Function (most-asked formula):** $$\frac{\theta_o(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s\left[L_a J s^2 + (R_a J + L_a B)s + (R_a B + K_b K_t)\right]}$$ **Time-Constant Form:** $$\frac{\theta_o(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_a \tau_m s^2 + (\tau_a + \tau_m) s + 1)}$$ **Key Constants and Their Meanings:** - $\tau_a = L_a/R_a$ — electrical time constant (armature inductance vs. resistance). - $\tau_m = R_a J / (K_b K_t)$ — mechanical time constant (inertia vs. motor damping). - $K_m = K_t / (R_a B + K_b K_t)$ — motor gain. **Closed-Loop Position Servo:** $$\frac{\theta_o(s)}{\theta_i(s)} = \frac{K_A K_m}{s(\tau_a \tau_m s^2 + (\tau_a + \tau_m) s + 1) + K_A K_m K_t'}$$ **AC Servo Motor Highlights:** - Two-phase induction motor with **high-resistance rotor**. - Torque $T \propto \sin(2\alpha)$ where $\alpha$ is the phase difference. - High rotor resistance $\Rightarrow$ **linear torque-speed** curve $\Rightarrow$ zero dead zone. **Static Error Constants — Memorise the Three Formulas:** $$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s), \quad K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,G(s)H(s), \quad K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2\,G(s)H(s)$$ **Steady-State Errors — Type-of-System Table:** | **Input** | **Type-0** | **Type-1** | **Type-2** | |---|---|---|---| | Step $R(s)=1/s$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | $0$ | $0$ | | Ramp $R(s)=1/s^2$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | $0$ | | Parabola $R(s)=1/s^3$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_a}$ | **Servo Motor Selection — Quick Heuristic:** - Low power, precision, brushless → **BLDC servo**. - High power, industrial → **AC servo**. - Low cost, simple, brushed → **Brushed DC servo**. - Open-loop, low resolution → **Stepper motor** (not a true servo). **Stability and Performance Rules of Thumb:** - Increase $K_A$ → faster response but **less stable** (smaller phase margin). - Add integral action → eliminates steady-state error but **reduces stability margin**. - Increase $B$ → more damping → smaller overshoot, but **slower** response. **Common Units to Remember:** - Torque: N·m - Inertia $J$: kg·m² - Damping $B$: N·m·s/rad - Back-EMF constant $K_b$: V·s/rad - Torque constant $K_t$: N·m/A - Velocity $\omega$: rad/s - Position $\theta$: rad - Time constants: seconds

topics.title: BLDC Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** BLDC Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** is a synchronous electric motor powered by direct current (DC) electricity, in which the mechanical commutation (switching of current direction in the windings) is replaced by electronic commutation. Unlike a conventional brushed DC motor, the BLDC motor has a permanent magnet rotor and a wound stator, with the rotor position feedback (typically via Hall-effect sensors, encoders, or sensorless back-EMF detection) processed by an electronic controller to drive the phase windings in a precise trapezoidal or sinusoidal switching sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A BLDC motor is classified as a *self-controlled synchronous motor* with a trapezoidal back-EMF waveform, where the inverter switching is synchronized to the rotor position, making the motor's speed equal to the inverter reference frequency.

### Key Constructional Features
- **Rotor:** Permanent magnets (typically NdFeB or SmCo) arranged in pairs (P = number of poles).
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core carrying concentrated, distributed, or fractional-slot concentrated windings.
- **Sensors:** Three Hall-effect sensors placed 120° electrical apart (or encoder) for rotor position feedback.
- **Controller:** Three-phase inverter (typically MOSFET/IGBT-based) producing a six-step trapezoidal waveform.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine a circular **merry-go-round** with magnets on it (rotor), and three flashlights positioned 120° apart around it pointing inward (stator windings). A smart controller turns each flashlight ON only when the magnet is directly opposite it, attracting the magnet. Just as the magnet arrives, the next flashlight is switched ON, creating a constant *pull* around the circle. This "magnetic chase" makes the rotor spin smoothly without any physical brushes rubbing against it. The Hall sensors are like *eyes* that tell the controller exactly where the magnet is at any instant.

### Why BLDC Motors Are Called "Intelligent" Components
BLDC motors are foundational components in **intelligent systems** because:
- They integrate **sensors** (Hall-effect) for closed-loop feedback.
- They rely on a **microcontroller/DSP** to perform real-time commutation logic.
- They offer high **efficiency (85–95%)**, **precision speed control**, and **long life** (>10,000 hours) due to the absence of brush wear.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency ($\eta$) | **85 – 95 %** | dimensionless |
| Power density | **5 – 7** | kW/kg |
| Speed range | **1000 – 100,000** | rpm |
| Torque ripple (trapezoidal) | **± 5 to ± 15** | % of rated torque |
| Operating temperature | **-40 to +125** | °C |
| Back-EMF constant ($K_e$) | **0.5 – 100** | V/(krpm) |
| Torque constant ($K_t$) | **0.05 – 1.0** | Nm/A |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The "How" and the "Why"

### Why Permanent Magnets on the Rotor?
The rotor carries **permanent magnets** because we want a *fixed magnetic field* on the rotating part. Since the rotor is not energized, no slip rings or brushes are needed for the rotor side — only the stator (stationary) is energized.

### How Does Commutation Happen Electronically?
- The stator has **three phases** (A, B, C) — often called a star (Y) connected winding.
- At any instant, **two phases are ON** and one phase is OFF.
- The **ON pair is determined by the rotor position**, which the Hall sensors read.
- A **six-step commutation sequence** (also called a *trapezoidal drive*) energizes the phases in the order: AB → AC → BC → BA → CA → CB → AB.
- Each step advances the stator field by **60° electrical**, and the rotor magnet *chases* this rotating field, always lagging by a small *torque angle* (load-dependent).

### Step-by-Step Logic of Six-Step Commutation
1. **Hall sensors** detect the rotor pole positions (N or S) and output a 3-bit Gray code.
2. The **controller decodes** this 3-bit code into one of six valid commutation states.
3. The **inverter (3-phase bridge)** switches ON the corresponding high-side and low-side MOSFETs/IGBTs.
4. Current flows through two stator phases, creating a stator magnetic field vector.
5. The interaction between the **stator field** and the **rotor magnet field** produces torque, rotating the rotor.
6. As the rotor moves 60° electrical, **Hall sensors toggle**, and the controller advances to the next commutation state.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a trapezoidal EMF?**
> The BLDC motor is wound and magnet-shaped deliberately to produce a **trapezoidal back-EMF** waveform (flat-top, 120° wide). This perfectly matches the **square-wave current** drive of the six-step inverter, giving nearly constant torque (with small ripple at commutation).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $E_b$ | Back-EMF | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | V (volts) | EMF induced per phase |
| $K_e$ | Back-EMF constant | $K_e = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ | V·s/rad | Voltage constant |
| $T$ | Electromagnetic torque | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | N·m | Developed torque |
| $K_t$ | Torque constant | $K_t = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{2\pi A}$ | N·m/A | Torque per ampere |
| $N$ | Rotor speed | $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot E_b}{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}$ | rpm | Mechanical speed |
| $P_m$ | Mechanical power | $P_m = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W (watts) | Output power |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T \cdot \omega}{V \cdot I}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of output to input |
| $f_e$ | Electrical frequency | $f_e = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{120}$ | Hz | Supply frequency to winding |
| $K_t = K_e$ | SI relationship | $K_t \text{(Nm/A)} = K_e \text{(V·s/rad)}$ | — | Identity in SI units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions / Assumptions in KTU 2024 derivations:**
> 1. Magnetic circuit is **linear** (no saturation).
> 2. Armature reaction is **neglected** (or compensated by advance angle).
> 3. Each phase is supplied with **rectangular (square) current** of $120°$ electrical duration.
> 4. **Trapezoidal back-EMF** with $120°$ flat top.
> 5. **Star-connected, three-phase** winding unless otherwise specified.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application Domain | Use of BLDC | Why Preferred |
|---|---|---|
| **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** | Traction motors in Teslas, Ola scooters | High power density, regenerative braking |
| **Drones / Quadcopters** | 4–8 motors for propulsion | High torque-to-weight ratio |
| **CNC Machines / Robotics** | Servo joint drives | Precise position and speed control |
| **HVAC / Refrigeration** | Compressor drives | Variable speed, energy savings |
| **Computer Hard Disks** | Spindle drive | Low noise, long life |
| **Medical Devices** | Infusion pumps, surgical tools | Sterile (no brush dust), reliable |
| **Consumer Appliances** | Washing machines, ceiling fans | Silent, electronically commutated (EC fans) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Back-EMF Equation ($E_b$)

The back-EMF in one phase of a BLDC motor is derived from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

> **Faraday's Law:** The EMF induced in a coil is proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Flux per pole:**
Let $\Phi$ be the useful flux per pole (in Webers, Wb).

**Step 2 — Flux cutting per revolution:**
In one mechanical revolution, a conductor cuts the flux $\Phi$, $P$ times (where $P$ is the number of poles).

**Step 3 — Total flux cut per minute:**
The conductor rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm), so the number of flux cuts per minute is:
$$\text{Flux cuts/min} = P \cdot \Phi \cdot N$$

**Step 4 — Average EMF per conductor:**
By Faraday's law, the average EMF induced in a single conductor is:
$$E_{\text{conductor}} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{(volts)}$$
