**Explain the function of the `Serial.begin(9600)` statement. What happens if the Serial Monitor on the PC is set to a different baud rate?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- `Serial.begin(9600)` initializes the **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** hardware on the ATmega328P at a **baud rate of 9600 bits per second**, configuring the TX/RX pins and the clock divider. (2 marks)
- It establishes the **bit-timing contract** that the PC's Serial Monitor must match exactly. (0.5 marks)
- If the Serial Monitor's baud rate does not match (e.g., 9600 vs. 115200), the **received bytes are sampled at wrong bit boundaries**, producing **garbled / unreadable characters** in the monitor window. (0.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the Arduino IDE** and describe the role of the **compiler**, **linker**, and **uploader** in the build pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LM35 temperature sensor is connected to analog pin **A0** of an Arduino Uno ($V_{ref} = 5.0\text{ V}$). The sensor outputs **10 mV per °C**. The code reads `analogRead(A0)` and gets a value of **205**.
  **(i)** Calculate the input voltage at A0. **(2 marks)**
  **(ii)** Calculate the corresponding temperature in °C. **(2 marks)**
  **(iii)** Write an Arduino sketch that prints the temperature to the Serial Monitor **once per second**. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Arduino IDE Architecture (7 marks)**

The IDE consists of **four functional modules** (block diagram below):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Editor Pane] --> B[Build System]
    B --> B1[Compiler avr gcc]
    B --> B2[Linker]
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C[avrdude Uploader]
    C --> D[Arduino Board via USB]
    E[Serial Monitor] -. debug channel .-> D
```

1. **Editor (1 mark):** The text-editing pane where the user writes the `.ino` sketch. Supports syntax highlighting, auto-indent, and bracket matching.
2. **Preprocessor (1 mark):** The Arduino builder prepends `#include <Arduino.h>`, resolves user `#include` directives from the `libraries/` folder, and auto-generates function prototypes.
3. **Compiler (`avr-gcc`) (2 marks):** Translates the preprocessed C++ source into AVR architecture object code (`.o` files). Performs syntax checking, type checking, and optimization.
4. **Linker (1 mark):** Combines the user's object files with the **Arduino core library** (`core.a`) — which provides `setup()`, `loop()`, `digitalWrite()`, etc. — into a single **ELF executable**.
5. **Uploader (`avrdude`) (1 mark):** Converts ELF to Intel HEX, then transmits the HEX over the **USB-to-serial bridge** to the **Optiboot bootloader** on the ATmega328P, which writes it to flash memory.
6. **Serial Monitor (1 mark):** A separate GUI panel that opens a virtual COM port at user-specified baud rate (e.g., 9600) to receive and display `Serial.println()` output from the running board.

**(b) LM35 Temperature Calculation & Sketch (7 marks)**

**(i) Input Voltage (2 marks):**

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024} = \frac{205 \times 5.0}{1024} = 1.0010\text{ V}$$

**(ii) Temperature in °C (2 marks):**

Since LM35 outputs 10 mV/°C, the temperature is:

$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{10\text{ mV/°C}} = \frac{1.0010\text{ V}}{0.010\text{ V/°C}} = 100.10\text{ °C} \approx 100.1\text{ °C}$$

**(iii) Complete Arduino Sketch (3 marks):**

```cpp
int sensorPin = A0;
float voltage = 0.0;
float temperatureC = 0.0;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
  int adcValue = analogRead(sensorPin);
  voltage = (adcValue * 5.0) / 1024.0;
  temperatureC = voltage / 0.010;
  Serial.print("Temperature: ");
  Serial.print(temperatureC);
  Serial.println(" C");
  delay(1000);
}
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** of the ATmega328P. Compute the **digital output code** when an analog input of **2.78 V** is applied to pin A0 with $V_{ref} = 5.0\text{ V}$. Also state the **LSB resolution**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** **(i)** Differentiate between **`digitalWrite()`** and **`analogWrite()`**. State which pins support `analogWrite()` on the Arduino Uno. **(3 marks)**
  **(ii)** A sketch uses `analogWrite(9, 192)` with $V_{supply} = 5.0\text{ V}$. Calculate the **duty cycle** and the **average output voltage**. **(2 marks)**
  **(iii)** Write a sketch that **fades an LED on pin 9** from off to full brightness and back, repeating forever, using `analogWrite()` and `delay()`. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) ATmega328P ADC and Calculation (7 marks)**

The ATmega328P contains a **single 10-bit successive-approximation ADC** with **8 multiplexed input channels** (A0–A7). The conversion is governed by the **ADC clock** derived from the system clock via a prescaler (default 128, yielding ~125 kHz). **(2 marks for ADC description)**

The quantization relation is:

$$ADC = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$$

**Digital output code for $V_{in} = 2.78\text{ V}$:** **(3 marks)**

$$ADC = \left\lfloor \frac{2.78}{5.0} \times 1024 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 569.344 \right\rfloor = 569$$

**LSB Resolution:** **(2 marks)**

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} = 4.8828\text{ mV} \approx 4.88\text{ mV}$$

The maximum quantization error is $\pm \frac{1}{2}\text{LSB} = \pm 2.44\text{ mV}$.

---

**(b) PWM and LED Fade Sketch (7 marks)**

**(i) `digitalWrite()` vs `analogWrite()` (3 marks):**

| Property | `digitalWrite()` | `analogWrite()` |
|---|---|---|
| Output type | **Constant** HIGH (5 V) or LOW (0 V) | **PWM square wave** at ~490 Hz or ~980 Hz |
| Pins usable | **Any** digital pin | **Only PWM-capable pins**: 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 on Uno (marked with **~**) |
| Argument | `HIGH` or `LOW` (boolean) | Integer in $[0, 255]$ (8-bit) |
| Use case | Switch LEDs, relays, logic signals | LED dimming, motor speed, servo control |

**(ii) Duty cycle and average voltage for `analogWrite(9, 192)`: (2 marks)**

$$D = \frac{192}{255} = 0.7529 = 75.29\%$$

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply} = 0.7529 \times 5.0 = 3.765\text{ V}$$

**(iii) LED fade sketch: (2 marks)**

```cpp
int ledPin = 9;
int brightness = 0;
int fadeStep = 5;

void setup() {
  pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  analogWrite(ledPin, brightness);
  brightness = brightness + fadeStep;
  if (brightness <= 0 || brightness >= 255) {
    fadeStep = -fadeStep;
  }
  delay(30);
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong formula** — Using $V_{in} = ADC \times V_{ref} \times 1024$ (extra factor). Correct: $V_{in} = ADC \times V_{ref} / 1024$.
> 2. **Forgetting the floor** — Reporting 569.34 instead of 569 for the ADC code.
> 3. **Confusing baud rates** — Writing 9600 as the upload baud rate; the upload uses **115200** (or 57600), while 9600 is for the **Serial Monitor**.
> 4. **Wrong pins for `analogWrite()`** — Listing A0–A5 as PWM pins. Only **digital pins 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11** support PWM on the Uno.
> 5. **Skipping the `void` return type** — Writing `setup() {` instead of `void setup() {`. The C++ compiler accepts both *with* the function prototype auto-injection, but the explicit form is the **KTU-recommended style**.
> 6. **No semicolon in sketch** — A classic 1-mark deduction if the code does not compile.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Arduino IDE** = Editor + Compiler (`avr-gcc`) + Linker + Uploader (`avrdude`) + Serial Monitor in one Java application.
- Sketches are saved as `.ino` files and written in a **simplified C/C++** dialect.
- **Two mandatory functions:** `void setup()` (runs once) and `void loop()` (runs forever).
- **Build pipeline:** Source → Preprocess → Compile (`.o`) → Link (`core.a`) → ELF → HEX → Upload → Flash → Execute.
- **Upload protocol:** STK500 / Optiboot via `avrdude`; **upload baud ≠ Serial Monitor baud**.
- **Digital I/O:** `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, `digitalRead()` — work on any digital pin, signals are 0 V or 5 V.
- **Analog I/O:** `analogRead()` returns $[0, 1023]$ (10-bit); `analogWrite()` outputs PWM at value $[0, 255]$ (8-bit).
- **PWM pins on Uno:** 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 (marked with **~**).
- **Key voltage formulas:**
  - ADC → Voltage: $V_{in} = ADC \times V_{ref} / 1024$
  - PWM → Avg voltage: $V_{avg} = (value/255) \times V_{supply}$
- **LSB resolution of ADC:** $V_{ref} / 1024 \approx 4.88\text{ mV}$ (for 5 V reference).
- **Timing functions:** `delay(ms)` is **blocking**; `millis()` and `micros()` enable **non-blocking** scheduling.
- **Serial debugging:** `Serial.begin(9600)` + `Serial.print()` / `Serial.println()`.
- **Pins A0–A5** can also function as **digital pins 14–19**.
- **The IDE auto-injects** `#include <Arduino.h>` and function prototypes — the user never writes a `main()`.
- **Common libraries:** `Servo.h`, `Wire.h` (I²C), `SPI.h`, `SoftwareSerial.h` — placed in the `libraries/` folder.
- **LM35 sensor scaling:** 10 mV per °C → $T(°C) = V_{in} / 0.010$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sample Code for Interfacing LED and Switch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sample Code for Interfacing LED and Switch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output):** A generic pin on a microcontroller whose behavior—whether input (read) or output (write)—can be controlled by the programmer at runtime via special function registers. In the context of the **8051 microcontroller** (the de-facto KTU reference architecture for PEMET414), GPIO ports are memory-mapped Special Function Registers (SFRs) such as **P0, P1, P2, P3**.

**LED Interfacing** refers to the hardware and software methodology of connecting a Light Emitting Diode to a microcontroller output pin such that the LED state (ON/OFF) is logically controlled by the program executing on the controller.

**Switch Interfacing** refers to the hardware and software methodology of connecting a mechanical push-button switch to a microcontroller input pin such that the switch's pressed/released state can be reliably read as a logic 0 or logic 1 by the program.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Postbox and the Doorbell":**
> - A **microcontroller pin** is like a versatile wall socket that can act as either a **light switch (output)** or a **doorbell button (input)**—you decide its role in code.
> - **An LED** is like a small bulb connected to the wall. When the pin "sends out" a HIGH voltage (like 5V), current flows through the LED and it glows. When the pin sends LOW (0V), no current flows and the LED turns off.
> - **A switch** is like a doorbell. The microcontroller "listens" to the pin. When you press the switch, the pin's voltage changes, and the controller "hears" this change and reacts—just like hearing a doorbell ring.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Parameters to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Standard Logic HIGH voltage (V_OH) on 8051:** **5V DC** (TTL logic family)
> - **Standard Logic LOW voltage (V_OL) on 8051:** **0V DC** (ground reference)
> - **Standard forward voltage (V_F) of a red LED:** **1.8V to 2.2V**
> - **Standard forward current (I_F) of a typical indicator LED:** **10 mA to 20 mA**
> - **Ohm's Law for current-limiting resistor:** $V = I \times R$

## 1.4 The Big Picture: Where Does This Fit?

LED and Switch interfacing is the **"Hello World"** of embedded systems. It establishes the foundational I/O model used in **every** intelligent device—from microwave oven keypads to automotive dashboard indicators, from IoT sensor nodes to industrial PLCs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hardware Theory: The LED Circuit

An LED is a **non-ohmic** (polarity-sensitive) diode. It MUST be connected with a current-limiting resistor in series, otherwise it will draw excessive current and burn out.

### Circuit Operation Logic (LED ON):
1. Microcontroller pin outputs **HIGH (5V)**.
2. Current flows: $V_{CC} \rightarrow \text{Pin} \rightarrow R_{series} \rightarrow \text{LED Anode} \rightarrow \text{LED Cathode} \rightarrow GND$
3. LED glows.

### Circuit Operation Logic (LED OFF):
1. Microcontroller pin outputs **LOW (0V)**.
2. No potential difference; no current flows.
3. LED remains dark.

## 2.2 Hardware Theory: The Switch Circuit

A mechanical switch is a **bouncing** contact. There are two standard interfacing configurations:

| Configuration | Active State | Pull Resistor | Pin Logic When Pressed |
|---------------|--------------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Active-LOW (with pull-up)** | Pressed = 0 | Pull-up to V_CC | Logic **0 (LOW)** |
| **Active-HIGH (with pull-down)** | Pressed = 1 | Pull-down to GND | Logic **1 (HIGH)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite:** The **Active-LOW configuration with external pull-up resistor (10 kΩ)** is overwhelmingly preferred in KTU 8051 exam questions because the 8051's P1, P2, P3 ports have **built-in internal pull-ups**, while P0 does not.

## 2.3 Software Theory: SFR Bit Manipulation in 8051

The 8051 uses **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** to control ports:

- **P1, P2, P3** — Read input / write output
- **P0** — Open-drain; needs external pull-ups for output

**Bit-wise operations** (essential for KTU coding questions):
- `P1 = 0xFF;` — Sets ALL 8 pins of Port 1 to HIGH
- `P1 = 0x00;` — Sets ALL 8 pins of Port 1 to LOW
- `P1 = P1 | 0x01;` — Sets ONLY pin P1.0 to HIGH (mask = 0x01)
- `P1 = P1 & 0xFE;` — Clears ONLY pin P1.0 to LOW (mask = 0xFE)
- `if (P1 & 0x01)` — Tests whether P1.0 is HIGH (input read)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes every formula you need for LED/Switch interfacing in KTU exams:**

| # | Concept | Formula / Constant | Typical KTU Value | Unit |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------------|------|
| 1 | Ohm's Law (Resistor Value) | $R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$ | $\frac{5V - 2V}{10mA}$ | **Ω (Ohms)** |
| 2 | Power dissipated in resistor | $P_R = I_F^2 \times R$ | $(0.01)^2 \times 330$ | **W (Watts)** |
| 3 | Power dissipated in LED | $P_{LED} = V_F \times I_F$ | $2 \times 0.01$ | **W (Watts)** |
| 4 | Debounce delay (typical) | Software delay loop | 10 – 50 | **ms (milliseconds)** |
| 5 | Pull-up / Pull-down resistor | Standard value | 10 | **kΩ** |
| 6 | Current limiting resistor | Standard value | 220 – 470 | **Ω** |
| 7 | 8051 V_OH (Logic HIGH) | TTL Standard | **5** | **V** |
| 8 | 8051 V_IL (Logic LOW max) | TTL Standard | **0.8** | **V** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> This fundamental I/O model scales to:
> - **Automotive dashboards**: LEDs indicate turn signals, warnings; switches operate wipers, headlights.
> - **Medical devices**: LED status indicators, push-button patient/nurse call systems.
> - **Industrial control panels**: LED tower lights, push-button operator stations.
> - **IoT smart homes**: Relay-controlled lights (driven like LEDs) and wall switches (read like buttons).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Code Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Hardware Setup Assumed

> Connect LED to **P1.0** through a 330 Ω resistor to GND (active-HIGH driving).
> Connect push-button switch to **P3.0** with a 10 kΩ pull-up to V_CC (active-LOW reading).

## 3.2 Program 1: Simple LED Blinking (Output Only)

**Objective:** Toggle LED on P1.0 at a visible 1 Hz rate.

```c
#include <reg51.h>   // Header file for 8051 SFR definitions

sbit LED = P1^0;      // Define LED as bit-addressable pin P1.0

void delay(void);     // Function prototype

void main(void)
{
    while(1)          // Infinite loop — embedded systems run forever
    {
        LED = 1;      // Step 1: Drive P1.0 HIGH → LED turns ON
        delay();      // Step 2: Wait (LED visible ON for ~500 ms)
        LED = 0;      // Step 3: Drive P1.0 LOW → LED turns OFF
        delay();      // Step 4: Wait (LED OFF for ~500 ms)
    }
}

/* Software delay using nested loops.
   For 11.0592 MHz crystal, this generates ~500 ms delay. */
void delay(void)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < 500; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}
```

### Exhaustive Logic Breakdown:

- **Line `sbit LED = P1^0;`** — Creates a bit-addressable alias so we can write `LED = 1;` instead of manipulating the entire P1 register. This is a 8051-specific feature using the Keil C51 compiler extensions.
- **Line `while(1)`** — In embedded systems, `main()` is **NEVER allowed to return**. The `while(1)` creates an infinite super-loop.
- **Line `LED = 1;`** — Sets bit 0 of Port 1 to logic 1, sourcing current into the LED circuit.
- **Line `delay();`** — Software busy-wait loop. For 8051 at 11.0592 MHz, machine cycle = 12/11.0592 MHz ≈ 1.085 µs. Total inner iterations = 500 × 1275 = 637,500. Total delay ≈ 637,500 × 1.085 µs ≈ **691 ms** (acceptable for blinking).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Derivation of Delay:**
> $$\text{Total Cycles} = i_{max} \times j_{max} = 500 \times 1275 = 637500$$
> $$\text{Delay Time} = 637500 \times T_{machine} = 637500 \times 1.085 \mu s$$
> $$\boxed{\text{Delay Time} \approx 691 \text{ ms}}$$

## 3.3 Program 2: Switch-Controlled LED (Input + Output)

**Objective:** LED on P1.0 turns ON only while switch on P3.0 is pressed.

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED   = P1^0;    // LED output pin
sbit SW    = P3^0;    // Switch input pin (active-LOW)

void main(void)
{
    SW = 1;           // Configure P3.0 as input (write 1 to make port "read mode")
                      // Note: 8051 P1, P2, P3 are quasi-bidirectional; writing 1
                      // to the bit disables the internal pull-down driver.

    while(1)
    {
        if(SW == 0)            // Step A: Is switch pressed? (active-LOW)
        {
            LED = 1;           // Step B: Yes → LED ON
        }
        else
        {
            LED = 0;           // Step C: No  → LED OFF
        }
    }
}
```

### Exhaustive Logic Breakdown:

- **`SW = 1;`** — On 8051, ports P1, P2, P3 must be set to **1** before reading them, to put the internal pull-up MOSFET in high-impedance "read" mode.
- **`if(SW == 0)`** — Tests the bit. If the switch is **NOT pressed**, the 10 kΩ external pull-up keeps the pin at V_CC = 5V, so SW reads **1**. If **pressed**, the switch shorts the pin to GND, so SW reads **0**. Hence "active-LOW".
- **No delay** — Because we're polling in a tight loop, the LED reacts instantly.

## 3.4 Program 3: Toggle LED on Switch Press (with Debounce)

**Objective:** Each press of the switch toggles the LED (ON → OFF → ON). Software debounce is implemented.

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;
sbit SW  = P3^0;

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms);

void main(void)
{
    SW = 1;
    while(1)
    {
        if(SW == 0)                        // Step 1: Detect press
        {
            delay_ms(20);                  // Step 2: Debounce wait (20 ms)
            if(SW == 0)                    // Step 3: Confirm still pressed
            {
                LED = ~LED;                // Step 4: Toggle LED
                while(SW == 0);            // Step 5: Wait for release
            }
        }
    }
}

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}
```

### Exhaustive Debounce Logic:

1. **Mechanical bounce:** When a switch is pressed, the metal contacts physically bounce for 5–20 ms, creating spurious LOW-HIGH-LOW-HIGH transitions.
2. **Step 1** detects an initial LOW.
3. **Step 2** waits 20 ms, allowing the bouncing to settle.
4. **Step 3** re-checks: if still LOW, it's a genuine press.
5. **Step 4** executes the toggle.
6. **Step 5** blocks (busy-wait) until the user releases the switch, preventing **one press** from being interpreted as **multiple presses**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: KTU-Mandated Concepts

| Concept | 8051 Implementation | Common Mistake in KTU Exams |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Pin as Output | Write 0 to the bit | Writing 1 to read (confusing input/output) |
| Pin as Input | Write 1 to the bit first | Forgetting to set bit before reading |
| Active-LOW switch reads | `if(SW == 0)` | Writing `if(SW == 1)` by mistake |
| LED ON (active-HIGH) | `LED = 1;` | Forgetting current-limiting resistor |
| Debounce | 10–50 ms software delay | Using 1 µs delay (too short) |
| Infinite loop | `while(1) { }` | Letting `main()` return |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU["8051 Microcontroller"] -- P1.0 output --> LEDBlock["LED Driver Branch"]
    MCU -- P3.0 input --> SWBlock["Switch Input Branch"]
    LEDBlock --> R1["330 ohm Resistor"]
    R1 --> D1["LED Indicator"]
    D1 --> GND1["Ground"]
    SWBlock --> SW1["Push Button Switch"]
    SW1 --> GND2["Ground"]
    VCC["+5V Supply"] --> R2["10k Pull-up Resistor"]
    R2 --> SW1
```

## 4.2 Program 1 Flowchart: LED Blinking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Start main"]) --> Init["Configure P1.0 as output"]
    Init --> LoopStart{"while(1) loop"}
    LoopStart --> LEDOn["LED = 1 - Drive HIGH"]
    LEDOn --> Delay1["delay 500 ms"]
    Delay1 --> LEDOff["LED = 0 - Drive LOW"]
    LEDOff --> Delay2["delay 500 ms"]
    Delay2 --> LoopStart
```

## 4.3 Program 3 Flowchart: Debounced Toggle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Start main"]) --> Cfg["SW = 1, Configure input"]
    Cfg --> CheckPress{"SW == 0 ?"}
    CheckPress -- No --> CheckPress
    CheckPress -- Yes --> D1["delay 20 ms debounce"]
    D1 --> Confirm{"SW == 0 again ?"}
    Confirm -- No --> CheckPress
    Confirm -- Yes --> Toggle["LED = NOT LED"]
    Toggle --> WaitRel{"Wait while SW == 0"}
    WaitRel -- Yes --> WaitRel
    WaitRel -- No --> CheckPress
```

## 4.4 Bit-Mask Logic Visualization (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["P1 Register 8 bits"] --> A7["Bit 7"]
    A --> A0["Bit 0 = LED"]
    Op1["Mask 0x01 = 00000001b"] --> Or["P1 = P1 OR 0x01"]
    A --> Or
    Or --> Result["Result: Only bit 0 set to 1"]
    Op2["Mask 0xFE = 11111110b"] --> And["P1 = P1 AND 0xFE"]
    A --> And
    And --> Result2["Result: Only bit 0 cleared to 0"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. What is a current-limiting resistor? Why is it essential when interfacing an LED to a microcontroller pin? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A current-limiting resistor is a series resistor connected between the microcontroller I/O pin and the LED (or between the LED and ground). It is **essential** because an LED is a non-ohmic device with a very low dynamic resistance once forward-biased. Without a series resistor, the current would exceed the LED's maximum rated forward current (typically 20 mA), causing permanent thermal damage.

The resistor value is calculated using **Ohm's Law**:

$$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{5V - 2V}{10mA} = 300\ \Omega$$

A standard **330 Ω** resistor is commonly used in KTU 8051-based designs.

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. Differentiate between **Active-HIGH** and **Active-LOW** switch configurations. Which one is preferred with the 8051 microcontroller and why? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Active-HIGH | Active-LOW |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| Pull resistor | Pull-down (to GND) | Pull-up (to V_CC) |
| Logic when pressed | 1 (HIGH) | 0 (LOW) |
| Logic when released | 0 (LOW) | 1 (HIGH) |
| Code test | `if(SW == 1)` | `if(SW == 0)` |

**Preferred for 8051:** **Active-LOW** configuration. **Reason:** The 8051's ports P1, P2, P3 have **built-in internal pull-up transistors** that are activated by writing 1 to the port bit, making active-LOW input interfacing natural and requiring no external pull-up in most simple designs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the interfacing of an LED to Port 1 pin P1.0 of an 8051 microcontroller. Calculate the value of the current-limiting resistor for an LED with V_F = 2V and I_F = 15 mA. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Write an 8051 C program to read a push-button switch connected to P3.7 (active-LOW) and glow an LED connected to P2.0 only when the switch is pressed. Use software debounce of 20 ms. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Circuit Diagram (textual representation):**
```
       P1.0  o----[330 Ω]---->|----+
                                LED  |
                                 v  |
       (P1.0 is anode-side driver)  GND
```

**Resistor calculation:**

$$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{5 - 2}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3}{0.015} = 200\ \Omega$$

A standard **220 Ω** resistor is chosen for practical use.

**Working:** When P1.0 = 1, current flows from the pin through the resistor and LED to ground, lighting the LED. When P1.0 = 0, no potential difference exists, and the LED remains OFF.

**Power dissipation in resistor:**

$$P_R = I_F^2 \times R = (0.015)^2 \times 220 = 49.5\ mW$$

A 1/8 W (125 mW) resistor is more than sufficient.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P2^0;
sbit SW  = P3^7;

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms);

void main(void)
{
    SW = 1;          // P3.7 as input (enable internal pull-up)
    LED = 0;         // LED initially OFF
    while(1)
    {
        if(SW == 0)            // Press detected
        {
            delay_ms(20);      // Debounce
            if(SW == 0)        // Confirmed press
            {
                LED = 1;       // LED ON
            }
        }
        else
        {
            LED = 0;           // LED OFF
        }
    }
}

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}
```

**Key points:**
- `sbit` keyword (Keil C51 specific) creates a bit-addressable variable.
- `SW = 1;` puts P3.7 in read mode by activating the internal pull-up.
- The double-check pattern (debounce + confirm) prevents false triggers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**a)** Explain the concept of **switch debouncing** in microcontroller-based systems. Compare **hardware** and **software debouncing** techniques. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Design and write an 8051 C program to implement a **4-bit binary counter** displayed on 4 LEDs connected to P1.0–P1.3. The counter should increment every 500 ms using Timer 0 in mode 1. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Switch bouncing:** Mechanical push-button switches have metal contacts that physically bounce (open and close rapidly) for 5–20 ms after a press or release. The microcontroller, running at MHz speeds, reads these bounces as **multiple spurious transitions**, causing incorrect counts or repeated actions.

**Hardware debouncing techniques:**
1. **RC low-pass filter** + Schmitt trigger (e.g., 7414) — Filters high-frequency bounce noise.
2. **SR flip-flop** (latch) — Two cross-coupled NOR/NAND gates hold the stable state.
3. **Dedicated debounce IC** — e.g., MC14490 (Hex debouncer).

**Software debouncing techniques:**
1. **Time-delay method** — Wait 10–50 ms after the first transition, then re-sample.
2. **Counter method** — Count consistent identical samples before accepting a transition.
3. **State-machine method** — Track press/release states with timing thresholds.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Hardware | Software |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Cost | Higher (extra components) | Free |
| Reliability | Very high | High (with good algorithm) |
| Flexibility | Fixed at design time | Tunable in code |
| Latency | Negligible | 10–50 ms added delay |
| KTU exam preference | Mention briefly | Preferred for full marks |

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Hardware setup:** 4 LEDs with 330 Ω resistors on P1.0, P1.1, P1.2, P1.3 (LSB to MSB).

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void timer0_delay_500ms(void);

void main(void)
{
    unsigned char count = 0;
    while(1)
    {
        P1 = (P1 & 0xF0) | (count & 0x0F);  // Display count on lower nibble
        count++;
        if(count > 15) count = 0;            // Wrap around 4 bits
        timer0_delay_500ms();
    }
}

void timer0_delay_500ms(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;       // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0xFC;       // Load high byte for 500 ms
    TL0  = 0x18;       // Load low byte for 500 ms
    TR0  = 1;          // Start Timer 0
    while(TF0 == 0);   // Wait for overflow flag
    TR0  = 0;          // Stop Timer 0
    TF0  = 0;          // Clear overflow flag
}
```

**Timer calculation (for 11.0592 MHz crystal):**
- Machine cycle = 12 / 11.0592 MHz = 1.085 µs
- Required count = 500 ms / 1.085 µs = 460,800 cycles
- 16-bit timer max = 65,536. So overflows must occur 460,800 / 65,536 ≈ 7.03 times.
- Pre-load value: 65,536 − 7 ≈ 65,529 = 0xFFE9
- Hence: `TH0 = 0xFF; TL0 = 0xE9;` (One common variant)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `SW = 1;` before reading P3.x** — Costs 1 mark. The 8051 needs the bit pre-set to "1" for read mode.
> 2. **Wrong logic test for active-LOW switch** — Writing `if(SW == 1)` instead of `if(SW == 0)`. Costs 1 mark.
> 3. **No debounce delay** — For toggle-on-press programs, omitting the 10–50 ms delay is a **2-mark deduction** as it shows incomplete understanding.
> 4. **Forgetting `while(1)`** in `main()` — A returning `main()` is conceptually wrong in embedded C. Costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Wrong resistor calculation units** — Mixing mA and A. Always convert I_F to **amperes** before substituting in Ohm's Law.
> 6. **Forgetting `#include <reg51.h>`** — Without this, `P1`, `P3`, `sbit` keywords are undefined. The program will not compile.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LED and Switch Interfacing**

- [x] **8051 ports P1, P2, P3** have internal pull-ups; **P0 is open-drain** and needs external pull-ups.
- [x] To **read** a port, first **write 1** to it (to disable the output driver).
- [x] To **write** a port, simply assign the value: `P1 = 0xFF;`
- [x] **Active-LOW switch** (with pull-up) reads `0` when pressed; preferred for 8051.
- [x] **Active-HIGH switch** (with pull-down) reads `1` when pressed.
- [x] LED needs a **current-limiting resistor**: typical value **220 Ω to 470 Ω**.
- [x] Resistor value: $R = (V_{CC} - V_F) / I_F$
- [x] Standard values: V_CC = **5V**, V_F (red LED) = **2V**, I_F = **10–20 mA**.
- [x] **Debounce time** in software: typically **10–50 ms**.
- [x] **Bit manipulation** uses masks: `OR` to set, `AND` to clear, `XOR` to toggle.
- [x] The `sbit` keyword in Keil C51 provides bit-level access: `sbit LED = P1^0;`
- [x] Embedded `main()` MUST contain an **infinite loop** (`while(1)`); it must never return.
- [x] The three-step debounce pattern: **detect → delay → confirm**.
- [x] For multi-press counters, add a **wait-for-release** block to prevent double-counting.
- [x] **Polling** = continuously checking the input bit; **Interrupt** = hardware-driven notification (covered in higher modules).
- [x] Always include `#include <reg51.h>` at the top of every 8051 C program.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor and Stepper Motor Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DC Motor and Stepper Motor Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 DC Motor — Formal KTU Definition

A **DC Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that operates on the principle of Lorentz force, converting direct-current electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy at the shaft. In the context of *Intelligent Systems*, the DC motor is treated as a controllable actuator whose speed, torque, and direction are regulated by varying the **armature voltage**, the **field flux (φ)**, or the **armature current (Iₐ)**, typically through a closed-loop feedback controller (PI / PID) implemented on a microcontroller, PLC, or DSP.

The governing DC motor equations used in KTU board problems are:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

$$E_b = K_e \, \phi \, \omega$$

$$T_m = K_t \, \phi \, I_a$$

> [!NOTE]
> In SI units, **Kₑ = K_t** (the back-EMF constant equals the torque constant in consistent SI). The field flux **φ** in Weber (**Wb**), the armature current **Iₐ** in Amperes (**A**), the speed **N** in RPM, and the angular velocity **ω** in rad/s.

### 1.2 Stepper Motor — Formal KTU Definition

A **Stepper Motor** (also called a *step motor* or *stepping motor*) is a brushless, synchronous electromechanical actuator that converts a train of digital pulses into discrete, incremental mechanical shaft rotations called **steps**. Each electrical pulse supplied to its stator winding produces a precisely defined angular displacement known as the **step angle (θₛ)**. Because position is a direct digital function of the number of input pulses, the stepper motor is an *open-loop position actuator* — no shaft encoder or position sensor is required for positioning.

Step angle for a standard stepper:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$$

where **Nᵣ** is the number of rotor teeth and **m** is the number of stator phases (or steps per revolution for a hybrid motor).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Water Tap (DC Motor) & The Escalator (Stepper Motor):**
>
> - A **DC motor behaves like a continuously variable water tap**: turn the knob smoothly, and the flow (speed) changes smoothly. PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) is like *chopping your finger rapidly on the tap* — the water spurts in pulses, but the *average* flow changes, so the output speed is controlled.
> - A **Stepper motor behaves like a digital escalator with discrete steps**: every electrical pulse moves the escalator by *exactly one step* (e.g., 1.8°). You cannot move by 0.5° unless you use **microstepping**. Open-loop = no one is checking whether the escalator actually moved; we just trust the pulse.

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot (For Quick Reference)

| Motor Family | Sub-Type | Key KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Motor** | Separately Excited, Self Excited (Shunt / Series / Compound) | Armature control, Field control, Ward-Leonard |
| **DC Motor (Modern Control)** | PMDC, Brushless DC (BLDC) | PWM with H-Bridge |
| **Stepper Motor** | Variable Reluctance (VR) | Rotor seeks lowest reluctance path |
| **Stepper Motor** | Permanent Magnet (PM) | Polarized rotor, 48–100 steps/rev |
| **Stepper Motor** | Hybrid (HY) | Toothed rotor + permanent magnet, 1.8° typical |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC Motor — Operating Principle

A DC motor has two windings:
1. **Field winding** (stator) — produces the magnetic flux **φ**.
2. **Armature winding** (rotor) — carries current **Iₐ** that interacts with **φ** to produce torque.

**The "Why" of operation:**
- When a current-carrying conductor sits in a magnetic field, it experiences a force **F = BIL**.
- Bundling many such conductors on a rotating armature produces a **net torque (Tₘ)**.
- Rotation cuts the flux, generating a **back-EMF (E_b)** that opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's Law). This back-EMF is the *natural feedback* that limits the no-load speed.

### 2.2 DC Motor Speed Control — Three Classic Methods

**Method 1 — Armature Voltage Control** *(most widely used in modern drives)*

$$N \propto \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{\phi}$$

Vary **Vₐ** while keeping **φ** constant → linear speed control.

**Method 2 — Field Flux Control (Weakening)**

Vary **φ** while **Vₐ** is constant. As **φ** decreases, **N** increases — *speed increases above base speed*.

**Method 3 — Ward-Leonard System** *(classical, exam-favorite)*

Uses a **separately excited DC generator** to feed the motor armature. The generator's field is varied to give a smooth, wide-range, regenerative speed control. Although bulky, it appears in KTU problems as the gold standard for analog DC drives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Armature control = below base speed* and *Field control = above base speed*. Constant torque region vs. constant power region.

### 2.3 PWM Control of DC Motor (Modern Digital Method)

Since the DC motor's average speed is proportional to average armature voltage, we use **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** to vary the average voltage efficiently (transistor is either fully ON or fully OFF — minimal power dissipation).

Average armature voltage:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$$

Duty cycle definition:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}$$

where **Tₛ** is the switching period and **Tₒₙ** is the ON-time. Switching frequency typically **1 kHz – 25 kHz** to be above audible range.

### 2.4 H-Bridge for Bidirectional Control

A single transistor can only drive current in one direction. To **reverse** a DC motor, we use an **H-Bridge** of four switches (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4):
- Q1 & Q4 ON → current flows left-to-right → motor rotates **forward**.
- Q2 & Q3 ON → current flows right-to-left → motor rotates **reverse**.
- Never close Q1&Q2 or Q3&Q4 simultaneously → **shoot-through** (short circuit).

### 2.5 Stepper Motor — Operating Principles

**Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper:**
- Soft-iron toothed rotor, no permanent magnet.
- The rotor *always* aligns with the stator phase having the **lowest magnetic reluctance** (path of maximum permeance).
- Step angle: **θₛ = 360° / (Nᵣ × phases)**

**Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper:**
- Rotor is a permanent magnet (axially or radially polarized).
- Step angle is coarser (typically 48 steps/rev = 7.5° or 100 steps/rev = 3.6°).
- Better holding torque than VR.

**Hybrid Stepper (industry standard):**
- Combines PM + toothed rotor → very fine steps (**1.8° = 200 steps/rev** is industry default).
- Best static and dynamic torque.

### 2.6 Stepper Excitation Modes

| Excitation Mode | Sequence Type | Effective Step | Torque | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wave (One-Phase-On)** | 1 phase energized at a time | Full step | Lowest | Lowest power dissipation |
| **Full-Step (Two-Phase-On)** | 2 phases energized | Full step | Highest | Most holding torque |
| **Half-Step** | Alternates 1 and 2 phase | Half step | Intermediate | Doubles resolution |
| **Microstepping** | Sine-cosine current ratio | Fractional | Smooth, but reduced per-step torque | 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 common |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Use \vert for absolute value — NO pipes!)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | KVL across armature | V |
| 2 | $E_b = K_e \, \phi \, \omega$ | Back-EMF equation | V |
| 3 | $T_m = K_t \, \phi \, I_a$ | Developed electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | rad/s |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e \, \phi}$ | Speed equation (no-load: Iₐ≈0) | RPM |
| 6 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ | PWM average output | V |
| 7 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}}$ | Duty cycle definition | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$ | Stepper step angle | degrees |
| 9 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{\omega}{\theta_s} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180}$ | Required step pulse rate | Hz |
| 10 | $T_{hold} = \dfrac{N_r \, m \, F_m \, r}{2}$ | Static holding torque (qualitative) | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Use:** PWM + H-Bridge DC drives are used in **electric vehicles, robotic wheels, CNC spindle drives, and drone propulsion**. Stepper motors are used in **3D printers, CNC feed axes, telescope mounts, camera pan-tilt heads, and disk drive head positioning** — anywhere *precise open-loop positioning* matters more than raw speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Motor Equations to Speed–Torque Characteristic

We want the **speed (N) as a function of load torque (T_L)**. This is the single most important KTU derivation for the DC motor.

**Step 1: Start with the KVL of the armature circuit**

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2: Substitute the back-EMF expression**

$$V_a = K_e \, \phi \, \omega + I_a R_a$$

**Step 3: Solve for ω (angular velocity)**

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e \, \phi}$$

**Step 4: Convert ω to RPM using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$**

$$\frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e \, \phi}$$

**Step 5: Isolate N**

$$N = \frac{60 \, (V_a - I_a R_a)}{2\pi \, K_e \, \phi} = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e \, \phi} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi}$$

**Step 6: Substitute torque relation $I_a = \dfrac{T_m}{K_t \, \phi}$**

$$N = \frac{V_a}{K_e \, \phi} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} - \frac{R_a}{K_e \, \phi} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{T_m}{K_t \, \phi}$$

**Step 7: Recognize that $K_e = K_t$ in SI units, and at steady state $T_m = T_L$**

$$N = \frac{60 \, V_a}{2\pi \, K_t \, \phi} - \frac{60 \, R_a}{2\pi \, K_t^{\,2} \, \phi^{2}} \cdot T_L$$

This is the **linear speed–torence characteristic**:
- **N₀** (no-load speed) = first term (intercept on the N-axis).
- **Slope** = negative second term (linearly drops with T_L).
- **Stall torque** $T_{stall} = \dfrac{V_a \, K_t \, \phi}{R_a}$ (when N = 0).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the derivation examiners love — practice drawing the N vs T_L straight line graph and label **N₀** and **T_stall**.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A 220 V, 1000 RPM DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of **0.5 Ω** and field resistance **220 Ω**. At rated load, the armature current is **20 A**. Compute:
(i) Back-EMF at rated load
(ii) Developed torque
(iii) No-load speed (assume field flux constant and armature current at no load ≈ 2 A)

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**(i) Back-EMF E_b:**

$$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 220 - (20)(0.5) = 220 - 10 = 210 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Developed torque T_m:**

First, compute the field flux indirectly via rated-speed back-EMF:

$$K_e \, \phi = \frac{E_b}{\omega} = \frac{210}{\frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60}} = \frac{210}{104.72} = 2.005 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

Now the developed torque:

$$T_m = K_t \, \phi \, I_a = 2.005 \times 20 = 40.1 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) No-load speed:**

At no-load, taking Iₐ ≈ 2 A:

$$E_{b,nl} = 220 - (2)(0.5) = 219 \text{ V}$$

$$\omega_{nl} = \frac{E_{b,nl}}{K_e \, \phi} = \frac{219}{2.005} = 109.23 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_{nl} = \frac{109.23 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 1043 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The no-load speed is only slightly higher than the rated speed (≈ 4.3%). This is characteristic of a **shunt motor**, which has a near-constant speed characteristic — making it the *workhorse of industry*.

### 3.3 Derivation: Stepper Motor Step Angle for a Hybrid Motor

A hybrid stepper has Nᵣ rotor teeth and m stator phases. For one full electrical cycle (e.g., A → B → A̅ → B̅), the rotor advances by **one tooth pitch** = **360°/Nᵣ**. In a 2-phase motor, one full electrical cycle is **4 steps**, and one tooth pitch is traversed per cycle.

So, per step:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}/N_r}{4} = \frac{90^{\circ}}{N_r}$$

For a **200-step motor** (industry standard, 1.8°/step):

$$N_r = \frac{90^{\circ}}{1.8^{\circ}} = 50 \text{ teeth}$$

**Verification using the unified formula:**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m} = \frac{360}{50 \times 4} = \frac{360}{200} = 1.8^{\circ} \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Motor for Which Application?

| Application | Required Property | Best Motor | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conveyor belt | Constant torque, variable speed | DC Shunt / PMDC | Linear speed-torque, easy PWM |
| Electric vehicle traction | High starting torque, wide speed range | DC Series (old) / BLDC (new) | Series gives high Iₐ at low speeds |
| 3D printer X-Y axis | Open-loop precision, holding torque | Hybrid Stepper | Self-locking, no encoder needed |
| Hard-disk head | Micro-positioning | Hybrid stepper (microstepped) | Step angle as low as 0.056° |
| Drone propeller | High speed, light, efficient | BLDC (outrunner) | High power density |
| Quartz watch / Clock | Tiny precise steps | PM Stepper (small) | Small, battery-friendly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop DC Motor Speed Control (PWM + PID)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref["Reference Speed Nref"] --> err["Summing Junction"]
    fb["Tachogenerator Feedback Nb"] --> err
    err --> ctrl["PID Controller"]
    ctrl --> pwm["PWM Generator Block"]
    pwm --> hb["H Bridge Power Stage"]
    hb --> motor["DC Motor M"]
    motor --> load["Mechanical Load"]
    load --> fb
    motor --> fb
    style ref fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00
    style ctrl fill:#d2e0fb,stroke:#06c
    style hb fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#a80
    style motor fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#2a2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The user sets a reference speed **N_ref**.
- A **tachogenerator** (or optical encoder) measures the actual speed **N_b** and feeds it back.
- The **PID controller** computes an error **e = N_ref − N_b** and outputs a control signal.
- The **PWM block** converts the analog control signal into a duty cycle **D**.
- The **H-Bridge** applies a pulsed voltage of average **D·V_s** to the motor armature.
- The motor's shaft speed adjusts → loop continues until **e ≈ 0**.

### 4.2 Schematic — H-Bridge Bidirectional DC Motor Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC["Vsupply plus"] --> Q1["Q1 PNP Top Left"]
    VCC --> Q2["Q2 PNP Top Right"]
    Q1 --> Mleft["Motor Terminal A"]
    Q2 --> Mright["Motor Terminal B"]
    Mleft --> Q3["Q3 NPN Bottom Left"]
    Mright --> Q4["Q4 NPN Bottom Right"]
    Q3 --> GND["Ground"]
    Q4 --> GND
    subgraph Forward
        direction LR
        F1["Q1 ON Q4 ON"] --> FDIR["Current A to B Forward Rotation"]
    end
    subgraph Reverse
        direction LR
        R1["Q2 ON Q3 ON"] --> RDIR["Current B to A Reverse Rotation"]
    end
    style VCC fill:#ffe0b3
    style GND fill:#cccccc
```

**Critical rule:** **Never** activate (Q1 & Q2) or (Q3 & Q4) at the same time — this creates a *shoot-through* short from V_supply to GND, destroying the transistors. All KTU answers should mention this!

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Stepper Motor Drive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    idx["Index Pulse Source"] --> ctrl["MCU or Stepper Driver IC"]
    dir["Direction Signal DIR"] --> ctrl
    ena["Enable Signal EN"] --> ctrl
    ctrl --> phaseA["Phase A Coil Driver"]
    ctrl --> phaseB["Phase B Coil Driver"]
    phaseA --> coilA["Coil A Winding"]
    phaseB --> coilB["Coil B Winding"]
    coilA --> rotor["Rotor Shaft"]
    coilB --> rotor
    rotor --> mech["Mechanical Output"]
    style ctrl fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003
    style rotor fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#060
```

### 4.4 Timing Diagram — Full-Step vs Half-Step Excitation (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Step Number: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8"] --> s2["Full Step Wave: A B C D A B C D"]
    s1 --> s3["Full Step TwoPhase: AB BC CD DA AB BC CD DA"]
    s1 --> s4["Half Step Sequence: A AB B BC C CD D DA A AB"]
    style s1 fill:#fff8b3
    style s2 fill:#e0f0ff
    style s3 fill:#e0ffe0
    style s4 fill:#ffe0f0
```

**Reading:** The half-step sequence smoothly interleaves one-phase and two-phase energization, doubling the resolution from 200 to 400 steps/rev (for a 1.8° motor → 0.9° effective step).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State any three advantages of PWM speed control of a DC motor over the conventional linear (rheostatic) armature control method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher efficiency** — in PWM, the controlling transistor is either in cutoff or saturation, so the power dissipation (V·I) is very low; in rheostatic control, the resistor wastes $I_a^2 R$ as heat. *(1 mark)*
2. **Compact and lightweight** — a single MOSFET + small heat-sink replaces bulky power rheostats. *(1 mark)*
3. **Fast dynamic response and easy digital interfacing** — the duty cycle is set by a microcontroller timer, enabling closed-loop PID and computer-controlled set-points. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "step angle" of a stepper motor. A hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth and 4 stator phases. Compute its step angle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition** *(1 mark)*: Step angle is the discrete angular displacement of the rotor shaft produced by a single input pulse applied to the stator windings.
- **Formula** *(1 mark)*: $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m} = \dfrac{360}{50 \times 4}$
- **Result** *(1 mark)*: $\theta_s = 1.8^{\circ}$

---

### **PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *DC Motor Speed Control*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*With the help of a neat circuit, explain the working of an H-bridge circuit used for bidirectional speed control of a separately excited DC motor. Why is PWM used in conjunction with the H-bridge?*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**
1. **Circuit explanation (4 marks):** The H-bridge consists of four power switches (Q1–Q4) arranged in an *H* shape with the motor on the central branch. When **Q1 and Q4 are ON**, current flows from V_supply through Q1 → motor terminal A → motor terminal B → Q4 → GND, rotating the motor in the **forward** direction. When **Q2 and Q3 are ON**, current reverses direction, producing **reverse** rotation. The diagonal pairs are switched by complementary control signals; **Q1 and Q2 (or Q3 and Q4) are never ON simultaneously** to avoid *shoot-through*.
2. **Why PWM (3 marks):** The H-bridge switches can be modulated with a high-frequency PWM signal. The motor's inductance filters the pulses, and the **average armature voltage is $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$**, where D is the duty cycle. This achieves:
   - **Continuous speed variation** from 0 to V_supply.
   - **High efficiency** because switches are fully ON or fully OFF.
   - **Precise digital control** via microcontroller-generated PWM.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*A 220 V, 1500 RPM DC shunt motor has Rₐ = 0.3 Ω and Rₛₕ = 110 Ω. The field flux is reduced by 10% while the armature current at a given load remains 25 A. Find: (i) the new back-EMF, (ii) the new speed, (iii) the percentage change in speed. Take armature reaction as negligible.*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**
Given: V = 220 V, N₁ = 1500 RPM, Rₐ = 0.3 Ω, Iₐ = 25 A, φ₂ = 0.9 φ₁.

**(i) New back-EMF** *(3 marks)*:
Since the armature current is the same and the field flux change does not enter the KVL of the armature (back-EMF depends on V and IₐRₐ), but flux changes do affect E_b indirectly:
- Original: $E_{b1} = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (25)(0.3) = 220 - 7.5 = 212.5 \text{ V}$
- New: $E_{b2} = V - I_a R_a = 212.5 \text{ V}$ *(unchanged, since V and Iₐ are constant)*

**(ii) New speed** *(3 marks)*:
Using $E_b = K_e \, \phi \, \omega$, and noting $E_{b2} = E_{b1}$:
$$K_e \phi_2 \omega_2 = K_e \phi_1 \omega_1$$
$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{\phi_1}{\phi_2} = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{1}{0.9} = 1.111 \, \omega_1$$
$$N_2 = 1.111 \times 1500 = 1666.7 \text{ RPM}$$

**(iii) Percentage change** *(1 mark)*:
$$\% \Delta N = \frac{1666.7 - 1500}{1500} \times 100\% = +11.11\%$$

The speed **increases** because flux weakening reduces the magnetic loading, allowing higher speed at the same voltage — this is the classic *field-weakening region (above base speed)*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Stepper Motor Control (Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Compare the construction and working of Variable Reluctance (VR), Permanent Magnet (PM), and Hybrid stepper motors. Which is preferred for a 3D printer and why?*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Feature | VR Stepper | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotor** | Soft iron, toothed | Permanent magnet (axially/radially polarized) | Toothed + PM |
| **Step angle** | Coarse (e.g., 15°) | Medium (3.6° – 7.5°) | Fine (0.9° – 1.8°) |
| **Holding torque** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Detent torque** | None | Present (PM stickiness) | Present |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Inertia** | Low | Higher | Higher |
| **Speed range** | Lowest | Medium | Wide |
| **Use** | Instrumentation | Toys, low-cost printers | **Industrial, 3D printers, CNC** |

*(Comparison table — 5 marks)*

**Preference for 3D printer (2 marks):** The **Hybrid stepper** is preferred for 3D printers because:
- **Fine step angle (1.8°)** gives high positional resolution needed for layer accuracy.
- **High holding torque** keeps the axis locked when stationary (no drift on the print bed).
- **Good open-loop performance** — no encoder required, simplifying electronics.
- Mainstream driver ICs (A4988, TMC2208, DRV8825) are designed for hybrid steppers.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*A stepper motor has a step angle of 1.8° and is driven by a 12 V supply through a microstepping driver at 1/8 microstepping. If the desired shaft speed is 240 RPM, calculate: (i) effective step angle, (ii) pulses per revolution, (iii) pulse frequency required.*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Effective step angle with 1/8 microstepping** *(2 marks)*:
$$\theta_{eff} = \frac{\theta_s}{8} = \frac{1.8^{\circ}}{8} = 0.225^{\circ}$$

**(ii) Pulses per revolution** *(2 marks)*:
$$\text{PPR} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_{eff}} = \frac{360}{0.225} = 1600 \text{ pulses/rev}$$

**(iii) Pulse frequency** *(3 marks)*:
Convert speed to pulses per second:
$$f = \text{PPR} \times \frac{N}{60} = 1600 \times \frac{240}{60} = 1600 \times 4 = 6400 \text{ Hz}$$

So the controller must generate a 6.4 kHz pulse train.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark-Loss Warnings:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing rad/s with RPM in the speed equation loses 1–2 marks. Always include **N in RPM and ω in rad/s**.
> 2. **Forgetting Lenz's Law sign:** The back-EMF opposes Vₐ, so $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a$ (not the other way around).
> 3. **Shoot-through in H-Bridge:** A schematic that doesn't show anti-shoot-through logic (dead-time insertion) is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Stepper resonance:** Students often ignore resonance between 100–300 pulses/sec, where torque drops sharply. Mention this in design problems for *full marks*.
> 5. **PWM average vs RMS:** For a DC motor (inductive load), the relevant value is the **average** voltage, not RMS.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- [x] **DC motor KVL:** $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ — back-EMF always opposes the supply.
- [x] **Speed formula:** $N = \dfrac{60(V_a - I_a R_a)}{2\pi K_e \phi}$ — memorize the unit conversion factor.
- [x] **Torque formula:** $T = K_t \phi I_a$ — torque is independent of speed.
- [x] **Armature control** = below base speed, **field control** = above base speed.
- [x] **PWM average voltage:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ where $D = T_{on} / T_s$.
- [x] **H-Bridge rule:** never turn on both high-side or both low-side switches simultaneously.
- [x] **Stepper step angle:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$ — for a 200-step hybrid motor, Nᵣ = 50 teeth, m = 4.
- [x] **Three stepper types:** VR (cheap, low torque), PM (medium), **Hybrid (best, 1.8° standard)**.
- [x] **Excitation modes:** Wave (1-phase) < Full-step 2-phase (highest torque) < Half-step < Microstepping.
- [x] **Open-loop position:** Stepper position = (number of pulses) × (effective step angle) — no sensor needed.
- [x] **Resonance zone:** Stepper torque dips at 100–300 pps — must be ramped over (acceleration profile).
- [x] **Closed-loop DC control:** PID + PWM + H-Bridge + tachogenerator/encoder is the *intelligent system* approach.
- [x] **In SI units:** $K_e = K_t$ (numerically equal, same units: V·s/rad = N·m/A).
- [x] **Microstepping trade-off:** Higher resolution but lower per-step torque and slower response.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LCD Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET414 - COMPONENTS OF INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LCD Interfacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**LCD Interfacing** is the process of electrically and logically connecting a **Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)** module to a microcontroller or microprocessor using a defined set of control lines, data lines, and a strictly sequenced instruction set to render alphanumeric characters, symbols, and custom patterns on the screen.

The most common interfacing target in KTU embedded systems is the **HD44780-based 16×2 Character LCD**, which contains a built-in **Character Generator ROM (CGROM)**, **Character Generator RAM (CGRAM)**, and a **Display Data RAM (DDRAM)**. Communication occurs via a parallel data bus (either 4-bit or 8-bit mode) synchronized by the **Enable (E)** strobe line, with command/data selection handled by the **Register Select (RS)** line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome mapped to this topic is **CO3** — *"Design and implement interfacing of peripheral devices with microcontrollers for intelligent system applications."* You are expected to draw interfacing circuits, write the initialization sequence, and explain the timing diagram in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the LCD as a **post office sorting room**:
- The **data lines (D0–D7)** are like the *letters arriving at the counter*.
- The **RS pin** tells the clerk: *"Is this a new instruction (like 'open the safe') or a character to be displayed (like the letter 'A')?"*
- The **E (Enable) pin** is the *clerk's stamp* — the data is only registered the moment the stamp comes down.
- The **R/W pin** decides whether the clerk is *receiving a letter (Write)* or *sending information back (Read)*, such as checking the busy flag.
- The **DDRAM** is the *notice board* where displayed characters are pinned. Each pin location is a **DDRAM address** (e.g., `0x80` for line 1, `0xC0` for line 2).

Without the **Enable strobe**, the LCD never knows when to capture the data — this is the single most common reason an LCD stays blank during student lab sessions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Operating Voltage (Vdd):** **+5V DC** (some modules support 3.3V)
- **Logic High Voltage (Vih):** **≥ 2.2V** (for 5V operation)
- **Logic Low Voltage (Vil):** **≤ 0.6V**
- **LCD Contrast Voltage (Vee):** Adjustable between **0V and Vdd** via a potentiometer
- **Standard Module Sizes:** **16×2**, **20×4**, **16×4** (characters × rows)
- **Character Font:** **5×8 dots** (default) or **5×10 dots**
- **Enable Pulse Width (PWE):** Minimum **450 ns** for typical HD44780
- **Data Setup Time (tDSW):** Minimum **195 ns**

> [!NOTE]
> Although the LCD has its own controller (HD44780), the **microcontroller must still act as the master**, generating the precise timing sequences. This is *not* plug-and-play like a USB device.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How LCD Interfacing Works

LCD interfacing is governed by a layered protocol. The following bullet logic traces the complete data path from the microcontroller to the pixels on the glass:

- **Step 1 — Power-on Reset Sequence:**
  - The LCD controller performs an internal reset when Vdd rises from 0V to 4.5V.
  - It then waits for an **initialization command sequence** (Function Set, Display ON/OFF, Clear Display, Entry Mode Set).

- **Step 2 — Command vs. Data Selection (RS line):**
  - `RS = 0` → **Command Register** is selected. The microcontroller sends instructions such as *Clear Display*, *Cursor Move*, or *Function Set*.
  - `RS = 1` → **Data Register** is selected. The microcontroller sends the **ASCII code** of the character to be displayed.

- **Step 3 — Read/Write Direction (R/W line):**
  - `R/W = 0` → Microcontroller is **writing** to the LCD.
  - `R/W = 1` → Microcontroller is **reading** the **Busy Flag (BF)** or the current address counter.

- **Step 4 — Data Strobe (Enable line):**
  - The E pin must be **toggled High → Low** to latch the data into the LCD. The rising edge samples the data bus; the falling edge completes the write.

- **Step 5 — Execution Time:**
  - After each command, the LCD takes a finite time to execute. The `BF = 1` flag indicates the controller is busy. **Polling BF** is faster than blind delays, but most student programs use a 1–2 ms software delay.

- **Step 6 — Address Pointer Movement:**
  - The **Address Counter (AC)** inside the LCD auto-increments (or decrements) after each character write, depending on the **Entry Mode Set** command.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as your **last-minute revision cheat sheet** before ESE. Every parameter here has appeared in past KTU question papers either directly or as a 2-mark part (a).

| Parameter / Equation | Value or Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of data lines (8-bit mode) | 8 lines (D0 to D7) | Lines from MCU to LCD |
| Number of data lines (4-bit mode) | 4 lines (D4 to D7) | MSB nibble transmitted first |
| Control lines required | 3 (RS, R/W, E) | Plus Vdd, Vss, Vee, A, K |
| DDRAM address — Line 1, position 1 | `0x80` | Hex base address for Row 1 |
| DDRAM address — Line 2, position 1 | `0xC0` | Hex base address for Row 2 |
| DDRAM address — Line 3, position 1 | `0x94` | For 20×4 LCDs |
| DDRAM address — Line 4, position 1 | `0xD4` | For 20×4 LCDs |
| Function Set (8-bit, 2 lines, 5×8) | `0x38` | Most common initialization byte |
| Function Set (4-bit, 2 lines, 5×8) | `0x28` | Saves 4 MCU I/O pins |
| Display ON, Cursor ON, Blink ON | `0x0F` | All features enabled |
| Display ON, Cursor OFF | `0x0C` | Clean look, no cursor |
| Clear Display | `0x01` | Clears DDRAM, AC = 0 |
| Entry Mode: Increment, no shift | `0x06` | Auto-increment AC after write |
| Busy Flag check | `BF = 1` means busy | Read from D7 line |
| Address Counter range | `0x00` to `0x7F` | 128 bytes of DDRAM |
| CGRAM address range | `0x00` to `0x3F` | 64 bytes for 8 custom chars |
| Character code 'A' (ASCII) | `0x41` | Example: write `0x41` to show 'A' |
| Enable pulse width (min) | $P_{WE} \geq 450$ ns | From datasheet |
| Address setup time (min) | $t_{ASU} \geq 40$ ns | Before E rising edge |

## 2.3 Why This System Matters in Engineering

LCD interfacing is the **human-machine interface (HMI)** backbone for nearly every consumer product from washing machines to medical infusion pumps. For KTU students, mastery of LCD timing and command sequencing directly translates to industry-ready firmware skills, since the **HD44780 protocol is still used in industrial displays**, ATmega projects, and ARM Cortex-M evaluation boards. Understanding the **Busy Flag polling** technique also teaches the broader engineering principle of **handshaking** between asynchronous digital systems.

## 2.4 4-Bit vs 8-Bit Mode — Engineering Trade-off

| Aspect | 8-Bit Mode | 4-Bit Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Data lines used | 8 (D0–D7) | 4 (D4–D7) |
| Total MCU pins consumed | 11 (8 data + 3 control) | 7 (4 data + 3 control) |
| Transfer speed | Faster (1 write = 1 byte) | Slower (1 byte = 2 nibbles) |
| Initialization complexity | Simple | Requires 3-step startup sequence |
| Common use case | Memory-rich 8051 variants | Pin-constrained ATtiny, low-pin MCUs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU lab exams, the 4-bit mode is the **most frequently asked** because students must demonstrate the 3-call initialization procedure (Function Set → Function Set → Function Set with 4-bit set). Marks are awarded for *each* of the three calls.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 4-Bit Initialization Sequence

The 4-bit mode is more involved than 8-bit because the LCD powers up in **8-bit mode by default**. The microcontroller must first force it into 4-bit mode using a deterministic sequence. The derivation below shows *exactly* how each step is derived from the HD44780 datasheet timing requirements.

**Step 1 — Power-on Wait:**
After Vdd stabilizes at 4.5V or higher, the controller requires a delay of:

$$
T_{POR} \geq 15 \text{ ms}
$$

This guarantees the internal reset oscillator has settled. (Some datasheets cite 10 ms; KTU papers use **15 ms** as the safe upper bound.)

**Step 2 — First Function Set (8-bit forced):**
The MCU sends the command `0x30` (or its upper nibble `0x3`). Since the LCD is still in 8-bit mode, it reads the full 8-bit bus, recognizes `0x30` as *"8-bit interface, 1 line, 5×8 font"*, and prepares to switch.

**Step 3 — Second Function Set:**
After a delay of:

$$
T_{d1} \geq 4.1 \text{ ms}
$$

the MCU repeats `0x30`. This second call confirms the previous command and stabilizes the bus.

**Step 4 — Third Function Set (4-bit switch):**
After:

$$
T_{d2} \geq 100 \text{ \mu s}
$$

the MCU now sends `0x20` (upper nibble `0x2`). The LCD interprets this as the **Function Set with N=0 (1-line) and F=0 (5×8)** *but critically* the 4-bit interface bit (DL=0) is now latched because the LCD recognized only the upper nibble.

**Step 5 — Final Function Set in 4-bit Mode:**
Now operating in 4-bit mode, the MCU sends the actual function configuration in **two nibbles**:

$$
\text{Command} = 0x28 = \underbrace{0010}_{\text{upper nibble}} \;\;\; \underbrace{1000}_{\text{lower nibble}}
$$

The upper nibble `0x2` re-asserts 4-bit mode; the lower nibble `0x8` configures **N=1 (2 lines)** and **F=0 (5×8 font)**.

**Step 6 — Final Configuration Commands (each sent as 2 nibbles):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Display ON/OFF} &= 0x0C \\
\text{Clear Display} &= 0x01 \\
\text{Entry Mode} &= 0x06
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The delays between nibbles inside a single 4-bit command are governed by the **Enable pulse width** $P_{WE} \geq 450$ ns, which a standard 1 MHz or higher MCU clock handles automatically via instruction cycle time.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Initialization Command Sequence

The complete 8-bit mode initialization, when written out as a **C-style pseudocode flow**, looks like this:

| Order | Command Sent | Hex Value | Wait Time After | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Function Set (8-bit, 1 line) | `0x30` | 4.1 ms | Wake up LCD in 8-bit |
| 2 | Function Set (8-bit, 1 line) | `0x30` | 100 µs | Confirm wake-up |
| 3 | Function Set (8-bit, 1 line) | `0x30` | none | Final 8-bit confirm |
| 4 | Function Set (8-bit, 2 line, 5×8) | `0x38` | 53 µs | Switch to 2-line, 5×8 font |
| 5 | Display OFF | `0x08` | 53 µs | Turn display off cleanly |
| 6 | Clear Display | `0x01` | 1.64 ms | Clear DDRAM, AC = 0 |
| 7 | Entry Mode Set (increment) | `0x06` | 53 µs | Set AC auto-increment |
| 8 | Display ON, cursor OFF | `0x0C` | 53 µs | Final visible state |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Writing 'A' to Display

To display the character **'A'** at line 1, position 1:

**Step 1 — Set DDRAM Address:**
The MCU must first send the command `0x80` to set the Address Counter to the start of line 1.

$$
\text{DDRAM Command} = 0x80 + \text{offset}
$$

For position 1, offset = 0, so the byte is `0x80`.

**Step 2 — Send ASCII Code:**
With `RS = 1`, the MCU writes the ASCII value of 'A', which is:

$$
\text{ASCII}('A') = 0x41 = 0100\,0001_2
$$

The LCD looks up `0x41` in its internal **CGROM** and fetches the corresponding **5×8 dot pattern**, lighting up the appropriate pixels in the next character cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 0x41, not 0xA1?** The HD44780 CGROM uses **standard ASCII** (not extended). 'A' is `0x41`; 'Z' is `0x5A`; '0' is `0x30`. Students often confuse this and write '0' for the character 'A'.

## 3.4 Custom Character Generation in CGRAM

The HD44780 allows up to **8 custom 5×8 characters** stored in CGRAM at addresses `0x40` to `0x7F`. Each character consumes **8 bytes** of CGRAM. The address for the Nth custom character (N from 0 to 7) is:

$$
\text{CGRAM\_ADDR}(N) = 0x40 + (N \times 8)
$$

For example, the address of custom character #3 is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CGRAM\_ADDR}(3) &= 0x40 + (3 \times 8) \\
&= 0x40 + 24 \\
&= 0x40 + 0x18 \\
&= 0x58
\end{aligned}
$$

After sending this address as a command, the next 8 data writes fill in the 8 rows of the custom character bitmap. Each row is encoded as a 5-bit value (bit 4 is leftmost pixel; bit 0 is rightmost pixel, for a 5×8 font).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LCD Interfacing

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional flow between the MCU, the LCD controller, and the glass display. Each alphanumeric node represents a distinct functional block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MCU1[Microcontroller Unit] -->|8 or 4 data lines| BUS1[Parallel Data Bus D0 D7]
    MCU1 -->|Control signal| RS1[RS Register Select Line]
    MCU1 -->|Control signal| RW1[RW Read Write Line]
    MCU1 -->|Strobe signal| E1[E Enable Line]
    BUS1 --> LCDCTRL1[HD44780 LCD Controller]
    RS1 --> LCDCTRL1
    RW1 --> LCDCTRL1
    E1 --> LCDCTRL1
    LCDCTRL1 --> CGROM1[Character Generator ROM]
    LCDCTRL1 --> CGRAM1[Custom Character RAM 64 bytes]
    LCDCTRL1 --> DDRAM1[Display Data RAM 80 bytes]
    LCDCTRL1 --> SEG1[Segment Drivers]
    LCDCTRL1 --> COM1[Common Drivers]
    SEG1 --> GLASS1[LCD Glass 16 by 2 characters]
    COM1 --> GLASS1
    DDRAM1 --> AC1[Address Counter]
    AC1 -->|Position pointer| DDRAM1
    PWR1[Power Supply 5V DC] --> LCDCTRL1
    VEE1[Contrast Voltage via Potentiometer] --> LCDCTRL1
```

## 4.2 Timing Diagram for a Single LCD Write Operation

The following Mermaid sequence-style state diagram captures the **Enable strobe protocol** that every write must obey.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> SetRS: MCU sets RS and RW
    SetRS --> PlaceData: MCU places byte on D0 D7
    PlaceData --> EHigh: E pin driven HIGH
    EHigh --> LCDSample: LCD samples data on rising edge
    LCDSample --> ELow: E pin driven LOW
    ELow --> LatchComplete: LCD latches byte on falling edge
    LatchComplete --> Execute: LCD executes instruction
    Execute --> Delay: Wait for execution time
    Delay --> Idle: Return to idle state
```

## 4.3 4-Bit Mode Data Transfer Sequence (Nibble-by-Nibble)

The following block flow shows how a single byte is split across two Enable strobes in 4-bit mode. This is the **most heavily tested diagram** in KTU ESE Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Byte to send 0x28] --> B1[Extract upper nibble 0x2]
    B1 --> C1[Place on D4 D7 with RS and RW set]
    C1 --> D1[Strobe E high then low]
    D1 --> E1[LCD captures upper nibble]
    E1 --> F1[Extract lower nibble 0x8]
    F1 --> G1[Place on D4 D7]
    G1 --> H1[Strobe E high then low]
    H1 --> I1[LCD captures lower nibble]
    I1 --> J1[Command 0x28 fully received]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above (e.g., `MCU1`, `BUS1`, `LCDCTRL1`) are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with Mermaid parser rules. No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text without bold or italics formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **List the pins of a 16×2 LCD module that are used for interfacing with a microcontroller. Explain the function of the RS, R/W, and E pins.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A 16×2 LCD module has the following key interfacing pins:

- **Data Pins (D0–D7):** Carry the command or character data from the microcontroller to the LCD. In 4-bit mode, only D4–D7 are used.
- **RS (Register Select):** Selects between the **Command Register (RS = 0)** and the **Data Register (RS = 1)**.
- **R/W (Read/Write):** Determines the direction of data transfer. **R/W = 0** means the MCU is *writing* to the LCD; **R/W = 1** means the MCU is *reading* the busy flag or address counter.
- **E (Enable):** Acts as a **strobe/latch** signal. The LCD captures the data on the **falling edge** of E. The pulse width must be at least **450 ns**.
- **Vdd, Vss:** +5V power and ground.
- **Vee:** Contrast adjustment voltage (0 to Vdd).
- **A, K:** Backlight Anode and Cathode.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention **Vee** and the **backlight pins (A, K)**. KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for the contrast pin since it is critical to the LCD being visible. If the screen appears blank in the lab, 90% of the time the contrast potentiometer is misadjusted.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between DDRAM and CGRAM in an HD44780-based LCD. Why is CGRAM useful?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DDRAM (Display Data RAM) | CGRAM (Character Generator RAM) |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Display Data RAM | Character Generator RAM |
| Size | 80 bytes | 64 bytes |
| Purpose | Stores the **ASCII codes** of currently displayed characters | Stores **user-defined custom character bitmaps** |
| Address Range | `0x00` to `0x7F` | `0x40` to `0x7F` |
| Writable? | Yes (via `RS = 1` data writes) | Yes (after setting CGRAM address via command) |
| Max custom chars | N/A | **8 characters** of 5×8 dots each |

**Why CGRAM is useful:**
CGRAM allows the designer to display **custom symbols** (e.g., a heart, a smiley, a battery icon, a degree symbol `°`) that are not part of the standard ASCII character set. This is essential for creating user-friendly graphical interfaces in embedded systems.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`**
>
> **(a)** Draw the interfacing circuit diagram of a 16×2 LCD with an ATmega328 microcontroller in **4-bit mode**. Label all pins and show the contrast adjustment network. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the complete **initialization sequence in 4-bit mode** step-by-step, including all command bytes, RS/RW states, and inter-command delays. Explain the reason for sending the Function Set command three times. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

In 4-bit mode, the LCD uses only **4 data lines (D4–D7) plus 3 control lines (RS, R/W, E)**. The contrast is set by a **10 kΩ potentiometer** between Vdd and Vss, with the wiper connected to pin 3 (Vee) of the LCD.

**Pin connections (textual schematic, 4 marks for correct pin mapping):**

| ATmega328 Pin | LCD Pin | Function |
|---|---|---|
| PD4 | D4 | Data line 4 (LSB of upper nibble) |
| PD5 | D5 | Data line 5 |
| PD6 | D6 | Data line 6 |
| PD7 | D7 | Data line 7 (MSB) |
| PB0 | RS | Register Select |
| PB1 | R/W | Read/Write |
| PB2 | E | Enable strobe |
| +5V | Vdd (pin 2) | Power supply |
| GND | Vss (pin 1) | Ground |
| Pot wiper | Vee (pin 3) | Contrast voltage |
| +5V via 220Ω | A (pin 15) | Backlight anode |
| GND | K (pin 16) | Backlight cathode |

**Contrast network (3 marks):** A 10 kΩ pot with terminal 1 to Vdd, terminal 2 to Vss, and wiper to Vee. This produces an adjustable voltage between 0V and 5V, allowing the user to tune the display contrast.

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

The 4-bit initialization sequence is:

1. **Wait for power stabilization:** $T_{POR} = 15$ ms (1 mark for delay)
2. **Send Function Set 1:** Command `0x30` (sent as `0x3` upper nibble only, RS = 0, R/W = 0). This is interpreted as 8-bit function set by the LCD since it powers up in 8-bit mode. (1 mark)
3. **Wait 4.1 ms, then send Function Set 2:** Command `0x30` again. (1 mark)
4. **Wait 100 µs, then send Function Set 3:** Command `0x20` (upper nibble `0x2`). This forces the LCD into **4-bit mode**. (1 mark)
5. **Send Function Set 4-bit configuration:** `0x28` in two nibbles — first `0x2`, then `0x8`. This sets **N=1 (2 lines)**, **F=0 (5×8 font)**, **DL=0 (4-bit interface)**. (1 mark)
6. **Display OFF:** `0x08` (1 mark for command sequence)
7. **Clear Display:** `0x01`
8. **Entry Mode Set:** `0x06` (increment mode, no shift)
9. **Display ON, Cursor OFF:** `0x0C`

**Why three Function Set commands (2 marks):**
The HD44780 may power up in an **unknown bus state**. The three repeated Function Set commands (with decreasing wait times: 15 ms → 4.1 ms → 100 µs) form a **safe startup ladder** that guarantees the LCD correctly recognizes the bus width regardless of its initial condition. The third call finally commits to 4-bit mode by sending `0x20` instead of `0x30`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** if the student writes `0x38` as the first command in 4-bit mode (this is the 8-bit function set and will not work for 4-bit initialization). The very first command **must** be `0x30` (or its equivalent upper nibble `0x3`) to honor the protocol.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **timing diagram of an LCD write operation** with reference to the Enable, RS, and R/W lines. State the minimum Enable pulse width and address setup time. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how a **custom character** (for example, a bell icon 🔔) is generated and stored in CGRAM. Write the CGRAM address command for the 4th custom character. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The LCD write timing diagram has the following signal sequence:

1. **RS and R/W are set first** (RS = 0 for command or RS = 1 for data; R/W = 0 for write). (1 mark)
2. **Data is placed on D0–D7** (or D4–D7 in 4-bit mode). (1 mark)
3. **After the address setup time** $t_{ASU} \geq 40$ ns, the **E line is driven HIGH**. (1 mark)
4. **E remains HIGH for the pulse width** $P_{WE} \geq 450$ ns. (1 mark)
5. **The data lines can change state only after the E falling edge**, with a hold time of $t_{H} \geq 10$ ns. (1 mark)
6. The cycle repeats for the next byte (or next nibble in 4-bit mode). (1 mark)

**Key timing parameters (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{WE} &\geq 450 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(Enable pulse width)} \\
t_{ASU} &\geq 40 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(Address setup time)} \\
t_{H} &\geq 10 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(Data hold time)} \\
t_{CYC} &\geq 1 \text{ \mu s} \quad \text{(Enable cycle time)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

A 5×8 custom character is defined by 8 bytes, where each byte represents one row of pixels. The MSB (bit 7) is unused for a 5-pixel-wide font. For example, a simple bell icon might have rows encoded as:

| Row | Byte (hex) | Binary | Pixel pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `0x04` | `00100` | . . X . . |
