### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
*List the four layers of an IoT architecture. For a smart weather station, identify ONE component belonging to each layer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four layers are:

1. **Perception Layer** — DHT22 / BMP280 / MQ-135 sensors *(½ mark)*
2. **Network Layer** — ESP32 Wi-Fi radio + MQTT protocol *(½ mark)*
3. **Processing Layer** — Edge threshold logic on ESP32 + ThingSpeak cloud *(1 mark)*
4. **Application Layer** — Web/mobile dashboard for visualization and SMS alerts *(1 mark)*

*(Examiner accepts equivalent valid components.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
*Define the duty-cycle metric used in IoT power budgeting. Why is it critical for battery-powered nodes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Duty cycle $d$ is the fraction of time the system remains active within one operational period:

$$
d = \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}
$$

It is critical because the *time-averaged current* $I_{avg} = d \cdot I_{on} + (1-d) \cdot I_{off}$ directly determines battery life via $T_{life} = C_{bat}/I_{avg}$ *(1 mark)*. For solar-powered IoT nodes, a low duty cycle allows operation to remain energy-neutral even with small panels *(1 mark)*.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Design the hardware architecture of a *Solar-Powered IoT Weather & Air-Quality Monitoring Station*. Justify the choice of **microcontroller, sensors, communication protocol, and power source** for outdoor deployment. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Microcontroller — ESP32-WROOM-32** *(1.5 marks)*
- Integrated Wi-Fi eliminates the need for an external radio.
- Dual-core 32-bit @ 240 MHz supports on-edge filtering.
- Hardware deep-sleep current ~10 µA enables duty-cycling.
- 12-bit SAR ADC suffices for the MQ-135 gas channel.

**(2) Sensors** *(2 marks)*
- **DHT22** → temperature & humidity (digital, single-wire, ±0.5 °C accuracy).
- **BMP280** → barometric pressure and altitude (I²C, ±1 hPa).
- **MQ-135** → NH₃ / NOₓ / CO₂ proxy (analog, requires load resistor).

Justification: The trio covers all parameters mandated by CPCB ambient monitoring. DHT22 is chosen over DHT11 due to its wider range and higher accuracy.

**(3) Communication Protocol — MQTT v3.1.1 over TLS** *(1.5 marks)*
- Lightweight publish/subscribe model — *minimal bandwidth overhead* (~2-byte header).
- QoS levels (0/1/2) match reliability needs of environmental data.
- TLS encryption protects citizen-privacy when deployed in public spaces.

**(4) Power Source — 6 V/1 W Solar + TP4056 + 3 000 mAh Li-ion** *(2 marks)*
- Solar ensures energy-positive operation under Kerala's 4.5–5.5 peak sun-hours.
- TP4056 + DW01 provides CC-CV charging and over-discharge protection.
- 18650 Li-ion cell is field-replaceable, allowing *zero-downtime maintenance*.

---

**(b)** For the chosen 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, compute:
1. The **resolution (LSB)** in mV.
2. The **voltage** corresponding to an ADC code of **2 850**.
3. The **ideal SNR** in dB.

If the sensor produces an analog voltage of **1.85 V**, what is the **ppm concentration** of NH₃ (use $R_{L} = 10$ kΩ, $R_{0} = 30$ kΩ, $a = 116.602$, $b = -2.475$)? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(1) LSB resolution** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LSB} &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096} \\
&\approx 8.057 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \\
&\approx 0.806 \text{ mV per LSB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2) Voltage for code 2 850** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= \frac{ADC_{code}}{2^{12}} \cdot V_{ref} = \frac{2850}{4096} \cdot 3.3 \\
&= 0.6958 \cdot 3.3 \\
&\approx 2.296 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3) Ideal SNR** *(1 mark)*

$$
SNR_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}
$$

**(4) NH₃ ppm computation** *(2 marks)*

Step A — Sensor resistance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{s} &= R_{L} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{C}}{V_{RL}} - 1\right) \\
&= 10\,000 \cdot \left(\frac{5.0}{1.85} - 1\right) \\
&= 10\,000 \cdot (2.7027 - 1) \\
&= 10\,000 \cdot 1.7027 \\
&= 17\,027 \; \Omega \approx 17.03 \text{ k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Step B — ppm using curve-fit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_{s}}{R_{0}} &= \frac{17.027}{30} = 0.5676 \\
\text{ppm} &= 116.602 \cdot (0.5676)^{-2.475} \\
&= 116.602 \cdot \exp(-2.475 \cdot \ln(0.5676)) \\
&= 116.602 \cdot \exp(-2.475 \cdot (-0.5661)) \\
&= 116.602 \cdot \exp(1.4010) \\
&= 116.602 \cdot 4.058 \\
&\approx 473.2 \text{ ppm NH}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **above** the safe ambient threshold of 200 ppm — the embedded logic should trigger the alert flag.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **MQTT protocol stack** used in IoT systems. Compare it with HTTP for a low-power sensor network in terms of *header size, power consumption, latency, and reliability*. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**MQTT Architecture** *(3 marks)*
- **MQTT** = *Message Queuing Telemetry Transport*, an ISO-standard (ISO/IEC 20922) pub/sub protocol.
- Components: **Publisher**, **Subscriber**, **Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ).
- Runs over TCP/IP; with TLS → **MQTTS**.
- Topic hierarchy: `sems/college/blockA/floor2/temperature`.
- QoS levels:
  - *QoS 0* — at most once (fire-and-forget).
  - *QoS 1* — at least once (PUBACK).
  - *QoS 2* — exactly once (4-way handshake).

**Comparison Table** *(4 marks)*

| Metric | HTTP/1.1 | MQTT v3.1.1 |
|---|---|---|
| Header size | **~200–800 bytes** | **2-byte fixed header** |
| Power per request | High (full TCP handshake each time) | Low (persistent TCP connection) |
| Latency | High (~hundreds of ms for JSON parsing) | Low (~tens of ms) |
| Reliability | Manual retry on app layer | Built-in QoS 0/1/2 |
| Bandwidth | Wasteful for tiny payloads | Optimized for <1 KB telemetry |
| Suitability for low-power IoT | Poor | **Excellent** |

**Verdict:** For a sensor station publishing a 50-byte JSON every 30 s, MQTT saves ~95 % header overhead and ~80 % energy per publish cycle.

---

**(b)** A wireless sensor node transmits a packet of size **128 bytes** every **60 seconds** at a transmit power of **0.5 W** for **20 ms**. The node draws **25 mA** in active (transmit) state and **5 µA** in deep sleep from a **3.7 V, 2 000 mAh** Li-ion battery. Compute:
1. **Energy per packet** in Joules.
2. **Average current** of the node.
3. **Theoretical battery life** in days.

Assume $V_{DD} = 3.7$ V. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Energy per packet** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{pkt} &= P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} \\
&= 0.5 \text{ W} \cdot 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 0.020 \\
&= 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J} = 10 \text{ mJ per packet}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2) Average current** *(3 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{on} &= 25 \text{ mA}, \quad I_{off} = 5 \; \mu\text{A} = 0.005 \text{ mA} \\
t_{on} &= 20 \text{ ms} = 0.020 \text{ s} \\
T &= 60 \text{ s} \Rightarrow t_{off} = 59.980 \text{ s} \\
I_{avg} &= \frac{t_{on} \cdot I_{on} + t_{off} \cdot I_{off}}{T} \\
&= \frac{0.020 \cdot 25 + 59.980 \cdot 0.005}{60} \\
&= \frac{0.500 + 0.2999}{60} \\
&= \frac{0.7999}{60} \\
&\approx 0.01333 \text{ mA} \\
&= 13.33 \; \mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3) Battery life** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{life} &= \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2000 \text{ mAh}}{0.01333 \text{ mA}} \\
&\approx 150\,037 \text{ hours} \\
&\approx 6\,251.5 \text{ days} \\
&\approx 17.13 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** Real-world derating (self-discharge ~3 %/month, efficiency ~85 %, temperature effects) reduces this by ~30–50 %. Expect **~10 years** of practical autonomy — still excellent for a remote weather node.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers — Case-Study Questions (PEMET414)**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in final answers → **−1 mark** per occurrence (e.g., writing `74` instead of `74 dB`).
> 2. **Confusing ADC Vref** with supply voltage → ESP32 internal Vref **must** be calibrated; assuming 5 V on a 3.3 V pin causes off-by-50 % errors.
> 3. **Skipping the "Justify" component** in design questions — KTU awards 2–3 marks *purely* on engineering reasoning, not just the component name.
> 4. **In power-budget derivations, using $C_{bat}/I_{on}$** instead of $C_{bat}/I_{avg}$ → loses 2 marks; this is a *very* common error.
> 5. **MQTT vs HTTP comparison** — students often write only "MQTT is better" without the *header size / power / latency* numerical evidence; expect **−2 marks** without the comparison table.
> 6. **Not stating the Nyquist criterion** when discussing sampling intervals — at least mention $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 Case Study**

- ☐ A simple Embedded/IoT project is built on the **four-layer architecture**: *Perception → Network → Processing → Application*.
- ☐ Always justify **component selection** by linking to *power, range, accuracy, and cost* — not just naming the part.
- ☐ **ADC reconstruction formula:** $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC_{code}}{2^{n}} \cdot V_{ref}$; remember $n$ for ESP32 = **12**, $V_{ref}$ = **3.3 V** unless externally calibrated.
- ☐ **Ideal SNR** of an N-bit ADC = $6.02N + 1.76$ dB — memorize this for instant marks.
- ☐ **MQ-series gas sensor** calibration follows $ppm = a(R_s/R_0)^b$; the voltage divider gives $R_s = R_L(V_C/V_{RL} - 1)$.
- ☐ **Power budgeting** hinges on the **average current** $I_{avg} = \dfrac{t_{on} I_{on} + t_{off} I_{off}}{T}$; never use peak current for life estimation.
- ☐ **Solar-augmented nodes** become *energy-positive* when $I_{avg} < I_{solar}^{eff}$ — this is the design goal for unattended IoT.
- ☐ **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ — choose sampling interval by the slowest meaningful physical change, not by code convenience.
- ☐ **MQTT** is preferred over HTTP for IoT due to *2-byte header*, persistent TCP, and built-in QoS.
- ☐ Always include a **decision-logic flowchart** for the "intelligent" component — examiners award marks for explicit threshold rules.
- ☐ Use **deep-sleep mode** as a default; ESP32 deep-sleep current is **~10 µA**, three orders of magnitude lower than active mode.
- ☐ For KTU answers, *show every algebraic step* — partial marks are awarded for transitions, not just final numbers.
- ☐ Cite the **MQTT QoS level** (0/1/2) explicitly when designing the publish protocol.
- ☐ Mention **TLS / security** for any IoT project that handles citizen or industrial data — this is a 2024-scheme evaluation hot-spot.
